name: Home
title: Home
description: Entry page that loads dynamic highlights for services and events and provides direct navigation to visit planning, sermons, events, serving, giving, room reservations, and care.

core_features: Load homepage highlight data on initial page load using getHomeHighlights, including upcomingServices, featuredEvents, and optional announcementText.
Display a dismissible announcement area that shows announcementText when provided by getHomeHighlights.
List upcoming worship services from upcomingServices with basic details and clickable cards that navigate to service_detail.html with the serviceId URL parameter.
List featured events from featuredEvents with basic details and clickable cards that navigate to event_detail.html with the eventId URL parameter.
Provide a "View all events" interaction that navigates to events.html with view=list in the URL query string.
Provide primary navigation actions to Plan a Visit, Sermons, Events, Serve, Give, and Care sections from the Home page.
Provide quick-action tiles for Plan a Visit, Reserve a Room, and Need Care that navigate to visit.html, room_reservations.html, and care.html respectively.
user_workflows: Discover upcoming services and open details: User opens Home -> page calls getHomeHighlights -> upcomingServices are rendered as a list of service cards -> user clicks a specific service card -> browser navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={selectedServiceId}.

See a featured event and view all details: User opens Home -> page calls getHomeHighlights -> featuredEvents are rendered as event cards -> user clicks a specific event card -> browser navigates to event_detail.html?eventId={selectedEventId}.

Browse all events from Home: User opens Home -> page calls getHomeHighlights -> featuredEvents section shows a "View all events" link or button -> user activates that link -> browser navigates to events.html?view=list.

Plan a visit from Home: User opens Home -> page calls getHomeHighlights (data not required to trigger navigation) -> user clicks the primary "Plan a Visit" action or the Plan a Visit quick-action tile -> browser navigates to visit.html.

Access room reservations: User opens Home -> page calls getHomeHighlights (data not required for this action) -> user clicks the "Reserve a Room" quick-action tile -> browser navigates to room_reservations.html.

Request care or counseling: User opens Home -> page calls getHomeHighlights -> user clicks the "Need Care?" quick-action tile -> browser navigates to care.html where care and counseling workflows continue.

Access core ministry areas: User opens Home -> page calls getHomeHighlights -> user uses main-task navigation links to go to sermons.html (Sermons), events.html?view=list (Events), serve.html (Serve), give.html (Give), or care.html (Care).
interactions: Page load: On initial load of index.html, the page invokes getHomeHighlights with no parameters; while awaiting the response, the UI may show loading states for the announcement area, upcoming services, and featured events; on success, data is passed to the relevant components; on failure, error states are shown.

Announcement dismissal: If announcementText is non-empty, the announcement component renders it with a close control; clicking the close control hides the announcement component for the current page session without changing the URL or calling any API.
Service card click: In the upcoming services list, each rendered service card has an onClick handler; when clicked, the browser navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={serviceIdFromCard}.
"Plan a Visit" primary action: A prominent Plan a Visit control on the page has an onClick handler that navigates to visit.html (no query parameters).
Plan a Visit quick-action tile: In the quick-actions area, the Plan a Visit tile has an onClick handler that navigates to visit.html (no query parameters).
Reserve a Room quick-action tile: In the quick-actions area, the Reserve a Room tile has an onClick handler that navigates to room_reservations.html (no query parameters).
Need Care quick-action tile: In the quick-actions area, the Need Care tile has an onClick handler that navigates to care.html (no query parameters).
Featured event card click: In the featured events list, each event card has an onClick handler; when clicked, the browser navigates to event_detail.html?eventId={eventIdFromCard}.
"View all events" interaction: In the featured events section, a "View all events" link or button has an onClick/onActivate handler that navigates to events.html?view=list.

Header/main navigation interactions: Navigation items or links labeled Plan a Visit, Sermons, Events, Serve, Give, and Care trigger navigations respectively to visit.html, sermons.html, events.html?view=list, serve.html, give.html, and care.html when clicked.

Error and empty-state handling: If getHomeHighlights returns an error or empty arrays for upcomingServices or featuredEvents, the respective components display an error or empty state message area (functional feedback only) without using static domain data, and no cards are rendered.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page does not require or interpret any incoming URL parameters; it always loads the same functional layout and requests data by calling getHomeHighlights without parameters. Page state is derived entirely from the getHomeHighlights response and transient in-memory UI state (e.g., whether the announcement has been dismissed) that is not persisted to the URL or any storage. Outgoing navigation uses URL parameters to pass identifiers or view modes to other pages: clicking an upcoming service card navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={serviceId}, where serviceId is taken directly from the corresponding item in upcomingServices; clicking a featured event card navigates to event_detail.html?eventId={eventId}, where eventId is taken directly from the corresponding item in featuredEvents; clicking "View all events" navigates to events.html?view=list with a fixed view parameter value of 'list'; Plan a Visit triggers navigate to visit.html with no parameters; the Reserve a Room quick action navigates to room_reservations.html with no parameters; the Need Care quick action navigates to care.html with no parameters. Any extraneous query parameters that might be present on index.html are ignored by the Home page logic and are not propagated or modified by in-page interactions unless explicitly included in the outgoing URLs described above.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with approximately 3–4 columns for the left sidebar and 8–9 columns for the main content area within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear top-to-bottom structure: a global header with logo and main navigation at the very top, followed by a full-width photographic hero band. Beneath the hero, all core content sits inside a centered max-width container that splits into two columns: a left sidebar for home-page section navigation (Visit, Sermons, Events, Serving, Giving, Room Reservations, Care) and a right main content column for dynamic highlights (featured service, upcoming events, welcome/intro text). The main column leads with a compact utility element (e.g., a subtle RSS or updates indicator) followed by a large page title ('Welcome' or 'Home'), then a highlighted introductory paragraph and supporting content blocks.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear top-to-bottom structure: a global header with logo and main navigation at the very top, followed by a full-width photographic hero band. Beneath the hero, all core content sits inside a centered max-width container that splits into two columns: a left sidebar for home-page section navigation (Visit, Sermons, Events, Serving, Giving, Room Reservations, Care) and a right main content column for dynamic highlights (featured service, upcoming events, welcome/intro text). The main column leads with a compact utility element (e.g., a subtle RSS or updates indicator) followed by a large page title ('Welcome' or 'Home'), then a highlighted introductory paragraph and supporting content blocks.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header is relatively shallow and purely functional, using just enough vertical height for logo and horizontal nav. The hero image spans the full browser width and roughly 25–30% of the viewport height, acting as the primary visual anchor. Below, the main content band is constrained to a centered width (e.g., around 1080–1200px). Within this band, the left sidebar occupies roughly 25–30% of the width (about 3–4 grid columns out of 12), while the right main content area uses the remaining 70–75% (8–9 columns). Vertical spacing follows the 8px-based system: 24–32px between major sections (hero → content band, heading → body), 16–24px between sub-blocks in the main content, and tighter spacing in the sidebar where items are separated more by rules than by generous padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content density is moderate overall: the sidebar is relatively dense, with tightly stacked navigation links separated by thin dividers and minimal extra padding between each item, while still leaving enough breathing room to avoid a cramped feeling. The main column is more open: the page title has generous top and bottom margin, the introductory paragraph is clearly separated and given visual emphasis (green text, slightly looser line height), and subsequent highlight blocks have 16–24px vertical gaps. There are reasonable but not luxurious outer margins around the main container—enough to feel orderly and content-first without modern oversized whitespace.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the church logo and primary navigation at the top, establishing orientation. It then moves down to the large photographic hero that sets emotional tone. From the bottom-right area of the hero, the eye is naturally guided into the main content column where the large page title on the right attracts attention more strongly than the sidebar. Below the title, the highlighted introductory paragraph draws the next focus, followed by scanning down through prominent home highlights (e.g., upcoming service, key events). The left sidebar, visible in peripheral vision, offers a clear vertical list of home-related sections and becomes a secondary path for users who want to jump directly to Visit, Sermons, Events, Serving, Giving, Room Reservations, or Care. This creates a primary vertical storytelling flow in the main column with a secondary lateral flow through sidebar navigation.

layout.overall_description: The Home page presents as a centered, two-column informational layout framed by a traditional header and a warm hero image. At the very top, a horizontal header stretches full width: the church logo sits at the top-left, aligned with a horizontal main navigation row that runs to the right (e.g., Home, About, Ministries, Sermons, Events, Give). The header is shallow and simple, with flat background color and no strong visual effects. Directly beneath it, a wide photographic hero spans edge to edge across the browser, occupying about a quarter to a third of the screen height. The photo might show the church building or congregational life, slightly dimmed or overlaid with a subtle tint so that any optional hero text remains readable. Below the hero, the content is pulled into a centered max-width container. This container is split into two columns: on the left, a vertical sidebar about one quarter of the container width lists Home sub-sections such as 'Plan Your Visit,' 'Sermons,' 'Events,' 'Serving,' 'Giving,' 'Room Reservations,' and 'Care.' Each item sits on its own line with a thin horizontal rule between items; the currently active section (for the Home page, likely 'Home' or 'Highlights') is highlighted with a different text color or background strip but still within the same simple, flat style. On the right, taking up roughly three quarters of the width, the main content column begins with a small utility element near the top-left of this column (like an RSS badge or 'Latest updates' marker), followed by a large, clear page title such as 'Welcome to [Church Name].' The title has generous space above and below, making it the first thing users read after the hero. Immediately under the title, a first paragraph of introductory copy is set in green or a distinct accent color, giving it the feel of a lead-in or pastoral greeting. To the right side of this introductory paragraph area, an illustrative icon or small graphic sits aligned with the top of the text, floating within the width of the main column without breaking the two-column page structure. Beneath the intro, additional home highlights—like a feature card for this Sunday’s service, a short list of upcoming events, a brief note about online sermons, and key calls to action (Plan a Visit, Watch Sermons, Give Online)—are stacked vertically with consistent 16–24px spacing. The entire page feels structured and utilitarian, with modest whitespace, clear content hierarchy, and a calm, traditional tone appropriate for a church site.

name: Plan a Visit & Services
title: Plan a Visit & Services
description: Search and filter upcoming worship services by day, time, style, and children’s programming, and view a summary of any existing visit plan.

core_features: Load available filter options for day of week, service style, time range presets, and children’s age ranges using getServiceFilterOptions.
Apply URL parameter values (dayOfWeek, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo, serviceStyle, hasChildrenProgram, childrenAgeMin, childrenAgeMax) as initial filter state on page load.
Search upcoming worship services using searchWorshipServices with the current filter criteria.
Display search results as a list of service cards showing key information including day, time, location, service style, and whether children’s programming is available.
Highlight children’s programming age ranges per service by using the childrenAgeRanges data returned from searchWorshipServices.
Allow users to refine search results via interactive filters (day of week, time range, service style, and children’s programming/age range).
Synchronize filter state with the URL query parameters so that the current search can be bookmarked and restored on reload or navigation from other pages.
Display an on-page summary of the current VisitPlan (if any) using getCurrentVisitPlanSummary, including selected service and children’s programs.
Let users navigate to service_detail.html for a selected service by clicking on a service card, passing the corresponding serviceId in the query string.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Arriving from another page with pre-selected filters (e.g., index.html or contact.html)
1) User clicks a link to visit.html from index.html or contact.html; the link includes one or more query parameters such as dayOfWeek, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo, serviceStyle, hasChildrenProgram, childrenAgeMin, and/or childrenAgeMax.
2) On page load, the service-search-controller component reads the current URL query parameters.
3) service-search-controller calls getServiceFilterOptions to retrieve available values for dayOfWeekOptions, serviceStyleOptions, timeRangePresets, and childrenAgeRanges.
4) The service-filters-panel initializes its controls using the options from getServiceFilterOptions and sets the current selected values based on the URL parameters (where applicable).
5) service-search-controller constructs a search request for searchWorshipServices using the resolved filter values from the URL and/or defaults derived from the filter options.
6) searchWorshipServices returns a list of services and a totalCount; service-search-controller passes these services to the service-results-list for display.
7) Concurrently, service-search-controller calls getCurrentVisitPlanSummary; the visit-plan-summary-panel displays the current plan (if hasPlan is true).

Workflow 2: User refines search filters after initial load
1) User interacts with the service-filters-panel (e.g., selects a different day of week, adjusts a time range, picks a service style, or toggles children’s programming/age filters).
2) The filter changes update the internal filter state in service-filters-panel and emit a filtersUpdated event to service-search-controller.
3) On form submission (e.g., clicking an Apply Filters button or equivalent), service-filters-panel triggers an onSubmit event with the current filter values.
4) service-search-controller receives the submitted filter values, normalizes them (e.g., converts hasChildrenProgram into a boolean onlyWithChildrenProgram, parses numeric ages if present), and updates the browser URL query parameters to reflect the new filter state.
5) After updating the URL, service-search-controller calls searchWorshipServices with the normalized filter values.
6) The returned services are passed to service-results-list, which re-renders the list of service cards.
7) If searchWorshipServices returns no services (totalCount is 0), service-results-list calls the no-results-handler logic to display an appropriate empty state message or guidance, without relying on hardcoded service data.

Workflow 3: User selects a service to see details and continue planning
1) User scans the service-results-list and clicks on a specific service card of interest.
2) The service-results-list triggers an onServiceCardClick event containing the selected serviceId.
3) A navigation handler constructs a URL for service_detail.html with a serviceId query parameter equal to the clicked service’s serviceId.
4) The browser navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId=<selected serviceId>, where the user can view detailed information and potentially add this service to a VisitPlan via functionality on that page (not part of this page).

Workflow 4: User returns to the page to view or confirm an existing visit plan
1) A user who previously created or updated a VisitPlan on another part of the site navigates to visit.html (with or without filters in the URL).
2) On page load, in parallel with loading filter options and service search results, service-search-controller calls getCurrentVisitPlanSummary.
3) If getCurrentVisitPlanSummary returns hasPlan as true, visit-plan-summary-panel shows the visitPlan, including its service information and any associated childrenPrograms.
4) The user may use this summary to verify the chosen service time and children’s program locations, using the service-results-list for context if they want to compare with other services.
5) If there is no existing plan (hasPlan is false), visit-plan-summary-panel displays a neutral state indicating that no visit is currently planned, without assuming any specific messaging content.

Workflow 5: User shares or bookmarks a filtered view
1) After adjusting filters, service-search-controller updates the URL query parameters to reflect the current filter criteria.
2) The user copies the page URL or bookmarks it in the browser.
3) When the bookmarked/ shared URL is opened later (by the same user or someone else), the page is loaded with the query parameters already present.
4) service-search-controller again reads these parameters on load, applies them as initial filter values, and calls searchWorshipServices with corresponding arguments so that the same filtered list of services is shown, as far as current data allows.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial data loading on page load
- Trigger: Page load / onPageLoad.
- Behavior: service-search-controller reads any existing URL query parameters, calls getServiceFilterOptions, calls searchWorshipServices with the initial filter values, and calls getCurrentVisitPlanSummary. It then distributes the results to service-filters-panel, service-results-list, and visit-plan-summary-panel.

Interaction: Filter change (field-level)
- Trigger: User selects or changes a value in any filter control (day of week, time preset, custom time range, service style, children’s program toggle, or children’s age range selection).
- Behavior: service-filters-panel updates its internal filter state and can optionally trigger a filtersUpdated event to allow real-time responses (e.g., enabling/disabling controls, updating visible time presets). URL is not updated yet until submission.

Interaction: Filter submission / Apply filters
- Trigger: User activates the filter submission mechanism (e.g., clicks an Apply Filters button or equivalent control on the service-filters-panel).
- Behavior: service-filters-panel emits an onSubmit event with the current filter state; service-search-controller normalizes the filters, updates the URL query parameters to match that state, and calls searchWorshipServices with the normalized values. Returned services update service-results-list.

Interaction: Clear / reset filters
- Trigger: User activates a reset/clear filters control on service-filters-panel.
- Behavior: service-filters-panel resets its internal state to defaults based on getServiceFilterOptions and clears all filter-related URL query parameters via service-search-controller. service-search-controller then calls searchWorshipServices with no filter constraints (or with a neutral set of parameters) so the results list reflects an unfiltered state.

Interaction: Service card click
- Trigger: User clicks anywhere on a service card (or an explicit 'View Details' action) in service-results-list.
- Behavior: service-results-list triggers onServiceCardClick with the associated serviceId. A navigation handler constructs and navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId=<serviceId>. No additional page-level state is stored locally; subsequent navigation and state handling occur on service_detail.html.

Interaction: View existing visit plan summary
- Trigger: Page load and any explicit refresh action in visit-plan-summary-panel (if implemented).
- Behavior: service-search-controller calls getCurrentVisitPlanSummary; visit-plan-summary-panel binds to the returned visitPlan data when hasPlan is true and displays the service and childrenPrograms summaries. If hasPlan is false, a non-committal empty state is shown. No mutation of VisitPlan occurs on this page.

Interaction: Browser navigation using back/forward
- Trigger: User uses browser back/forward buttons after changing filters.
- Behavior: The URL query parameters change according to browser history. service-search-controller listens for URL change events and re-parses query parameters, updates service-filters-panel’s filter state, and calls searchWorshipServices so that service-results-list reflects the state represented by the current URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The URL query parameters are the authoritative representation of the current filter state for this page. On page load and on browser navigation events, service-search-controller reads the following parameters: dayOfWeek, startTimeFrom, startTimeTo, serviceStyle, hasChildrenProgram, childrenAgeMin, and childrenAgeMax. If a parameter is present and valid, it is applied directly to the filter state and used when calling searchWorshipServices. For dayOfWeek and serviceStyle, the values must match available options from getServiceFilterOptions; values that do not match available options are ignored. For startTimeFrom and startTimeTo, the component treats them as time-of-day strings; if one is missing, only the provided bound is used. The hasChildrenProgram parameter is interpreted so that the string 'true' applies a constraint onlyWithChildrenProgram = true when calling searchWorshipServices; any other value or absence of the parameter results in no children-program-only restriction being applied. childrenAgeMin and childrenAgeMax, when present and parseable, are converted to numbers and passed to searchWorshipServices as childrenAgeMin and childrenAgeMax. If only one of the age bounds is valid, only that bound is applied. When users apply or reset filters via service-filters-panel, service-search-controller updates the URL query parameters to reflect the new state (adding, updating, or removing keys as appropriate) before calling searchWorshipServices. The searchWorshipServices interface is always called using the current in-memory filter state that mirrors the URL; when no filter parameter is present, that dimension of the search is left unconstrained (by omitting the parameter or passing a neutral value, depending on implementation). getServiceFilterOptions and getCurrentVisitPlanSummary do not depend on URL parameters and are always called without parameters; their results are used solely for UI configuration and display.

components.functionality: Orchestrates data loading and state management for the page, including reading and writing URL parameters, calling getServiceFilterOptions, searchWorshipServices, and getCurrentVisitPlanSummary, and coordinating data between filters, results list, and visit plan summary.

Presents interactive controls for filtering worship services by day of week, time range (including optional use of timeRangePresets), service style, and children’s programming (including age range). It initializes its options from getServiceFilterOptions and its selected values from URL parameters supplied by service-search-controller, and it emits events when filters change or are submitted.

Displays the list of worship services returned by searchWorshipServices as individual service cards. Each card shows core information such as name, startDateTime, endDateTime, dayOfWeek, serviceStyle, serviceLocation, hasChildrenProgram, and any childrenAgeRanges. It also handles empty states when no services are returned and exposes a click interaction to navigate to service_detail.html for a given service.

Shows a concise summary of the current VisitPlan retrieved via getCurrentVisitPlanSummary, including the selected service’s name, timings, day, location, and any childrenPrograms with their ageRangeLabel and location. If no visit plan exists, it displays a neutral state. The component is read-only on this page and does not create or modify VisitPlan records.

Evaluates the output of searchWorshipServices (services array and totalCount) and determines when the current filters yield no services. It signals service-results-list to render an appropriate empty state and may prompt service-filters-panel (via service-search-controller) to suggest adjusting filters, without embedding any static service data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with approximately 3–4 columns for the left sidebar and 8–9 columns for the main content area within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same global structure as the mockup: top logo + global navigation bar, then a full-width photographic hero band about 25–30% of viewport height. Below the hero, place a centered max-width content container split into two columns: a left sidebar (roughly 3–4/12 width) with the existing section navigation, and a right main content column (roughly 8–9/12 width). All five given components live in the right main content column and stack vertically in a linear flow: service-search-controller at the top, service-filters-panel just beneath it as a tightly related block, service-results-list as the main body area, with the visit-plan-summary-panel visually attached to the right side of the main column (similar to the illustrative icon in the mockup). The no-results-handler appears inside the service-results-list region when needed, replacing or overlaying the list content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same global structure as the mockup: top logo + global navigation bar, then a full-width photographic hero band about 25–30% of viewport height. Below the hero, place a centered max-width content container split into two columns: a left sidebar (roughly 3–4/12 width) with the existing section navigation, and a right main content column (roughly 8–9/12 width). All five given components live in the right main content column and stack vertically in a linear flow: service-search-controller at the top, service-filters-panel just beneath it as a tightly related block, service-results-list as the main body area, with the visit-plan-summary-panel visually attached to the right side of the main column (similar to the illustrative icon in the mockup). The no-results-handler appears inside the service-results-list region when needed, replacing or overlaying the list content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain the mockup’s proportions: the hero is full-width and shallow-to-medium height (about 25–30% of the initial viewport). The content band below is constrained to a centered container (~1040–1200px wide) with a two-column split: 25–30% of this width for the left sidebar navigation and 70–75% for the right main content. Within the main content column, the service-search-controller and service-filters-panel together use the upper 20–30% of the vertical space (with generous spacing around the title/search), while the service-results-list takes the majority of vertical space below. The visit-plan-summary-panel occupies about 30–35% of the main column’s width when floated to the right (similar to a large right-aligned illustration) and 10–20% of vertical space, depending on content. Vertical gaps between major blocks follow the 24–32px rhythm; internal component padding follows 8–16px values.
layout_strategies.content_density: Match the mockup’s moderate density: the left sidebar remains denser, with compact stacked links and thin dividers. The right main column is more open—especially around the page title and introductory/search area—while the filters and results list are more utilitarian and slightly denser, similar to the sidebar but with clearer grouping. The service-search-controller has more breathing room (larger top and bottom margins around the title and primary search control). The service-filters-panel uses a tighter but orderly grid of controls. The service-results-list balances clear separation between service rows with modest vertical padding, avoiding clutter. The visit-plan-summary-panel is compact but given slightly more internal padding to feel like a self-contained card/aside.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Follow the mockup’s vertical-first visual flow: users first register the global nav and hero image, then their gaze moves down to the right main column where a prominent page title and primary search control (service-search-controller) sit near the top. From there, attention naturally drops to the service-filters-panel directly beneath. The eye then continues downward into the service-results-list as the main reading/browsing area. In peripheral vision, the visit-plan-summary-panel is discovered on the right side of the main column, similar to the illustrative icon in the mockup—it doesn’t compete with the main heading but is easy to discover when scanning right. If no results are available, the no-results-handler occupies the central area of the results region, immediately visible where users expect to see services.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width photographic hero, a centered content band is split into a narrow left sidebar and a wider right main column, echoing a traditional informational template. The left column holds the existing section navigation in a dense vertical list with thin separators, while the right column feels more open and task-focused. At the top of this right column, the page title and primary visit-planning controls (service-search-controller) stretch almost the full width, with comfortable vertical spacing and subtle alignment to the left. Directly beneath, the service-filters-panel forms a tidy, horizontal-then-vertical arrangement of filter fields, occupying the full width but kept shallow with 8–16px padding. Below this, the service-results-list extends downward as the main content body, listing services in a straightforward vertical stack. Along the right side of this main column—beginning around the transition from filters to results—the visit-plan-summary-panel appears as a right-aligned box, similar to a large floating illustration, occupying about one-third of the column width on larger screens while the results list content flows to its left. When space is constrained, this summary drops below in the linear flow. The overall effect is a clean, somewhat traditional layout where the main scanning path runs from the heading and search at the top-right downward through filters and results, with the summary panel and left navigation available in the periphery without overwhelming the primary task.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-search-controller sits at the very top of the right main content column, directly beneath the hero band and aligned with the right column’s left edge. It spans nearly the full width of this main column (about 90–100% of the right column width, leaving only the standard inner padding on both sides). The top edge of this component is separated from the bottom of the hero by a generous vertical gap of roughly 24–32px, reinforcing its role as the entry point to the page content. The internal layout mirrors the mockup’s heading section: a small, subtle control or label (e.g., a compact toggle, icon, or text link like “Get updates” or a small indicator) sits near the top-right or top-left in a single line, analogous to the RSS badge. Below that, flushed left, is a large page title such as “Plan a Visit & Services,” styled as the primary typographic element of the page, occupying about 60–70% of the component’s width. Under the title, an introductory line or brief instruction (e.g., “Find a worship service that fits your schedule and family.”) appears in a slightly emphasized style (which could be green or otherwise highlighted, echoing the green intro paragraph) and spans roughly the same width as the title. Immediately beneath this intro, a primary search input or compact search row (date/time quick pick, keyword, or a ‘Find services’ primary control) stretches horizontally, centered within the right column but aligned to the left edge, occupying around 60–70% of the main column width. Vertical spacing inside this component uses 16–24px between the title, intro, and search row, preserving the spacious feel. The bottom of the service-search-controller is separated from the service-filters-panel by a 16–24px gap, visually linking them while still defining distinct blocks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the standard document flow as the first block in the right column. It is not sticky or fixed; as the user scrolls, it moves off-screen, consistent with the static nature of the original mockup’s heading area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-filters-panel sits directly beneath the service-search-controller in the right main content column, separated by a moderate vertical gap of about 16–24px. It spans the full usable width of the main column (roughly 100% of the right column width minus the column’s inner padding), forming a shallow horizontal band of controls. The panel’s top aligns visually with the left edge of the page title above, maintaining a strong left margin line. Inside the panel, filter controls (day of week, time, service style, children’s programming, etc.) are arranged in a compact, grid-like layout reminiscent of a utilitarian form: on larger screens, the first row may show 3–4 filter fields side-by-side, each occupying about 20–30% of the panel width with 8–16px horizontal gaps; additional filters wrap to the next line as needed. Vertical spacing within the panel is modest (8–12px between rows of fields), producing a density similar to the sidebar menu but with slightly more breathing space due to field borders and labels. The entire panel is contained within a subtle rectangular area defined by an optional light border or divider line at the bottom, visually grouping it as a control region before the results. A 24px vertical gap separates the bottom of this panel from the top of the service-results-list, signaling a transition from input controls to output content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in the normal flow of the main content column and grows vertically as filters are added or as fields wrap to additional rows. It is not sticky by default; it scrolls away with the rest of the content, matching the static, document-like pattern of the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-results-list occupies the main body area of the right content column, positioned immediately below the service-filters-panel with a clear vertical separation of about 24px. Its left edge aligns with the left edges of the title and filters above, creating a strong vertical column of content. On larger screens, the results list conceptually occupies about 65–70% of the main column width, with the remaining 30–35% reserved for the visit-plan-summary-panel when that panel is present and floated to the right. Visually, the list’s container still spans the full main column width, but the actual service items are laid out with enough right-side padding/margin (about 24–32px) so that the summary panel can sit on the right without overlapping text. Each service result is displayed as a distinct row or card with a modest vertical padding (12–16px top and bottom) and 8–16px horizontal padding, separated by thin horizontal rules akin to the sidebar’s separators, creating a clear but moderately dense list. Key details (service name, time, style, children’s options) are aligned to the left and stacked or arranged in a short multi-line layout, preserving a content-first, text-heavy style rather than a card-heavy design. The list extends downward, consuming the majority of the vertical space below the fold as more services are shown, reinforcing it as the main interactive reading area. When there are few results, the empty vertical space below is left clean and unadorned; when there are many, the list simply scrolls, keeping the structure consistent. The no-results-handler appears inside this area when triggered, replacing or sitting where results would normally be.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives in the normal vertical flow beneath the filters, expanding or contracting in height depending on the number of results. On larger screens, it behaves like a text column that leaves room on the right side (through padding or max-width constraints) for the visit-plan-summary-panel to float alongside; on smaller screens, it expands to full width as the summary panel collapses below, reverting to a single-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit-plan-summary-panel is visually treated as a secondary but important aside, analogous to the right-aligned illustrative icon in the mockup but expanded into a small informational panel. It is anchored within the right main content column, beginning roughly at the vertical region where the service-filters-panel transitions into the top of the service-results-list. On larger screens, the panel is right-aligned within the main column: its right edge lines up with the right inner edge of the main column, and its width occupies about 30–35% of the main column. The left edge of this panel overlaps spatially with the right side of the service-results-list region, so that service items appear to the left and the summary panel sits as a vertical block to the right, similar to a floating sidebar. There is a horizontal gap of approximately 16–24px between the left edge of the summary panel and the main body of the results text, ensuring clear separation. Vertically, the top edge of the panel aligns roughly with the first visible portion of the results list (just below the filters), giving the impression that it is attached to the results area rather than the filters or the title. The panel has slightly more internal padding (16–24px on all sides) than the average list item, making it feel like a self-contained, comfortable card. Inside, a concise heading such as “Your Visit Plan” sits at the top, followed by a short list of key selections (chosen service, arrival time, kids’ check-in, etc.) in a tidy vertical stack. The bottom of the panel ends above the fold for typical content, but as the user scrolls, both the panel and results list move together, maintaining the traditional, non-sticky feel. On tablets and smaller screens, once the available width drops below a certain threshold, the visit-plan-summary-panel breaks out of this floated position and stacks beneath the service-results-list, taking 100% of the main column width with a 24–32px vertical gap above it, preserving readability on narrow layouts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, behaves like a right-floating aside within the main content column: it is positioned to the right of the service-results-list content area but scrolls normally with the page (not sticky). On smaller screens, it reverts to a standard block-level element in the vertical flow and spans the full column width below the results list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-results-handler is not a standalone block at the page level but an informational state embedded within the service-results-list area. When activated (i.e., when no services match the current filters), it appears centered within the results list’s content region, occupying the same horizontal space that the list items normally would. Its left alignment follows the same left margin as the regular result rows, and its right side respects the same padding that leaves room for the visit-plan-summary-panel on larger screens. Vertically, it replaces the list items, typically appearing 24px below the bottom edge of the service-filters-panel (the same distance where the first result row would normally appear). The message itself is presented as a single, clean block of text—potentially with a short explanatory sentence and a secondary line suggesting how to adjust filters—surrounded by modest padding (16–24px) and optionally a very subtle border or background tint to differentiate it from the surrounding white space. If the visit-plan-summary-panel is present, this no-results message still appears in the left main portion of the column, with the summary panel remaining visible on the right for continuity. When the handler is visible, the vertical space below it is kept relatively minimal, allowing the message to feel central without appearing lost in empty space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears conditionally within the service-results-list container, effectively replacing the list of results. It remains centered in the main content area and scrolls naturally with the page; it does not alter the layout of the surrounding columns beyond hiding the individual result items.

name: Service Details
title: Service Details
description: Displays detailed information for a selected worship service and lets users add the service and its children’s programs to a visit plan, with a live visit-plan summary.

core_features: Load and display full details for the worship service identified by the serviceId URL parameter using getServiceDetails(serviceId).
Show date, start/end time, day of week, service style, location, and teaching pastor information for the selected worship service.
List all active children’s programs associated with the service, including age ranges, timing, and locations, using the childrensPrograms data from getServiceDetails.
Indicate whether the currently viewed service is already part of the current visit plan using the isInCurrentVisitPlan flag from getServiceDetails and the summary from getCurrentVisitPlanSummary.
Provide an 'Add to Visit Plan' action that uses addServiceToVisitPlan(serviceId) to add or replace the selected service in the user’s current visit plan.

Provide an 'Add Children’s Program' action for each children’s program that uses addChildrensProgramToVisitPlan(childrenProgramId) and reflects membership via each program’s isInCurrentVisitPlan flag.
Display a compact visit-plan summary panel that shows the selected service and children’s programs using getCurrentVisitPlanSummary().
Refresh the on-page visit-plan summary and service/program in-plan indicators after each successful addServiceToVisitPlan or addChildrensProgramToVisitPlan call.
Handle loading and error states for all interface calls (initial load, add service, add children’s program) without relying on any hardcoded data values.
user_workflows:
1) View service details from another page:
   - User clicks a link to service_detail.html that includes a serviceId query parameter.
   - The page reads serviceId from the query string.
   - The page calls getServiceDetails(serviceId) to retrieve service and childrensPrograms data.
   - In parallel (or immediately after), the page calls getCurrentVisitPlanSummary() to get the current visit plan.
   - The page displays the service details, children’s program list, and visit-plan summary panel.
   - The service-level 'in visit plan' status and each children’s program 'in visit plan' status are rendered based on the interface responses.

2) Add the viewed service to the visit plan:
   - User clicks the 'Add to Visit Plan' button in the service details header.
   - The page disables the button (or shows a loading state) and calls addServiceToVisitPlan(serviceId) using the current URL’s serviceId.
   - On success, the page updates the visit-plan summary panel using the visitPlanSummary returned from addServiceToVisitPlan.
   - After the mutation, the page calls getCurrentVisitPlanSummary() again to synchronize full plan state.
   - Optionally, the page also re-calls getServiceDetails(serviceId) or updates local service state so that isInCurrentVisitPlan is accurate for the viewed service and its children’s programs.
   - The 'Add to Visit Plan' button changes to indicate the service is now included (e.g., disabled or changed label depending on chosen interaction design).

3) Add a children’s program to the visit plan:
   - User locates a suitable children’s program in the children’s program list.
   - User clicks the corresponding 'Add Children’s Program' action for that program.
   - The page disables that program’s button (or shows a loading state) and calls addChildrensProgramToVisitPlan(childrenProgramId) for that program.
   - On success, the page updates the visit-plan summary panel using the visitPlanSummary returned from addChildrensProgramToVisitPlan.
   - The page then calls getCurrentVisitPlanSummary() to ensure the full plan is in sync.
   - Optionally, the page re-calls getServiceDetails(serviceId) or updates the childrensPrograms list state to reflect isInCurrentVisitPlan on that program.
   - The specific program’s control updates to indicate it is now part of the visit plan.

4) Review current visit plan while viewing a service:
   - At any time while viewing service_detail.html, the user can see the visit-plan summary panel populated by getCurrentVisitPlanSummary().
   - The panel shows whether a service is selected, its date/time and location, and any children’s programs already in the plan.
   - If the current page’s serviceId is different from the plan’s serviceId, the panel and the service header will reflect that mismatch (e.g., plan showing a different service name than the details page).

5) Navigate back to the list of services:
   - User clicks the 'Back to all services' link.
   - The page navigates to visit.html without adding or changing URL parameters (based on the outgoing_connections definition).
   - Any active visit plan remains managed by back-end/session state and is accessible when the user later returns to service_detail.html or other visit-related pages via getCurrentVisitPlanSummary().
interactions: Click 'Back to all services' link: triggers navigation to visit.html (no additional parameters).

Initial page load (onInit / onMount): reads serviceId from the URL query string; if present, calls getServiceDetails(serviceId); independently calls getCurrentVisitPlanSummary() to populate the visit-plan summary panel.

Click 'Add to Visit Plan' button for the service: sends addServiceToVisitPlan(serviceId); on success, updates visit-plan summary state and in-plan indicators; on failure, shows an error message and re-enables the button.

Click 'Add Children’s Program' button for a specific program: sends addChildrensProgramToVisitPlan(childrenProgramId); on success, updates visit-plan summary state and in-plan indicators for that program; on failure, shows an error message and re-enables that button.
Handling of loading states: while getServiceDetails or getCurrentVisitPlanSummary are pending, the relevant components show loading indicators and disable actions that depend on that data.

Handling of error states: if getServiceDetails or getCurrentVisitPlanSummary fail, the page displays error messaging in the relevant components and does not attempt to perform actions that require missing data until the user retries or navigates away.

URL-based context switching: if the user navigates to the same page with a different serviceId in the URL, the page re-runs getServiceDetails(newServiceId) and re-renders service details and children’s program list while reusing the existing getCurrentVisitPlanSummary state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is derived from the serviceId URL parameter and the responses from the assigned interfaces. On initial load and whenever the URL changes to service_detail.html?serviceId=XYZ, the page reads the serviceId value from the query string. If a serviceId is present, getServiceDetails(serviceId) is called to fetch the specific WorshipService and its associated childrensPrograms, including isInCurrentVisitPlan flags. Regardless of serviceId, getCurrentVisitPlanSummary() is called to determine whether there is an existing VisitPlan and, if so, which service and children’s programs it includes. The visit plan itself is not stored in localStorage; it remains managed by the back end or session and is always retrieved via getCurrentVisitPlanSummary. When addServiceToVisitPlan(serviceId) or addChildrensProgramToVisitPlan(childrenProgramId) are called, they update server-side visit-plan state. After each successful mutation, the page refreshes state by calling getCurrentVisitPlanSummary() again (and optionally getServiceDetails(serviceId)) to keep the UI consistent. The URL remains the single source for which service is in focus; it is not changed by mutations on this page. Navigating away via the 'Back to all services' link switches to visit.html without parameters; the plan state persists only through the interfaces, not through any URL or localStorage storage on this page.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and subsequent refreshes for the page. On initialization, reads the serviceId from the URL query string, calls getServiceDetails(serviceId) if available, and calls getCurrentVisitPlanSummary(). Manages high-level loading and error states and passes fetched data to child components.

Displays key information about the selected worship service (name, date/time, day of week, service style, location, and teaching pastor) and provides the main 'Add to Visit Plan' action. Reflects whether the service is already in the current visit plan using the isInCurrentVisitPlan field from getServiceDetails and/or comparison with the VisitPlan summary.

Renders a list of children’s programs associated with the current service using the childrensPrograms array from getServiceDetails. For each program, shows name, age range, timing information, location, capacity (if available), and whether it is already in the current visit plan via each item’s isInCurrentVisitPlan flag. Delegates per-program actions to child row components.

Represents a single children’s program row within the list. Displays program-specific data (name, ageRangeLabel, ageMin/ageMax, checkinStartTime, programStartDateTime, programEndDateTime, location, capacity, isActive). Provides an 'Add Children’s Program' action that is enabled only when the program is active and not already in the visit plan. Updates its visual state based on isInCurrentVisitPlan.

Displays a compact summary of the current visit plan as returned by getCurrentVisitPlanSummary(). Shows whether a plan exists, the selected service (service name, date/time, location), and any children’s programs added (name, ageRangeLabel, location). Updates whenever the visit plan changes due to addServiceToVisitPlan or addChildrensProgramToVisitPlan calls.

Encapsulates the logic for adding the currently viewed service to the visit plan. Receives the current serviceId from the page controller or URL, calls addServiceToVisitPlan(serviceId) when triggered, manages loading and error states for that action, and notifies other components (like the header and summary panel) to refresh by invoking getCurrentVisitPlanSummary().

Handles adding an individual children’s program to the visit plan. When a user clicks 'Add Children’s Program' on a list item, this component calls addChildrensProgramToVisitPlan(childrenProgramId), manages the request’s loading and error states, and then triggers a refresh of getCurrentVisitPlanSummary() and, if needed, getServiceDetails(serviceId) to keep program isInCurrentVisitPlan flags consistent.

Provides a simple navigation control labeled to return the user to the list of services. When activated, navigates to visit.html as specified in outgoing_connections, without altering visit-plan state.

Shows and hides loading indicators for the service details area and children’s program list while getServiceDetails(serviceId) is in progress. Also displays an appropriate error message if the service details cannot be loaded or if serviceId is missing or invalid, guiding the user to use the navigation controls.

Manages loading and error feedback for getCurrentVisitPlanSummary() and visit-plan-related mutations. While plan data is being fetched or updated, indicates a loading state inside the visit-plan summary panel. On errors, shows clear messaging without exposing any hardcoded data values.

Displays transient success or error messages based on the results of addServiceToVisitPlan and addChildrensProgramToVisitPlan calls. Messages are derived from the message field in the interface responses where available, ensuring no hardcoded business data is used.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with approximately 3–4 columns for the left sidebar and 8–9 columns for the main content area within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the global nav and a full-width photographic hero, the Service Details content sits in a centered, max-width container. This container is split into two columns: a narrower left column used as a functional sidebar for navigation and visit-plan context, and a wider right column for the detailed service content, actions, and children’s programs. The page flows vertically: back link and visit-plan summary in the left sidebar; service header, add-to-plan actions, feedback messages, and children’s program list stacked in the right column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the global nav and a full-width photographic hero, the Service Details content sits in a centered, max-width container. This container is split into two columns: a narrower left column used as a functional sidebar for navigation and visit-plan context, and a wider right column for the detailed service content, actions, and children’s programs. The page flows vertically: back link and visit-plan summary in the left sidebar; service header, add-to-plan actions, feedback messages, and children’s program list stacked in the right column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies 25–30% of the viewport height and spans 100% width. The content container below has a max width of about 1040–1120px and is centered. Inside it, the left sidebar takes roughly 25–30% of the content width, and the main content column takes 70–75%. Vertical spacing between major blocks (header, feedback, lists) is in the 24–32px range, while internal padding in panels and list items uses 8–16px values. The visit-plan summary panel uses most of the left column’s vertical space, while the right column expands to accommodate detailed text and lists.
layout_strategies.content_density: The sidebar is moderately dense, with the back link and summary panel stacked with modest spacing and thin dividers echoing the original navigation list. The main content column is more open: the service title has generous spacing above and below, the intro/metadata block is clearly separated, and the children’s program list has distinct list items with comfortable padding but not excessive whitespace. Overall density feels like a traditional informational site—functional and readable without feeling airy or overly modern.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the global nav and hero, the eye is drawn first to the large service title in the right main column. Directly underneath, the service metadata/intro and main add-to-plan button invite interaction. From there, the eye moves downward through any feedback messages and into the children’s programs list. The left sidebar—containing the back link and a clearly framed visit-plan summary—sits in peripheral view and becomes a secondary focus as users consider their selections. When loading states are active, they appear in the same physical positions as their respective content, preserving the expected flow.

layout.overall_description: The Service Details page sits beneath the church website’s global header and a warm, full-width hero image that might show the sanctuary or congregation. Immediately below this hero, a centered content band appears, divided into a narrower left sidebar and a wider right main column, both aligned to the same top baseline. At the top of the left column, a simple text-based back link allows users to return to the list of services; beneath it, a clean, boxed visit-plan summary panel fills most of the sidebar height, showing a compact overview of the planned visit. In the right column, a prominent service title and its key details (time, location, brief description) form the visual anchor at the top, with an ‘Add this service to your visit plan’ control visually paired with the header. Below this, plan-related feedback messages surface confirmations or errors inline, followed by a vertically stacked list of children’s programs, each with its own add-to-plan action aligned on the right. The layout uses simple horizontal rules, flat backgrounds, and 8–16px padding to maintain a traditional, content-first church aesthetic. As users interact, loading states and feedback messages appear exactly where changes occur—within the main content area and the visit-plan summary—allowing the eye to travel naturally from the hero, to the service title, to the detailed options, while keeping the evolving visit plan visible in the sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is not visually rendered but defines the structure of the entire main content band beneath the hero. It controls a two-column layout: a left sidebar occupying roughly 28% of the centered content width and a right main content column occupying roughly 72%. When the service data is still loading, it replaces the entire right column’s content (service header, feedback messages, children’s program list, and add-to-plan actions) with the service-detail-loading-state placeholder, while leaving the left column’s general structure (back link and visit-plan summary shell) visible so the overall layout remains recognizable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible structural/controller element; governs conditional rendering of the main column’s content versus its loading state without affecting the page’s fixed grid or container dimensions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned at the very top of the right main content column, the service header spans the full width of that column from left padding to right padding. It sits horizontally aligned with the back-to-services-link in the left sidebar, so their top edges form a straight visual line across the content band. The header includes the service title as a large, bold line centered on the right column’s text area, with approximately 24–32px of space above it and 16–24px below. Directly under the title, a short intro line or key detail (e.g., time and location) uses a slightly emphasized, possibly colored text treatment similar to the green intro paragraph from the mockup, occupying roughly 70–80% of the column width and leaving comfortable right-side breathing room. The header’s content defines the main focal point for the page before the user scrolls down into the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the right column; its height grows with content but it always retains generous top and bottom margins to establish hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The children-program-list appears directly beneath the service header and any intervening feedback messages, occupying the full available width of the right main column. There is approximately 24px of vertical space separating the header/feedback block from the top of the list. The list is a vertical stack of list items, running from left padding to right padding of the column, with each item separated by a subtle horizontal rule or a 16–24px vertical gap. The list can extend downward beyond the fold, but maintains consistent left alignment with the service header text, reinforcing the column’s straight vertical reading line.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list within the right column; it expands downward as items are added and pushes the page height, while preserving a consistent top offset below the header/feedback block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each children-program-list-item is a horizontally oriented row within the main list, occupying nearly the full width of the right column. Internally, it uses 12–16px of padding on all sides. The program name and key details (age group, time, room) are left-aligned and sit in the left 60–70% of the item’s width. On the far right side of the same row, aligned vertically to the middle, sits the child-program-add-to-plan-action control, visually echoing the right-aligned illustration from the mockup by providing a clear focal point at the end of the line. Items are separated from each other by a light horizontal rule or a 16px gap, resulting in a moderately dense but orderly stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static rows within the list; they maintain a consistent height proportional to their content and keep their right-aligned action fixed at the same horizontal position across all items for easy scanning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit-plan-summary-panel sits in the left sidebar, directly below the back-to-services-link, separated from it by approximately 16–24px and a thin horizontal rule. The panel occupies nearly the full width of the sidebar, with 12–16px internal padding on all sides. It presents a compact summary of the user’s current visit plan (selected service and children’s programs) in a vertical stack. The panel’s top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the service header in the right column once the back link and its small gap are accounted for. Vertically, the summary panel extends downward and becomes the dominant element in the sidebar, filling most of its height; on taller screens it may end above the fold, leaving some white space beneath. Its design is flat and box-like, perhaps with a subtle border or background tint to differentiate it from the page background without heavy shadows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves like a sidebar card. On larger screens it can be configured as sticky relative to the viewport top (e.g., fixed within the visible area once scrolled), so the plan summary remains visible as users scroll through the right-column content; on smaller screens, it falls back to a standard static block to avoid overlap.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-add-to-plan-action control is visually paired with the service header at the top of the right column. It appears either to the right of the service title (on the same horizontal row) or just beneath the title but aligned to the right edge of the text column. In either case, it sits within the upper 20–25% of the visible content band and occupies roughly the width of a standard button (about 15–20% of the column’s width), leaving generous white space around it. There is typically 8–16px of vertical space between the title and the control if they’re stacked. This placement mirrors the mockup’s top-of-article utility (like an RSS badge) and ensures the primary add-to-plan action is seen immediately after the service details are read.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline with the service header block. Its position is responsive: on wider screens it can sit on the same line as the title by leveraging a right-aligned column; on narrower screens it naturally wraps to a new line while maintaining right alignment within the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each children-program-list-item, the child-program-add-to-plan-action is a compact control anchored to the far right edge of the row. It is vertically centered relative to the program name and details text. The control’s width is minimal—only as wide as a short label or icon+label—so that the program description remains the dominant visual. There is about 8–12px of horizontal padding between the right edge of the button and the right padding of the list item, maintaining a consistent right margin across all items. This repetitive placement along the right edge creates a column of call-to-action elements that users can quickly scan down, similar to how the illustrative icon in the mockup draws the eye along the right side of the text block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each list item; its position is fixed to the right side of the item and remains aligned vertically with the text content, maintaining consistent spacing even as lines wrap or items become taller.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-services-link is positioned at the top-left of the content band, in the left sidebar, just below the hero image and aligned with the left edge of the main content container. It occupies only a single line of text, with 8–12px vertical padding to give it click-friendly height but keep it visually light. There is roughly 24px of space between the bottom of the hero and this link, matching the spacing above the service header on the right. A thin horizontal rule or a 16px gap separates it from the visit-plan-summary-panel below, echoing the original mockup’s stacked sidebar navigation links. The link is the first interactive element in the sidebar and serves as a subtle secondary navigation option compared to the main content on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored to the top of the left column. It does not stick during scroll; if the visit-plan-summary-panel is sticky, this link scrolls away with the page content above the sticky threshold.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the service-detail-loading-state occupies the entire right main content column in place of the service-header, service-add-to-plan-action, plan-feedback-messages, and children-program-list. It starts at the same top offset where the service-header would appear, aligned horizontally with the top of the visit-plan-summary-panel. The loading state might be represented by grey blocks or skeleton lines where the title, metadata, and list items normally sit, extending downward for approximately the same height as a typical first viewport of content. The left sidebar remains visible, so the page still reads as a two-column layout even while the right side is in a loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the right column’s substantive content while data is being fetched; it is fully static and scrollable if the placeholder exceeds the viewport height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit-plan-loading-state appears inside the bounds of the visit-plan-summary-panel in the left sidebar whenever the plan data is in transition (for example, immediately after adding a service or children’s program). It overlays or replaces the normal summary contents while maintaining the panel’s outer dimensions and border. The placeholder elements (e.g., small skeleton rows or a centered spinner) are centered within the panel, both horizontally and vertically, leaving 12–16px of padding between the placeholder and the panel edges so the panel still feels like a cohesive card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Confined to the visit-plan-summary-panel’s area; it does not shift the panel’s position, only its inner content. It appears and disappears inline without affecting the overall sidebar height or the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Plan-feedback-messages are displayed in the right main column directly beneath the service header and its add-to-plan action, and above the children-program-list. They span the full width of the text column, from left to right padding, and are separated from the header by roughly 8–16px to keep them visually connected to the action that triggered them. Each message appears as a slim horizontal band with modest 8–12px vertical padding and a flat, lightly tinted background to differentiate success, warning, or error states, echoing the utilitarian feel of the site. A 16–24px gap separates the bottom of the last feedback message from the top of the children-program-list, so the list reads as a new section. When no messages are present, this vertical space collapses and the list sits closer to the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline block within the right column. Messages appear and disappear without shifting the overall layout structure: they simply occupy or free up vertical space between the header and the list. If multiple messages are stacked, they remain within this same region and scroll normally with the page.

name: Events & Calendar
title: Events & Calendar
description: Browse and manage church events in calendar, week, or list view with filters, sorting, and quick saving to My Events.

core_features: Load dynamic filter and sort options using getEventFilterOptions, including categories, date range presets, day-of-week options, time range presets, price filters, and sort choices.
Display events using searchEvents with support for calendar, week, and list views, driven by the view URL parameter and internal view state.
Filter events by category, date range, day of week, time range, and price, synchronizing applicable filters with URL parameters.
Sort events using sort options from getEventFilterOptions and the sortBy URL parameter.
Allow navigation from event listings to the Event Detail & Registration page (event_detail.html?eventId=...).
Enable quick saving of events to the user's My Events list using saveEventToMyEvents, with visual indication of saved items.
Provide pagination or incremental loading for event results using searchEvents page and pageSize parameters.
Offer a direct shortcut to the My Events page (my_events.html) from within the Events & Calendar page.
user_workflows: View events with default or incoming filters: 1) User navigates to events.html (optionally with URL parameters such as view, category, dateFrom, dateTo, dayOfWeek, timeFrom, timeTo, priceFilter, sortBy). 2) Page loads and calls getEventFilterOptions to populate filter controls. 3) Page reads URL parameters and initializes EventViewState and filter controls accordingly. 4) Page calls searchEvents with the derived filters, viewType (from view or default), sortBy, and initial page values. 5) Events are rendered in the appropriate view (calendar, week, or list) with the current filter and sort state reflected in the UI.

Change view between calendar, week, and list: 1) User clicks a view toggle control. 2) The viewType in EventViewState is updated. 3) The view URL parameter is updated to match the selected view type. 4) searchEvents is called again with the new viewType while preserving existing filters, sortBy, and pagination reset to the first page. 5) The events results are re-rendered in the selected view.

Filter events by category and date range: 1) User changes the category filter (e.g., via a dropdown populated from getEventFilterOptions.categoryOptions). 2) The selected category is stored in EventViewState and the category URL parameter is updated. 3) If the user chooses a date preset, the page maps that preset's startDate and endDate to the dateFrom and dateTo URL parameters and EventViewState currentStartDate/currentEndDate. 4) searchEvents is called with updated filters. 5) Event listings update based on the new category and date range.

Filter events by day of week, time, and price: 1) User selects one or more days of week (from options provided by getEventFilterOptions.dayOfWeekOptions). 2) Selected days are stored in EventViewState.selectedDaysOfWeek and serialized into the dayOfWeek URL parameter as a comma-separated list. 3) User optionally sets time range (either by selecting a time preset from getEventFilterOptions.timeRangePresets or by adjusting manual timeFrom/timeTo controls). 4) timeFrom and timeTo values are stored in EventViewState and mirrored to corresponding URL parameters. 5) User selects a price filter option (from getEventFilterOptions.priceOptions); this is stored and mirrored to priceFilter in the URL. 6) searchEvents is called with updated filters, and results refresh.

Sort events: 1) User selects a sort option from a control populated with getEventFilterOptions.sortOptions. 2) The selected sortBy value is stored in EventViewState and written to the sortBy URL parameter. 3) searchEvents is called with the new sortBy while preserving other filters and viewType. 4) Event listings refresh in the new order.

Browse event details: 1) User clicks on an event card in any view. 2) The eventId for that card is used to navigate to event_detail.html?eventId=... via standard link navigation. 3) The Event Detail & Registration page uses this eventId to load detailed information (outside the scope of this page).

Save an event to My Events: 1) User clicks a Save/Add to My Events button on an event card. 2) The button triggers saveEventToMyEvents with the corresponding eventId. 3) On success, the component reads the response (success, isNewlyAdded, message). 4) If successful, the in-memory isSaved state for that event is updated (and if needed, a subsequent searchEvents call may be made to refresh isSaved flags). 5) The button label and/or state change to indicate the event is saved. 6) User can click a My Events shortcut to open my_events.html in a new navigation.

Paginate or load more events: 1) User reaches the bottom of the event list or clicks a pagination control. 2) The page parameter in the URL is updated (if implemented as a query parameter) or maintained in internal state. 3) searchEvents is called with the new page value while keeping filters, sortBy, and viewType unchanged. 4) New events are either appended to the existing list (for load-more patterns) or replace the list (for paged results), depending on the chosen pagination behavior.

Share a filtered view: 1) User adjusts view, filters, and sorting until desired. 2) The corresponding URL parameters (view, category, dateFrom, dateTo, dayOfWeek, timeFrom, timeTo, priceFilter, sortBy, and optionally page) reflect the current state. 3) User copies the page URL. 4) Another visitor opens this URL; on load, the page reads the URL parameters, applies them to EventViewState and filter controls, and calls searchEvents so the same filtered view is reproduced.
interactions: View toggle interaction: Clicking a view-type control triggers onViewChange, which updates EventViewState.viewType, updates the view URL parameter, resets pagination, and calls searchEvents with the new viewType.

Category filter interaction: Changing the category selection triggers onCategoryChange, which updates EventViewState.selectedCategory, updates the category URL parameter, and calls searchEvents with updated filters.

Date range selection: Selecting a date range preset triggers onDatePresetChange, which reads the preset's startDate and endDate from getEventFilterOptions, sets EventViewState.currentStartDate/currentEndDate, updates dateFrom/dateTo URL parameters, and calls searchEvents. Adjusting manual date fields (if provided) triggers onDateRangeChange with the same behavior.

Day-of-week filter interaction: Selecting or deselecting a day-of-week checkbox triggers onDayOfWeekChange, which updates EventViewState.selectedDaysOfWeek, serializes the array into a comma-separated string (e.g., monday,tuesday) for the dayOfWeek URL parameter, and calls searchEvents.

Time range filter interaction: Selecting a time range preset triggers onTimePresetChange, which loads timeFrom/timeTo values from the preset, sets EventViewState.timeFilterStart/timeFilterEnd, updates timeFrom/timeTo URL parameters, and calls searchEvents. Editing manual time inputs triggers onTimeRangeChange with similar behavior.
Price filter interaction: Changing the price option triggers onPriceFilterChange, which updates EventViewState price state, updates priceFilter URL parameter, and calls searchEvents.
Sort interaction: Changing the sort dropdown triggers onSortChange, which updates EventViewState sortBy, updates sortBy URL parameter, resets pagination, and calls searchEvents.

Search interaction (query input): Submitting the search form triggers onSearchSubmit, which passes the entered query string into searchEvents while keeping existing filters and viewType. The query can be maintained in component state and optionally synchronized to a dedicated URL parameter if desired by implementation, without affecting the required parameters.

Event card click: Clicking anywhere on an event card (aside from controls like Save) triggers onEventCardClick, which navigates the browser to event_detail.html?eventId=... using the eventId from the searchEvents result.

Save/Add to My Events interaction: Clicking the save button on an event card triggers onSaveEventClick, which calls saveEventToMyEvents with the eventId. Based on the API response, the component updates the visual isSaved state for that event and may display feedback using the message field.
My Events shortcut interaction: Clicking a My Events link or button triggers onMyEventsClick, which navigates to my_events.html in the same tab or new tab according to design.

Pagination / Load more interaction: Clicking pagination controls or a Load More button triggers onPageChange or onLoadMore. onPageChange updates the page parameter (if present) and calls searchEvents with the new page; onLoadMore increments the page value and appends results if hasMore is true.

URL change handling: When the browser's URL changes via back/forward navigation, a listener triggers onUrlParamsChange, which re-parses view, category, dateFrom, dateTo, dayOfWeek, timeFrom, timeTo, priceFilter, sortBy (and optionally page), updates EventViewState and filter controls, and calls searchEvents to keep the UI consistent.
