Each sermon-card is separated by either a thin horizontal rule extending across the column or a subtle bottom border, reflecting the mockup’s preference for flat, minimal dividers over raised cards. The list can extend vertically as far as needed, filling the right column down the page, while the left filter sidebar continues alongside it. There is a clear gap of roughly 24–32px between the last sermon-card and the pagination controls at the bottom of the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrollable vertical list within the right column; expands and contracts depending on the number of items, always remaining anchored below the summary and above the pagination.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each sermon-card is a full-width row inside the sermon list, occupying 100% of the right column’s width between its left and right padding. The card content is arranged in a simple horizontal-then-vertical structure: the sermon title appears prominently at the top-left of the card area, with date, speaker, and duration metadata in a smaller line beneath it. Any action elements, such as a subtle 'Watch Later' icon/button, are aligned to the right side of the card’s row, roughly in line with the title or metadata. Vertical padding inside each card is modest (about 12–16px top and bottom), and there is no heavy box shadow—only a light bottom border or a thin rule below each card, matching the clean, flat aesthetic. The visual effect is a compact but readable list where titles are easy to scan from top to bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Repeated, stacked element within the sermon-list container; it does not have independent positioning and simply expands vertically to fit its content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sermon pagination controls sit directly below the sermon list in the right main column. They are placed after a clear vertical gap of approximately 24–32px from the last sermon-card, giving the sense of the list ending before the navigation begins. The controls are arranged in a horizontal row of simple text links or buttons (e.g., Previous, page numbers, Next) with minimal styling and flat appearance, consistent with the traditional informational feel. The entire pagination row is aligned toward the right edge of the main column, while still respecting the column’s inner padding, so it feels like a footer utility for the list rather than a major focal point. There is additional bottom margin (about 32px) between the pagination and the bottom of the content container or the next section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored at the bottom of the sermon-list region; scrolls normally with the page and remains at the end of the content sequence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sermon detail view occupies the same right main column area as the sermon list and pagination, but appears instead of them when a specific sermon is selected. It stretches the full width of the right column, starting at the same vertical position as the results controls previously did. At the top of the detail view, a back or 'All Sermons' link is aligned left, followed by a large sermon title styled similarly to the main page heading but slightly smaller, with 16–24px of space above and below for emphasis. Below the title, key metadata (date, speaker, series) are arranged in a compact horizontal or stacked layout, followed by the main media player or embedded video centered in the column. Additional description, scripture references, and related links flow vertically beneath, with section headings separated by 24–32px of vertical spacing to keep the reading experience orderly. Throughout, the left filter sidebar remains untouched, maintaining the two-column structure. When active, the detail view pushes the page height down and scrolls like a long article within the bounds of the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the sermon-list region within the right column: it replaces the list and pagination in the layout when opened, then yields that space back when the user navigates back to browsing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Watch Later shortcut is a navigation-style link within the sermon-filter-panel on the left sidebar. It appears near the top of the panel, just below any overarching 'Filters' or 'Browse' label, occupying the full available width of the sidebar minus its inner padding. Vertically, it uses similar padding to other sidebar items (around 12–16px top and bottom) and is separated from adjacent filter sections by a thin horizontal rule above and/or below. Visually, it is treated like the 'current page' state in the original mockup’s sidebar: a slightly stronger text weight or color and perhaps a subtle background tint across the full width of the row, making it stand out among the other, more utilitarian filter options while still remaining secondary to the main right-column content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static item within the left sidebar’s vertical stack; scrolls with the filter panel and does not change position independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager has no direct visual presence on the page. Conceptually, it sits behind all interactive components in the two-column layout—the search form, filter panel, sort control, results summary, sermon list, pagination, and detail view—keeping their state synchronized with the browser URL. It ensures that the current filters, search query, page number, and whether the user is in list or detail mode are reflected in the URL without adding any additional elements to the visual layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller with zero layout footprint; it does not occupy any space, does not affect the flow of elements, and behaves purely as an invisible behavioral layer over the existing layout.

name: Watch Later Playlist
title: Watch Later Playlist
description: Displays the user’s Watch Later sermons, allows playback, reordering, and removal, and highlights newly added sermons when navigated from the Sermons page.

core_features: Load the user’s Watch Later playlist using getWatchLaterPlaylist and display all sermons in a list with key metadata (title, date, speaker, duration, topics, series).
Allow users to start playback of any sermon directly from the playlist (video or audio) using getSermonDetails for the selected sermon.
Provide controls for sorting the playlist (e.g., by date, title, duration) and re-rendering the current list order client-side without new API calls.
Enable removal of individual sermons from the playlist using removeSermonFromWatchLater and update the in-memory playlist to reflect changes.
Handle the highlightSermonId URL parameter to scroll to and visually emphasize a specific sermon that was just added from sermons.html.
Provide navigation back to the Sermons page via a "Back to Sermons" link (sermons.html) while preserving or adding URL parameters as needed.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – View Watch Later playlist: (1) User navigates to playlist.html via header navigation or a link from another page; (2) On page load, the page-init component parses URL parameters (including optional highlightSermonId); (3) The page calls getWatchLaterPlaylist; (4) When the response returns, the playlist list component renders the sermons in the default or URL-specified sort order; (5) If no sermons exist, the empty-state component displays a message and a link back to sermons.html.

Workflow 2 – Highlight a sermon after adding from Sermons page: (1) User adds a sermon to Watch Later on sermons.html; (2) That page redirects or links to playlist.html with ?highlightSermonId={id}; (3) On load, playlist.html calls getWatchLaterPlaylist; (4) After rendering, the playlist list component locates the sermon matching highlightSermonId, scrolls it into view, and applies a temporary highlighted state; (5) An optional notification indicates that the sermon has been added and highlighted.

Workflow 3 – Play a sermon from the playlist: (1) User clicks a sermon row or a dedicated "Play" action within that row; (2) The page updates the URL to include activeSermonId={id} (preserving other parameters like sort and highlightSermonId); (3) The playback panel component detects the activeSermonId change and calls getSermonDetails with that ID; (4) When getSermonDetails returns, the playback panel shows the sermon metadata and initializes playback using the available videoUrl or audioUrl; (5) The currently active sermon row is visually marked as playing.

Workflow 4 – Remove a sermon from Watch Later: (1) User clicks a "Remove" action on a sermon row; (2) A confirmation interaction (inline or dialog) is triggered from the sermon row component; (3) On confirmation, the component calls removeSermonFromWatchLater with the sermonId; (4) If the response indicates success, the local playlist state is updated by removing that sermon from the in-memory list and the view re-renders; (5) If the removed sermon matches activeSermonId, the playback panel clears or shows a non-active state; (6) An optional notification shows that removal succeeded or failed based on the returned success flag and message.

Workflow 5 – Change sorting of the playlist: (1) User interacts with the sort controls (e.g., dropdown) in the playlist controls component; (2) The sort choice updates the URL parameter sortBy and optionally sortDirection; (3) The playlist list component reorders the currently loaded sermons array in memory according to the selected sort values; (4) The reordered list is rendered without further API calls; (5) If a highlightSermonId exists, its position and highlighting remain consistent within the new order.

Workflow 6 – Navigate back to Sermons: (1) User clicks the "Back to Sermons" link in the header of the playlist page; (2) The page navigates to sermons.html, optionally including parameters such as sermonId or topic if the implementation needs to deep link; (3) No playlist API calls are needed during this navigation; (4) User continues browsing sermons from the Sermons page.
interactions: On page load (onMount of the page-init component), parse URL parameters (highlightSermonId, activeSermonId, sortBy, sortDirection) and trigger getWatchLaterPlaylist; store the resulting playlist and sermons in page state.

On successful response from getWatchLaterPlaylist, pass the playlist metadata and sermons array to the playlist header, controls, list, and empty-state components; if highlightSermonId exists, instruct the list to locate and highlight that sermon.
On click of a sermon row or "Play" button (onClickPlay), update the URL to include activeSermonId for that sermon; trigger getSermonDetails(sermonId) and bind the result to the playback panel.

On completion of getSermonDetails, bind the sermon data (including videoUrl, audioUrl, description, scriptureReference, durationMinutes, isInWatchLater) to the playback panel for playback and detailed display.

On click of "Remove" for a sermon (onClickRemove), optionally open a confirmation dialog or inline confirmation; on confirm, call removeSermonFromWatchLater(sermonId); on success, remove that sermon from the local playlist state and re-render the list; on failure, display an error notification using the returned message.
On change of sort selection (onChangeSort), update the sortBy and sortDirection URL parameters and reorder the in-memory sermons list accordingly; re-render the list in the new order.
On click of the "Back to Sermons" link (onClickBackToSermons), navigate to sermons.html with any desired query parameters, without modifying playlist state further.
On detection that the playlist response contains no sermons, display the empty-state component instead of the list and playback panel; allow the user to navigate to sermons.html to add sermons.

On URL change (e.g., via browser navigation or in-page interactions), re-parse URL parameters; if activeSermonId changes, load that sermon’s details via getSermonDetails; if sort parameters change, reapply sorting to the current sermons array; if highlightSermonId appears or changes, re-run the highlighting logic.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters to manage view and interaction state. (1) highlightSermonId: When present (e.g., playlist.html?highlightSermonId={id}), after getWatchLaterPlaylist completes and the list is rendered, the list component searches for the sermon with sermonId matching highlightSermonId, scrolls that item into view, and applies a temporary visual highlight. The parameter is kept in the URL for potential future reference but does not trigger additional API calls by itself. (2) activeSermonId: When present (e.g., playlist.html?activeSermonId={id}), the playback panel treats that sermon as the currently active one. On initial page load or whenever activeSermonId changes, the page calls getSermonDetails(sermonId=activeSermonId) and binds the returned sermon data to the playback panel. Clicking a sermon’s play action updates activeSermonId in the URL (preserving any existing highlightSermonId and sorting parameters), which also supports browser back/forward navigation restoring the last active sermon. (3) sortBy and sortDirection: Sorting state is managed with URL parameters such as sortBy (e.g., 'date', 'title', 'duration') and sortDirection (e.g., 'asc', 'desc') so that the playlist order is reproducible and shareable via URL. When these parameters change via the sort controls, the page does not call any additional APIs but reorders the current sermons array returned by getWatchLaterPlaylist and re-renders the list accordingly. On first load, if sort parameters are absent, a default sort (e.g., order returned by getWatchLaterPlaylist) is used. (4) Other parameters: Additional page-level flags (such as viewMode for compact vs detailed list) may be represented via URL parameters, and the corresponding components read them on load and on URL changes to adjust rendering. No playlist data is stored in localStorage; all data is obtained via the assigned interfaces, and all view state is encoded in the URL for consistency and deep-linking.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page load: parses URL parameters (highlightSermonId, activeSermonId, sortBy, sortDirection), calls getWatchLaterPlaylist, and distributes the playlist and sermons data to child components. It also listens to URL changes (e.g., browser back/forward) to update activeSermonId and sorting state, triggering getSermonDetails when activeSermonId changes.
Displays the functional page title and core playlist metadata, such as the playlist name and count of sermons, and provides the "Back to Sermons" navigation link.

Provides user controls for managing the displayed playlist view, primarily sorting options (e.g., by date, title, duration) and optional view mode toggles. It reads initial sortBy and sortDirection from URL parameters and updates them when the user changes the sort selection.

Shows an informational message and navigation options when getWatchLaterPlaylist returns a playlist with no sermons. It suggests navigating to the Sermons page to add items and hides playback-related components.

Renders the list of sermons in the Watch Later playlist using the sermons array from getWatchLaterPlaylist. Applies sorting based on sortBy and sortDirection, marks the sermon corresponding to activeSermonId as active, and applies a temporary highlight to the sermon corresponding to highlightSermonId if present. Delegates individual item interactions to sermon row components.

Represents a single sermon row in the playlist. Displays sermon information (title, date, speakerName, topics, series, durationMinutes) using data from getWatchLaterPlaylist. Provides actions to play (which triggers selection of this sermon) and to remove the sermon from the playlist. It can render special states such as active (currently playing) and highlighted (matches highlightSermonId).

Handles user confirmation for removing a sermon from the playlist. When triggered by a sermon row, it shows the relevant sermon context (without hardcoded values) and on confirmation calls removeSermonFromWatchLater. After a successful response, it instructs the playlist list component to update the in-memory sermons collection and, if applicable, the playback panel to clear active playback for that sermon.

Displays the currently selected sermon details and embeds the appropriate media player (video or audio) using data from getSermonDetails for the sermon identified by activeSermonId. If no activeSermonId is set or if the playlist is empty, it shows a non-playing state. It may also provide a quick action to remove the currently playing sermon using removeSermonFromWatchLater.

Shows transient messages such as confirmation that a sermon added from the Sermons page has been included and highlighted, or error/success messages from removeSermonFromWatchLater and getSermonDetails. It may reference the sermon involved without using hardcoded values, relying solely on dynamic data from the APIs.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with approximately 3–4 columns for the left sidebar and 8–9 columns for the main content area within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same overall pattern as the mockup: global top navigation and logo (outside this spec), then a full-width photographic hero. Below the hero, a centered max-width content container is split into two columns: a left sidebar with vertical section navigation (site-wide) and a right main content column dedicated to the Watch Later Playlist. Inside the main column, the playlist page content flows from top to bottom: page initialization (invisible), then playlist header, optional notification banner, playlist controls, and then a two-panel content area where the sermon list sits on the left and the playback panel on the right. The empty state replaces the list area when there are no items, and the confirmation dialog appears above everything as an overlay.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same overall pattern as the mockup: global top navigation and logo (outside this spec), then a full-width photographic hero. Below the hero, a centered max-width content container is split into two columns: a left sidebar with vertical section navigation (site-wide) and a right main content column dedicated to the Watch Later Playlist. Inside the main column, the playlist page content flows from top to bottom: page initialization (invisible), then playlist header, optional notification banner, playlist controls, and then a two-panel content area where the sermon list sits on the left and the playback panel on the right. The empty state replaces the list area when there are no items, and the confirmation dialog appears above everything as an overlay.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero image spans 100% width and roughly 25–30% of the initial viewport height. Below, the centered content container uses the remaining vertical space. Horizontally, the left navigation sidebar occupies about 25–30% of the container width; the main playlist column uses the remaining 70–75%. Within that main column, the sermon playlist list occupies around 60–65% of the column width on the left, while the playback panel uses about 35–40% on the right, mimicking the text-and-illustration balance from the mockup. Vertical spacing follows the 8px base system: 24–32px gaps between major sections (header, controls, content panels) and 8–16px padding inside individual components.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderate density: the sidebar remains relatively dense with tightly stacked links and subtle separators, while the playlist main column feels more open. The playlist header and notification banner have generous breathing room around them. Playlist controls are compact but not cramped, with 8–12px padding around buttons. The list rows follow the sidebar pattern: single-line height plus 8–12px vertical padding and thin dividers between rows. The playback panel has slightly more padding (16–24px) to echo the more spacious content area treatment from the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup: eyes move from top nav and logo to the large hero image, then naturally to the right main column and the bold playlist title near the top. From the title, attention flows downward through any notification banner, into the playlist controls, then into the left-aligned list of sermons. The playback panel on the right acts like the illustrative icon in the mockup—secondary but visible in peripheral vision—drawing the user’s gaze laterally after they scan the list. The confirmation dialog interrupts this flow when triggered by dimming the background and centering the user’s focus on the decision.

layout.overall_description: The Watch Later Playlist page sits within the church site’s standard shell: a logo and global navigation bar run across the top, followed by a warm, full-width hero photo that fills about the upper quarter of the screen. Beneath this, a centered content band is divided into a narrower left sidebar of site sections and a wider right column for playlist content. In the main column, a compact inline badge leads into a large, traditional-style page title announcing the Watch Later Playlist. Just below, a soft-toned notification banner may appear, spanning the main column width without feeling heavy. Under that, a row of playlist controls stretches left-to-right, still within the main column. The lower half of this column is split visually into two: on the left, a vertically scrolling list of sermons with thin separators, and on the right, a slightly inset playback panel that feels like an illustration field—prominent but clearly secondary to the list. When there are no items, a centered empty-state message replaces the list region, leaving the right playback area absent or visually quiet. If the user chooses to remove an item, the rest of the page dims and a modal confirmation appears centered on the screen, consistent with the site’s sober, utilitarian aesthetic. The overall impression is one of structured calm: content-first, modestly spaced, and easy to scan top-to-bottom while still giving a clear left-right relationship between the list and the current sermon playback.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component does not render visible UI. It lives logically at the top of the main playlist column, running as the page loads to set up data, highlight newly added sermons, and determine whether to show the empty state or the playlist list. It occupies no visual space and does not affect layout; all visible spacing is defined by the other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller with no direct spatial behavior; it runs before or alongside initial render and does not occupy layout space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist header appears at the top of the main content column on the right, just below the hero band and aligned with the top edge of the sidebar links. It spans the full width of the main column (approximately 70–75% of the centered content container). At the very top-left of this area, a small inline badge (e.g., an RSS-style icon or playlist indicator) sits with about 8px of padding around it, followed immediately to its right by the large page title text: “Watch Later Playlist.” The badge and title share a single horizontal row, with the badge occupying only about 5–10% of the row width and the title taking the remaining space, left-aligned. Vertically, there is 24–32px of space above the header (to the hero/content boundary) and 16–24px of space below it before any notification banner, emphasizing it as the primary entry point for the page content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element, anchored to the top of the main content column and scrolling naturally with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist controls bar sits directly below the header area (and below any notification banner, when present) in the main column. It spans nearly the full width of the main column but visually aligns with the left edge of the playlist header text above. The controls—buttons such as “Play All,” “Reorder,” “Clear,” or filters—are arranged horizontally starting from the left, with 8–16px gaps between them, following the moderate density seen in the mockup’s utility elements. The bar itself has 8–12px vertical padding and 16px horizontal padding on both sides, giving it a compact but breathable feel. There is roughly 16–24px of space above it (to separate it from header/banner) and 16–24px below it before the main playlist/list area begins, establishing it as a distinct yet integrated control band. On narrow screens, the controls can wrap to multiple lines while maintaining left alignment, but they remain in the same vertical order between the header and the list/playback area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the flow of the main column; it scrolls with the content and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state display occupies the main content area where the sermon playlist list would normally appear. It is anchored within the main column beneath the playlist controls. Horizontally, it is centered within the main column but constrained to about 60% of the main column width so it feels focused rather than sprawling. Vertically, it sits with approximately 32–48px of margin above it (from the controls) and below it before the end of the main content area, and its content (icon, headline, and helper text) is vertically centered within that block. When the empty state is shown, the sermon playback panel on the right is either hidden or visually minimized to avoid a lopsided layout, so the empty state visually feels like a single centered feature replacing the two-panel list/playback combination. The design uses subtle iconography and soft typography consistent with the site’s traditional tone, with no bold color blocks to maintain the page’s understated aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the sermon-playlist-list in the same spatial region; it appears only when the list is empty and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sermon playlist list forms the left portion of the lower main content area. Starting just below the playlist controls (with 16–24px vertical separation), it aligns with the left edge of the header and controls, occupying about 60–65% of the main column width. The top of the list aligns vertically with the top of the sermon playback panel on the right, creating a cohesive two-panel band. The list is a vertical stack of playlist-item rows with thin horizontal dividers between them, echoing the sidebar navigation style. Each row extends the full width of this left panel. The list area can scroll vertically if it exceeds viewport height, but within the page it maintains the same left and right padding (16px) as other content blocks. A bottom margin of 32–48px separates the end of the list from the footer region, maintaining the moderate content density. Newly added sermons can be subtly highlighted within individual rows (e.g., a soft accent line on the left) without changing the list’s overall structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column but can have its own internal vertical scroll if the page layout calls for a fixed-height playlist band alongside a persistent playback panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each playlist item row appears as a horizontally oriented strip within the sermon playlist list, echoing the sidebar’s link rows but slightly more spacious. The row stretches the full width of the list panel, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16px horizontal padding. The sermon title is left-aligned and takes up the majority of the row’s width; metadata (date, speaker, duration) sits in a secondary line beneath the title or to the right, depending on available width. Action icons (such as drag handles for reordering and a remove button) are aligned to the far right, spaced 8–12px apart, and vertically centered within the row. A thin, light horizontal divider runs along the bottom edge of each row, matching the sidebar’s separators. When a row is highlighted as newly added, a subtle vertical color accent (e.g., a soft green line) appears along its left edge, about 4px wide, without changing its dimensions. The row height adjusts to content but typically occupies around 48–64px in height for a single-line title and metadata.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static row within a vertically flowing list; it can be draggable for reordering within the bounds of the list panel but does not change its overall position in the page structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove sermon confirmation dialog appears as a centered modal overlay on top of the entire page when the user attempts to remove a sermon from the playlist. The background content (hero, sidebar, playlist list, playback panel) is dimmed with a semi-transparent layer. The dialog itself is a simple rectangular box with a width of roughly 30–40% of the viewport on desktop, constrained to a comfortable reading width (e.g., 320–480px), and vertically centered in the visible portion of the screen. It has 24–32px padding on all sides, with the confirmation question text at the top, action buttons (Confirm/Cancel) aligned to the right at the bottom, and 16–24px vertical spacing between text and buttons. The dialog does not extend edge-to-edge; it floats gently over the dimmed page, in keeping with the clean, utilitarian style, using flat color fills and no heavy shadows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport that disables interaction with the underlying layout until dismissed; scroll position of the page beneath is preserved.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sermon playback panel occupies the right portion of the lower main content area, mirroring the illustrative icon area from the mockup. It begins at the same vertical position as the top of the sermon playlist list, with 16–24px of space below the playlist controls, and extends downward alongside the list. Horizontally, it takes up about 35–40% of the main column width and is aligned to the right edge of the main column. The panel has slightly more internal padding (16–24px) than the playlist list, giving it a contained, card-like feel while still being flat and shadow-free. The sermon title or current playing indicator sits at the top, followed by the audio/video player controls and any supplementary information (e.g., progress, duration). The right edge of the panel aligns flush with the main column edge, while a vertical gap of 24–32px separates it from the sidebar on the far left, preserving the two-column site structure. On narrower screens, the playback panel stacks below the playlist list, still occupying nearly the full main-column width but maintaining similar internal padding and spacing hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static relative to the main column but can be configured to remain visible while the playlist list scrolls (e.g., via internal scrolling in the list area) so that playback controls stay accessible without leaving the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist notification banner appears within the main content column immediately below the playlist header and above the playlist controls. It spans the full width of the main column, aligning with the header’s left and right edges. The banner is relatively shallow in height, with 8–12px vertical padding and 16px horizontal padding, and uses a soft background tint to differentiate it from the page background without being visually loud. It's ideal for messages like “2 new sermons added from your recent browsing.” A small icon on the left can accompany the text, with the message flowing to its right. There is about 8–12px of vertical space above and below the banner to separate it from the header and controls, respectively, maintaining the moderate spacing pattern. When not needed, the banner is completely absent, and the playlist controls move directly under the header, preserving the linear vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the main column that appears and disappears based on context; it scrolls with the rest of the content and does not remain fixed at the top of the viewport.

name: Volunteer Opportunities
title: Volunteer Opportunities
description: Browse and filter active volunteer roles by schedule, time commitment, and category, then open individual role details.

core_features: Load filter and sort options from getVolunteerFilterOptions and render them as interactive controls for day of week, time commitment, category, and sort order.

Read URL parameters (dayOfWeek, timeCommitment, category, sortBy, and paging-related parameters) on page load and when they change, and use them as the single source of truth for the current filter and sort state.
Call searchVolunteerRoles with the current filters, sortBy, page, and pageSize to retrieve a paginated list of active volunteer roles.

Display the list of volunteer roles returned by searchVolunteerRoles, showing key attributes (title, ministryArea, category, daysNeeded, timeWindowStart, timeWindowEnd, hoursPerWeek, location) for each role.
Provide sorting controls (using options from getVolunteerFilterOptions.sortOptions) to adjust the order of the results and update both the URL and the search results.
Provide pagination controls based on searchVolunteerRoles.totalCount and searchVolunteerRoles.hasMore to navigate between result pages and update both the URL state and the results.
Allow users to click a volunteer role title to navigate to volunteer_role.html with the volunteerRoleId in the URL parameters.
Handle loading and error states for both getVolunteerFilterOptions and searchVolunteerRoles, with clear feedback and retry mechanisms.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Initial page load with optional pre-filters
1) User arrives on volunteer.html (from navigation or a deep link) with or without URL parameters.
2) Page controller parses URL parameters for dayOfWeek, timeCommitment, category, sortBy, and any paging-related parameters.
3) Page controller calls getVolunteerFilterOptions.
4) After getVolunteerFilterOptions returns, page controller validates URL parameter values against the available options; any invalid values are ignored.
5) Page controller establishes the current filter and sort state from the validated URL parameters or from implementation-defined defaults based on the returned options.
6) Page controller calls searchVolunteerRoles with the constructed filters, sortBy, page, and pageSize.
7) When searchVolunteerRoles returns, the role list, result summary, and pagination components render based on the response.

Workflow 2: User refines search using filters
1) User adjusts one or more filter controls (day of week, time commitment, category) in the filter component.
2) Filter component informs the page controller of the new filter state via an event handler.
3) Page controller updates the URL parameters to reflect the new filter values, resetting or adjusting any paging-related parameters as needed.
4) Page controller calls searchVolunteerRoles with the updated filters, current sortBy, and appropriate paging parameters.
5) When the new results return, the role list updates to match the filtered set, and the summary text reflects the current filters and result count.

Workflow 3: User changes sort order
1) User selects a sort option from the sort control (options populated from getVolunteerFilterOptions.sortOptions).
2) Sort control notifies the page controller of the new sortBy value.
3) Page controller updates the sortBy parameter in the URL and resets any paging-related parameters as needed.
4) Page controller calls searchVolunteerRoles with the current filters and the new sortBy.
5) Role list re-renders with the sorted results when the call completes.

Workflow 4: User pages through results
1) User uses pagination controls (e.g., next/previous page or direct page selection).
2) Pagination component emits an event with the newly selected page number.
3) Page controller writes the new page number into the URL parameters, preserving filters and sortBy.
4) Page controller calls searchVolunteerRoles with the unchanged filters and sortBy, and the new page value.
5) When the response returns, the role list updates to display the roles for the selected page, and pagination controls reflect the new current page and whether more pages are available based on hasMore and totalCount.

Workflow 5: User opens a role detail
1) In the role list, the user clicks the title of a specific volunteer role.
2) The role list component triggers navigation to volunteer_role.html, appending the volunteerRoleId from the clicked role to the URL as a parameter.
3) The browser loads volunteer_role.html, which can then use the volunteerRoleId parameter to display detailed information for that role.

Workflow 6: Handling API errors and retries
1) If getVolunteerFilterOptions fails, the page controller sets an error state for filter loading.
2) An error component displays a message and a retry control.
3) User activates the retry control, which triggers the page controller to call getVolunteerFilterOptions again without altering URL parameters.
4) If searchVolunteerRoles fails, the page controller sets an error state for search results while preserving the current filter and sort state.
5) An error component within the results area displays a message and retry control.
6) User activates the retry control, which triggers the page controller to re-call searchVolunteerRoles with the current filter, sortBy, and paging state.
interactions: On page initialization, the page controller reads URL parameters, calls getVolunteerFilterOptions, derives current filter/sort state, and then calls searchVolunteerRoles.

When the user changes the day-of-week filter, the filter component triggers an onFilterChange event with the new dayOfWeek value; the page controller updates the URL parameter and re-executes searchVolunteerRoles.

When the user changes the time commitment filter, the filter component triggers onFilterChange with the new timeCommitment value; the page controller updates the URL parameter and re-executes searchVolunteerRoles.

When the user changes the category filter, the filter component triggers onFilterChange with the new category value; the page controller updates the URL parameter and re-executes searchVolunteerRoles.

When the user changes the sort order, the sort control triggers an onSortChange event with the new sortBy value; the page controller updates the sortBy URL parameter, resets paging as appropriate, and re-executes searchVolunteerRoles.

When the user interacts with pagination (e.g., chooses a different page), the pagination component triggers onPageChange with the selected page; the page controller updates the page parameter in the URL and re-executes searchVolunteerRoles.

When the user clicks on a volunteer role title, the list component triggers onRoleClick with the corresponding volunteerRoleId, causing navigation to volunteer_role.html with volunteerRoleId added to the URL parameters.

When API calls fail, error components expose an onRetry event; the page controller responds by re-invoking the appropriate interface (getVolunteerFilterOptions or searchVolunteerRoles) with the current state derived from the URL.
When the browser back/forward buttons are used, the page controller listens to URL changes, re-parses the parameters, and re-runs searchVolunteerRoles so the visible state always reflects the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The URL query string is the authoritative source for the page's filter, sort, and paging state. Supported URL parameters include dayOfWeek, timeCommitment, category, sortBy, and paging-related parameters such as page and pageSize (or equivalent naming chosen by implementation). On initial load and whenever the URL changes (including via browser navigation), the page controller parses these parameters and validates their values against the options returned from getVolunteerFilterOptions. Any parameter whose value does not match an available option (for dayOfWeek, timeCommitment, category, or sortBy) is ignored when constructing the filter state. Valid parameters are used to construct a filters object (containing selected day-of-week, time commitment, and category values) and a sortBy value, which are passed directly to searchVolunteerRoles along with numeric page and pageSize values. If certain URL parameters are absent, the page controller derives defaults based on implementation rules and the data from getVolunteerFilterOptions rather than hardcoded domain values. When the user interacts with filters, sorting, or pagination, the page controller updates the URL query string to reflect the new state (adding, updating, or removing parameters as needed) and then calls searchVolunteerRoles with the updated state. The rendered list, summary text, and pagination controls are always derived from the searchVolunteerRoles response paired with the state implied by the current URL. This approach ensures deep-linking and sharable URLs for any filtered/sorted result set without using localStorage.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, maintains in-memory view state derived from URL parameters, orchestrates calls to getVolunteerFilterOptions and searchVolunteerRoles, and passes data and callbacks to child components.

Displays and manages filter controls for day of week, time commitment, and category using options from getVolunteerFilterOptions and current selections derived from URL parameters; emits filter change events to the page controller.

Renders sorting options sourced from getVolunteerFilterOptions.sortOptions, highlights the currently selected sortBy from URL state, and notifies the page controller when the user selects a different sort option.

Shows a summary of the current result set (such as the total number of roles returned by searchVolunteerRoles) and the active filters derived from URL parameters, and can expose an action to clear all filters.
Displays the array of volunteer roles returned by searchVolunteerRoles, showing key attributes for each role. Handles empty-result states and delegates navigation when a role title is clicked.

Calculates and renders pagination controls using searchVolunteerRoles.totalCount, searchVolunteerRoles.hasMore, and current paging parameters from the URL, and emits page change events when the user navigates between pages.

Shows or hides loading states for filter options and search results based on the page controller’s internal loading flags while getVolunteerFilterOptions or searchVolunteerRoles calls are in progress.
Displays error information when getVolunteerFilterOptions fails and provides a control for retrying the request.
Displays error information when searchVolunteerRoles fails and provides a control for retrying the search with the current filter, sort, and paging state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with approximately 3–4 columns for the left sidebar and 8–9 columns for the main content area within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the original pattern: global header and top navigation across the very top, a full-width hero image band beneath, then a centered content container split into a left sidebar (site or ministry navigation) and a wider right main column. All volunteer-specific components live inside the right main column, within the page-controller. At the top of this column, the page title and brief explanatory intro sit much like the mockup’s heading and green intro paragraph. Directly beneath that intro, a functional control band runs horizontally: filters on the left, sort on the right. Below this, the results summary appears, followed by the vertically stacked list of volunteer roles, and finally pagination at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the original pattern: global header and top navigation across the very top, a full-width hero image band beneath, then a centered content container split into a left sidebar (site or ministry navigation) and a wider right main column. All volunteer-specific components live inside the right main column, within the page-controller. At the top of this column, the page title and brief explanatory intro sit much like the mockup’s heading and green intro paragraph. Directly beneath that intro, a functional control band runs horizontally: filters on the left, sort on the right. Below this, the results summary appears, followed by the vertically stacked list of volunteer roles, and finally pagination at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero image continues to occupy full width and roughly a quarter to a third of the initial viewport height. Below, the centered content container is approximately 1080–1200px wide on large screens. Within this container, the left sidebar takes around 25–30% of the width, and the right volunteer content column about 70–75%. Inside the right column, the control band (filters + sort) spans the full main-column width but is relatively shallow vertically. The roles list consumes most of the vertical space below, with pagination occupying a slim band at the bottom. Vertical spacing between major blocks (intro, control band, summary, list, pagination) follows 24–32px margins, and inner padding for components follows 8–16px based on the 8px scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density mirrors a traditional informational layout: the left sidebar links are relatively tight, but the right column is more open. Within the volunteer column, the heading and intro have generous breathing room; the filter and sort controls are moderately dense but not cramped; the roles list balances readable line lengths with modest vertical spacing between items. There is enough whitespace to separate functional groups (controls, results, pagination) without feeling airy or modern-minimalist—the emphasis is on clarity and utility rather than dramatic whitespace.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first hits the logo and navigation, then moves down to the warm hero image, establishing context. From there, attention moves into the right main column, landing on the large 'Volunteer Opportunities' heading. After reading the short intro, the user’s gaze drops to the filter controls on the left side of the control band, then scans rightward to the sort control. Once filters and sorting are set, the eye naturally moves down to the concise results summary line, then continues vertically through the list of roles. At the end of the list, the pagination draws attention as the next step. Error states and loading states appear in-line in the same vertical flow, occupying the space where the user expects feedback (just below filters or within the results area), so they are discovered naturally when something goes wrong or is in progress.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global header and a full-width hero photograph (perhaps showing congregants serving), a centered content band holds two columns: a narrow left sidebar of site navigation links and a wider right column dedicated to volunteer opportunities. At the top of the right column, the page-controller presents a clear, left-aligned 'Volunteer Opportunities' title with comfortable vertical spacing and a short, slightly emphasized introductory sentence beneath it, echoing the mockup’s highlighted first paragraph. Below this intro, a shallow, full-width control band runs horizontally: on the left, the set of volunteer filter controls; on the right, a compact sort dropdown. Beneath this band, a one-line results summary sits flush left, followed by a vertically stacked list of volunteer roles that fills most of the available vertical space in the main column. Each role appears in a simple, flat style consistent with the utilitarian design. At the bottom, pagination controls are centered with a modest margin above, signaling the end of the content column. When loading, a simple inline indicator occupies the roles area; when filters fail or results cannot load, the error messages appear exactly where the user is focused—under the controls or within the list area—maintaining a straightforward, top-to-bottom reading flow reminiscent of a traditional church information page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller occupies the entire right main content column beneath the hero image, starting at the same vertical level as the first item in the left sidebar. Horizontally, it spans roughly 70–75% of the centered content container, aligned to the right of the sidebar with a consistent gutter between columns (about 24–32px). At its top, there is a large, left-aligned page title 'Volunteer Opportunities' with generous top padding (24–32px from the content band’s top edge) and bottom margin (16–24px before the intro text). Directly under the title, a short intro paragraph sits, slightly more emphasized than body text (e.g., darker or tinted green as per the mockup’s highlighted first paragraph), spanning about two-thirds of the main column width, leaving a soft margin of empty space on the right to keep it airy. This introductory block has a 16–24px margin below it before the filter-and-sort control band begins. All other volunteer components (filters, sort, summary, list, pagination, status, and error messages) are vertically stacked within this controller, honoring a linear, top-to-bottom flow with clear spacing between sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level container in normal document flow; scrolls with the page and defines the vertical stack for all volunteer-related components within the right main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter controls sit directly below the intro paragraph inside the page controller, forming the left side of a horizontal control band. This band spans the full width of the right column. Within it, the filter controls occupy roughly 65–70% of the main column width, starting flush with the left edge of the column. Controls (such as dropdowns for schedule, time commitment, and category, plus any checkbox or tag filters) are arranged either in a single row or in two compact rows, with 8–16px horizontal gaps and 8px vertical gaps, following the utilitarian spacing from the mockup. The vertical padding above and below this filter section is about 12–16px, making it feel like a distinct, but not heavy, control area. There is a clear 16–24px gap below the filters (shared with the sort control row) before the results summary appears.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section in normal flow, anchored to the top of the results area; no sticky behavior. On narrower screens it naturally wraps, with filters stacking vertically while maintaining their position above the results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control shares the same horizontal band as the filter controls, aligned to the right edge of the right main column. While the filters start at the left, the sort dropdown (for example, 'Sort by: Newest, Time Commitment, Day of Week') is right-aligned and occupies roughly 20–25% of the main column width. It sits on the same vertical baseline as the primary row of filters, with 12–16px of vertical padding above and below, matching the filter area’s height so they read visually as a single unified bar. There is a consistent horizontal gap (at least 16–24px) between the rightmost filter element and the left edge of the sort control, preserving clarity. Below this combined control band, the sort control shares the same 16–24px bottom margin before the results summary, maintaining the linear rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline/block element within the control band; right-aligned in normal flow, no floating or sticky behavior. On narrow screens, it wraps to a second row beneath the filters but remains visually grouped with them.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary is positioned directly below the filter-and-sort control band, aligned with the left edge of the right main column. It is a single, concise line (e.g., 'Showing 12 volunteer roles, filtered by Sunday Mornings') that spans up to about 60–70% of the main column width, leaving white space to the right. The vertical spacing above the summary is modest (12–16px from the bottom edge of the control band), and the spacing below is about 16–24px before the roles list begins. This keeps the summary visually close to both the controls (as feedback) and the list (as context), without crowding either.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level text in normal flow; always appears between the control band and the results list, shifting down naturally as filters expand or collapse.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The roles list occupies the largest portion of the right main column, directly beneath the results summary. It spans nearly the full width of the main column (about 90–100% of its width, with internal padding of 16px on the left and right if contained in a panel) and extends downward as far as needed to display the current page of results. Each role is presented in a flat, card-like row or block with subtle separation—either a thin horizontal rule between items or 12–16px vertical spacing. The first role appears roughly 16–24px below the results summary. The list maintains consistent left alignment with the column content; any small illustrative icons for roles are placed toward the right side of each role row, mirroring the mockup’s pattern of an icon to the right of the introductory text. The vertical rhythm is steady and moderate, supporting scanning down the list without visual noise.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level container in normal flow; expands vertically with content. When loading or when a results error occurs, the loading indicator or results error message occupies this same region, replacing or overlaying list items in the vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination control is positioned directly beneath the roles list, acting as the visual footer of the main content column. It sits about 24–32px below the last role item, creating a clear separation that signals the end of the current results page. Horizontally, the pagination is centered within the right main column and spans roughly 40–60% of the column width, with individual page links or buttons arranged in a single row with 8–12px gaps. A small amount of padding (8–12px) above and below the pagination elements keeps them touch-friendly without feeling oversized. Beneath the pagination, there is a final 24–32px margin before the bottom of the content band or any site-wide footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section in normal flow; anchored at the bottom of the results area and moves downward as more items are added above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator appears within the same vertical space reserved for the roles list, centered both horizontally and with comfortable vertical spacing. When active, it sits approximately 24px below the results summary, occupying the full width of the main column but with the visual focus in the center (such as a spinner and 'Loading volunteer opportunities…' text). There is ample whitespace around it—at least 24px padding above and below—to distinguish it from other interface elements while still feeling part of the same content band. It replaces the roles list in the visual flow while data is loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element that conditionally appears in place of the roles list; it does not overlay other components but instead occupies their space in normal flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter error message is positioned directly beneath the filter-and-sort control band, but above the results summary, to associate it clearly with user input in the filters. It spans nearly the full width of the right main column, aligned to the left, with an inset text block and possibly a subtle colored border or background for emphasis. There is a tight vertical gap (8–12px) between the bottom of the control band and the top of the error message, and another 12–16px gap below the error before the results summary, ensuring the message is noticed without overly disrupting the layout. The block has 8–12px internal padding on all sides for readability while keeping with the compact, utilitarian feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element that appears conditionally; it pushes the results summary and everything below it downward in the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results error message occupies the space where the roles list would normally appear, directly beneath the results summary. It spans the full width of the main column, with the text centered or left-aligned depending on tone, and includes 16–24px of padding and 24px of vertical whitespace above and below to make it prominent and readable. There are no role items shown when this message is present, so it effectively replaces the list in the vertical flow. Any accompanying icon (e.g., a subtle warning symbol) is placed to the left of the text, keeping consistent with the flat, minimal style and maintaining a dignified, church-appropriate tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element that conditionally replaces the roles list; appears in the same vertical slot in normal flow and pushes pagination further down or removes it when there are no results.

name: Volunteer Role Detail
title: Volunteer Role Detail
description: Displays details for a specific volunteer role and allows visitors to submit an interest form for that role.

core_features: Read the volunteerRoleId from the URL query string and load the corresponding volunteer role details via getVolunteerRoleDetails.
Display full role information including description, schedule (daysNeeded and typical time window), estimated hoursPerWeek, requiredSkills, location, and ministry contact details when available.
Show a functional "I'm Interested" or "Sign Up" call-to-action that moves the user focus to the interest form section when clicked.
Render an interest form bound to the selected volunteer role, collecting name, email, preferredDay, preferredTimeWindowStart, preferredTimeWindowEnd, and optional notes.
Submit the interest form using submitVolunteerInterest and handle the API response to show success or error feedback on the page.
Conditionally enable or disable interest actions and the form when the loaded role is inactive (isActive is false) or when the role fails to load.
Optionally preserve and restore submission status using URL parameters so that feedback can remain visible after a page reload or when the page is re-opened.
user_workflows: View a volunteer role's details: 1) User arrives at volunteer_role.html with a volunteerRoleId in the query string (e.g., volunteer_role.html?volunteerRoleId={id}). 2) On page load, the page-init logic extracts volunteerRoleId and calls getVolunteerRoleDetails(volunteerRoleId). 3) While loading, the page shows a loading state instead of role content. 4) On successful response, the role header and details sections render using the returned role data. 5) If the role is active, the "I'm Interested" button and interest form are enabled. If the role is inactive or not found, an appropriate inactive/not-found state is shown and the interest actions are disabled.

Navigate back to the list of roles: 1) From the role detail header, the user clicks the "Back to Volunteer Opportunities" link. 2) The browser navigates to volunteer.html (no additional parameters are required by this page). 3) The current volunteerRoleId and any interestStatus URL parameters are not needed once the user leaves this page.

Submit an interest form for an active role: 1) After reviewing the role details, the user clicks the "I'm Interested" or similar call-to-action. 2) The page scrolls or focuses to the interest form section. 3) The user fills in name, email, preferredDay, preferredTimeWindowStart, preferredTimeWindowEnd, and optional notes. PreferredDay options are constrained to the role's daysNeeded if provided; otherwise, all allowed days from the VolunteerInterest preferredDay enum are offered. 4) On form submit, the page validates required fields (at least name, email, preferredDay) and basic email format. 5) If validation fails, the form displays inline validation messages and prevents submission. 6) If validation passes, the page calls submitVolunteerInterest with volunteerRoleId (from URL), name, email, preferredDay, preferredTimeWindowStart, preferredTimeWindowEnd, and notes. 7) While waiting for the API response, the form shows a submitting state and disables further input to prevent duplicate submissions. 8) On a successful API response (success = true), the page shows a success banner using the returned message/status, may optionally clear or lock the form, and can update the URL to include interestStatus=success and interestId={volunteerInterestId}. 9) On an error response (success = false) or network error, the page shows an error banner and re-enables the form.

Return to a previously submitted interest state: 1) A user who has just submitted the form or returns via a link that includes interestStatus and interestId in the query string (e.g., volunteer_role.html?volunteerRoleId={id}&interestStatus=success&interestId={interestId}) loads the page. 2) The page-init logic still calls getVolunteerRoleDetails using volunteerRoleId. 3) After role data loads, the URL-state logic reads interestStatus and interestId. 4) If interestStatus indicates a prior successful submission, the feedback component automatically shows a success state without requiring a new form submission, and the form may be pre-set to a submitted/locked state.
interactions: On page load, the system reads volunteerRoleId from the URL query string and triggers getVolunteerRoleDetails; the role details and form remain disabled until this call succeeds.
If volunteerRoleId is missing or getVolunteerRoleDetails returns no role or an error, the page shows a not-found or error message and hides/disables the interest form and call-to-action.
Clicking the "Back to Volunteer Opportunities" link triggers navigation to volunteer.html using a normal link or client-side router navigation.

Clicking the "I'm Interested" (or similar) button scrolls or jumps the viewport to the interest form component and sets focus to the first input field if the role is active; if the role is inactive, the button is disabled or not rendered.
Changing the preferredDay select field updates the internal form state for preferredDay and may influence suggested time windows, but does not call any external interfaces.
Changing preferredTimeWindowStart and preferredTimeWindowEnd updates internal form state; these values are sent as-is to submitVolunteerInterest on form submission.
On interest form submit, the form component gathers current field values, runs front-end validation, and if valid, calls submitVolunteerInterest with the current volunteerRoleId and form fields.

When submitVolunteerInterest resolves successfully, the feedback component receives the success flag, volunteerInterestId, status, and message, and displays an appropriate success message; the form component may reset or lock inputs and the URL-state manager may add or update interestStatus and interestId query parameters.
When submitVolunteerInterest returns an error or fails, the feedback component displays an error message and the form component re-enables submission controls.

When the user dismisses a success or error banner (if dismissal is supported), the feedback component hides the banner and the URL-state manager may remove any interestStatus-related parameters from the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the volunteerRoleId URL query parameter. On every load or URL change, the page extracts volunteerRoleId from the query string. If volunteerRoleId is present, the page calls getVolunteerRoleDetails(volunteerRoleId) and sets loading state until the call completes. If volunteerRoleId is missing or the API returns no role, the page moves into an error/not-found state in which the detail sections and interest form are not rendered or are disabled. The isActive field from the returned role further refines state: if isActive is false, the page shows the role details but disables or hides the interest call-to-action and form. Additional optional URL parameters can reflect submission state. For example, interestStatus can indicate the result of a recent interest submission (such as 'success' or 'error'), and interestId can hold the volunteerInterestId returned by submitVolunteerInterest. On load, the URL-state manager reads these parameters; if interestStatus is present and indicates a prior success, the feedback component shows a success message and the form may be presented as already submitted. The page does not use localStorage or other client-side persistence; all restorable state is derived from current URL parameters (volunteerRoleId, and optionally interestStatus and interestId) and the latest API responses from getVolunteerRoleDetails and submitVolunteerInterest.

components.functionality: Initializes the Volunteer Role Detail page by reading volunteerRoleId from the URL query string, invoking getVolunteerRoleDetails with that ID, and managing loading, success, and error states. It exposes the loaded role data and high-level loading/error flags to other components. If volunteerRoleId is missing or the API returns no role or an error, it sets a not-found/error state that downstream components use to hide or disable role-specific UI.

Displays the functional page heading for the selected volunteer role and provides navigation and primary actions. It renders the role title from the loaded VolunteerRole, may show category and ministryArea labels when available, and indicates when a role is inactive (based on isActive). It includes a "Back to Volunteer Opportunities" link that navigates to volunteer.html and an "I'm Interested" call-to-action button that, when the role is active, scrolls the view to the interest form and focuses the first field. If the loading state is active, it shows a loading placeholder; if the role is not found, it shows a generic not-found or error message and hides the interest button.

Renders the full details of the volunteer role once loaded. It uses VolunteerRole fields to show the description, daysNeeded, timeWindowStart, timeWindowEnd, hoursPerWeek, requiredSkills, location, and contactName/contactEmail if provided. The component composes a human-readable schedule from daysNeeded and the time window fields, and clearly indicates the typical time commitment from hoursPerWeek. Sections are conditionally displayed only when the corresponding data is available (for example, the required skills area is hidden if requiredSkills is empty). If the role is inactive, the section may include an informational inactive-state message while still showing descriptive details.

Collects and submits the user's interest in the current volunteer role. It displays input fields for name and email, a select control for preferredDay, optional inputs for preferredTimeWindowStart and preferredTimeWindowEnd, and a multiline input for notes. The preferredDay options are derived from the VolunteerInterest preferredDay enum; if the current VolunteerRole provides daysNeeded, the list is filtered to those days, otherwise all allowed days from the enum are available. The component validates required fields (name, email, preferredDay) and basic email format on submit. When validation passes and the role is active, it calls submitVolunteerInterest with volunteerRoleId (from the URL or page-init state) and the current form values. While the request is in progress, the form enters a submitting state (e.g., disabling inputs and the submit button) to prevent duplicate submissions. It emits success or error events based on the API response, which other components (such as feedback and URL-state manager) consume.

Displays inline feedback related to the interest submission process. It listens for submitVolunteerInterest responses from the volunteer-interest-form component and for any submission-related state derived from URL parameters. When a submission is successful (success = true), it shows a success state using the returned message and may display the status and volunteerInterestId for reference. When a submission fails or the API returns success = false, it shows an error state with an appropriate message. The banner remains visible until the user dismisses it or until the component determines it should be replaced (for example, by a subsequent submission).

Manages synchronization between the page's UI state and URL query parameters. On page load, it reads volunteerRoleId to ensure it is present and passes it to the page-init component. It also reads optional interestStatus and interestId parameters to determine if a previous submission state should be restored and forwards this information to the feedback and form components. After a successful call to submitVolunteerInterest, it can update the browser URL (using query parameters) to include or modify interestStatus and interestId without reloading the page, enabling the success state to persist across reloads or shared links that point back to this page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with approximately 3–4 columns for the left sidebar and 8–9 columns for the main content area within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: All visible components on this page live inside the main (right) column of a two-column layout that sits beneath the global header and full-width hero image. The left column is a navigation sidebar (implemented elsewhere), while the right column contains the volunteer-role-header at the top, followed by the volunteer-role-details-section, then the interest-submission-feedback (when shown), and finally the volunteer-interest-form. Non-visual components (volunteer-role-page-init and url-state-manager) are mounted at the page level and do not occupy visual space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All visible components on this page live inside the main (right) column of a two-column layout that sits beneath the global header and full-width hero image. The left column is a navigation sidebar (implemented elsewhere), while the right column contains the volunteer-role-header at the top, followed by the volunteer-role-details-section, then the interest-submission-feedback (when shown), and finally the volunteer-interest-form. Non-visual components (volunteer-role-page-init and url-state-manager) are mounted at the page level and do not occupy visual space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page uses a centered max-width content container (e.g., 1080–1200px) below the hero. Inside this container, the left sidebar takes roughly 25–30% of the width and the right main content column takes roughly 70–75%. Within the main column, the role header and details section occupy the full column width. The interest form also spans the main column but may visually read as slightly narrower on large screens due to internal padding. Vertical spacing between major blocks (header → details, details → feedback, feedback → form) follows the 24–32px rhythm, while internal spacing within each section uses 8–16px increments.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate. The sidebar (not part of these components) remains relatively dense with closely stacked links. In contrast, the main column where these components live has more breathing room: generous spacing around the role title, a comfortably spaced details section with clear intro text, and a well-organized but not overly airy form. Line spacing and padding inside sections use 8–16px increments, while there are noticeable but not excessive gaps (24–32px) between major sections to keep the page feeling informative and traditional rather than minimalistic or ultra-modern.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows the mockup: users enter through the global header and full-width hero, then are drawn to the top of the main content column. Their eye hits a small utility element (e.g., a subtle tag or icon area aligned with the role header), then the large role title. From there, attention moves down to the highlighted first paragraph of the details section, then naturally scans across to the illustrative icon on the right of that intro text. After digesting the role information, the eye continues downward to the status banner (if present) and then to the interest form. The sidebar on the left remains a secondary vertical anchor, discovered via peripheral vision, for navigating to other roles or related pages.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global navigation and a warm, full-width hero photograph that takes up roughly the top quarter of the viewport, the content is framed in a centered container divided into two columns. On the left, a tight vertical list of navigation links (with one highlighted) runs down the page; on the right, the main volunteer role content forms a taller, more open column. At the top of this right column, the volunteer role header stretches the full width, with a small, subtle utility element (such as a label or icon) aligned on the same baseline as a large, prominent role title. Directly beneath, separated by a comfortable gap, the role details section presents an introductory paragraph in green text, occupying most of the left side of the text area, while a small illustrative icon is visually aligned to the right, echoing the mockup’s right-anchored illustration. Additional role details stack below in a straightforward, text-first pattern. Further down, a slim status banner appears (when relevant), spanning the width of the main column and gently drawing attention without overpowering the page. Immediately under this banner, the interest form occupies the remainder of the main column, with clearly stacked fields and buttons, maintaining the utilitarian, church-appropriate feel. Overall, the page feels linear and content-forward: visual attention flows from hero to title, to intro text and icon, then down to the form, while the sidebar quietly supports navigation on the left.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is mounted at the root of the volunteer role detail page, conceptually just inside the main content area below the hero image but before any visible content in the DOM. It does not render any visible UI and therefore occupies no visual width or height in the layout. Its presence should not affect spacing or the two-column structure; the first visible element in the right column remains the volunteer-role-header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; exists purely as an invisible logic layer and does not participate in the visual layout or affect flow, margins, or alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the main (right) content column beneath the hero, the volunteer-role-header is positioned at the very top, aligned to the left edge of the main column with equal left padding to match the text alignment of other sections (e.g., 16–24px inside the content container). It spans the full width of the main column, roughly 70–75% of the overall content container width. Vertically, there is a 24–32px gap between the bottom of the hero image and the top of this header, making the page title the first clearly defined content element. The header itself follows the mockup’s pattern: a small inline utility element (such as a subtle badge, back link, or icon) appears above or to the left of the title baseline, taking no more than 15–20% of the row width, while the large role title text dominates the line, extending across the remaining width. There is 8–16px vertical padding above and below the title, with slightly larger bottom spacing to visually separate it from the details section below. On smaller screens, the header components stack vertically in the same order but still remain the top-most area of the main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element flowing in the document order at the top of the main column; it scrolls with the page and does not use sticky or fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The volunteer-role-details-section sits directly beneath the volunteer-role-header in the main column, separated by a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px to clearly distinguish it as the body content area. It spans the full width of the main column, matching the header’s left alignment and padding. The first paragraph of this section is styled as a highlighted intro in green text and positioned at the top-left of the section, occupying roughly 60–70% of the section width. Horizontally aligned to the right of this intro paragraph is a small illustrative icon or emblem (e.g., 64–96px wide), positioned so that its vertical center aligns approximately with the middle of the intro text block, echoing the mockup’s icon-on-the-right treatment. The space between the intro paragraph and the icon is modest (16–24px), ensuring they read as one connected horizontal band. Below this intro row, subsequent paragraphs, bullet lists, or role attributes stack in a linear vertical flow, each separated by 12–16px of vertical spacing to maintain a readable but efficient density. Overall vertical padding inside this section is about 16–24px from top content to bottom content, with no special background treatment so it stays content-first and minimal.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section in normal flow; expands vertically based on content. On narrower screens, the right-aligned icon naturally wraps below the intro paragraph, maintaining a small vertical gap (8–16px) while preserving the section’s hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The volunteer-interest-form is placed beneath the role details section within the main column. There is a clear vertical separation of approximately 32px between the bottom of the details content and the top of the form to signal a shift from information to action. When the interest-submission-feedback banner is visible, the form appears directly below that banner with a smaller gap (16–24px) to keep them visually connected. The form spans the full width of the main column but uses generous internal padding (16–24px on all sides) so that fields appear in a comfortable central band. Input fields are stacked vertically, aligned to the left, with 12–16px vertical spacing between them to maintain a moderate density consistent with the informational style. The submit button is aligned left under the final field, with 16–24px space above it, and may occupy 25–40% of the column width, making it noticeable but not oversized.
