There is 24–32px padding below the last form element before the end of this section, providing a clean visual endpoint to the page’s main content. On smaller screens, the form continues to occupy the full width with its padding adjusted as needed (e.g., 16px) to avoid feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element in the natural document flow. It expands vertically with its fields and scrolls with the page; there is no sticky or fixed behavior. Its vertical position shifts downward when the status banner is present, preserving consistent gaps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The interest-submission-feedback component appears as a slim status banner located in the main column directly above the volunteer-interest-form. It spans the full available width of the main column, aligning flush with the same left and right padding as the form and text content above. When visible, it occupies a low-profile horizontal band, approximately the height of 1–2 lines of text plus 8–12px of vertical padding above and below (total height roughly 32–48px), making it noticeable but not dominant. There is a modest vertical gap of 16–24px below this banner before the top of the form so that the banner feels connected to the form’s state, while a slightly smaller or equal gap (8–16px) above separates it from the bottom of the details section. The background color is a subtle tint (e.g., light green for success, gentle red or amber for errors) with simple, flat styling consistent with the overall utilitarian and traditional theme. The message text is left-aligned and kept to a single concise line when possible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element that only occupies space when rendered. It pushes the form downward when visible and disappears cleanly without leaving extra whitespace when not in use. It scrolls with the main content and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager is conceptually positioned at the page logic layer, associated with the volunteer role detail view as a whole but not visible in the UI. It synchronizes URL parameters and page state without rendering any interface elements, so it occupies no physical space in the layout. It should not alter the spacing or positioning of the hero, columns, or any of the visible components in the main content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; purely functional. It does not participate in the visual layout or document flow and has no impact on spacing, alignment, or scroll behavior.

name: Online Giving & Pledges
title: Online Giving & Pledges
description: Page for creating online giving pledges or immediate giving commitments to available church funds, using dynamic options from the server and optional URL parameters.

core_features: Load available funds, giving types, and frequency options via getGivingOptions on page initialization.
Interpret incoming URL parameters (fundId, givingType, amount) to preselect the fund, giving type, and amount in the form.
Allow users to select a giving type (e.g., pledge-only vs. immediate) from dynamically loaded givingTypes.
Allow users to select a fund/designation from dynamically loaded funds that are active and visible online.
Allow users to enter or adjust a giving amount, with optional prefill from the amount URL parameter.
Allow users to select a pledge frequency from dynamically loaded frequencyOptions (including one-time).
Collect donor information (name, email) and optional details such as start date and notes.
Display a live summary of the current giving/pledge configuration before submission.
Submit a pledge or giving commitment to the selected fund via submitPledge and show a confirmation or error message based on the response.
Provide navigation from the giving page to ministries.html for users who want to learn more about ministries before giving.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Start a ministry-specific pledge from ministries.html
1) User clicks a giving link on ministries.html that navigates to give.html with a fundId parameter (and optionally amount and givingType).
2) On load, the page calls getGivingOptions and waits for the response.
3) After getGivingOptions resolves, the page checks the fundId URL parameter and, if it matches an active, visible fund from the response, preselects that fund in the fund selector.
4) The page also applies the givingType and amount URL parameters (if present and valid) to the giving type and amount fields.
5) User reviews or changes giving type, fund, amount, frequency, and enters their name and email.
6) User submits the form, which calls submitPledge with the current form values.
7) On a successful response, the page displays the confirmation using the returned message and summary; on failure, the page shows an error and keeps the form data so the user can correct and resubmit.

Workflow 2: Make a general pledge from site navigation
1) User navigates to give.html from a header or footer link without URL parameters.
2) Page calls getGivingOptions and, after loading, selects the defaultGivingType and defaultFrequency and, if any fund has isDefault, preselects that fund; otherwise no fund is selected until the user chooses one.
3) User selects a fund, then enters amount, selects frequency, and fills donor details.
4) User reviews the summary panel and submits the form.
5) submitPledge is called with the current field values; upon success, the user sees confirmation and a reference to the pledgeId and status in the response.
6) Optionally, the page may update the URL (e.g., with fundId, givingType, amount) to allow the user to bookmark or share their configured pledge setup.

Workflow 3: Adjust pledge settings after arriving on the page
1) User arrives on give.html with some prefilled values from URL parameters or defaults.
2) User changes the giving type; the page updates the selected givingType state and, if needed, adjusts related UI (e.g., explanatory text or start date behavior) without reloading the page.
3) User switches to a different fund; the summary panel immediately reflects the new fund name by matching the selected fundId against the funds returned from getGivingOptions.
4) User modifies the amount and frequency; the summary updates in real time.
5) User enters or edits donor name, email, optional start date, and notes.
6) When user submits, the current state is sent to submitPledge; on error, relevant validation messages appear, and invalid fields are highlighted, while preserving the current selections.
interactions: On page load, the page triggers getGivingOptions; while loading, the giving form is disabled or shows a loading state until options are available.

After getGivingOptions resolves, the page reads fundId, givingType, and amount from the current URL query string and applies them if they match valid options and formats; invalid or missing values are ignored in favor of API defaults.

Changing the giving type (e.g., via radio buttons or a select input) updates internal state and the pledge summary; the page may also update the givingType parameter in the URL using history APIs without a full reload.
Changing the selected fund (via a fund dropdown) updates the selected fundId state and the summary display; the page may update the fundId URL parameter accordingly.
Editing the amount input updates the amount state and the summary in real time; the amount URL parameter may be updated as the user confirms or leaves the field.
Changing the frequency selection updates the frequency state and the summary; available options are taken exclusively from the frequencyOptions returned by getGivingOptions.

Typing into donor name and donor email fields updates the respective state used in submitPledge; client-side validation ensures both are present and that the email format is valid before allowing submission.
Editing optional fields such as startDate and notes updates the local form state; startDate is used directly as the startDate parameter for submitPledge if filled.

Clicking the primary submit button triggers form validation; if validation passes, the page calls submitPledge with fundId, amount, givingType, frequency, donorName, donorEmail, startDate (if any), and notes (if any).
While submitPledge is in progress, the page shows a submitting state (e.g., disabled button) and prevents duplicate submissions until a response is received.

On successful submitPledge response where success is true, the page displays a confirmation section using message, summary.fundName, summary.amount, summary.givingType, and summary.frequency, and may reveal the pledgeId and status.
If submitPledge returns success as false or an error, the page shows an error alert with the returned message and keeps the form state unchanged so the user can modify and retry.
Clicking the "Learn about our ministries" link navigates the user to ministries.html without altering Pledge state on this page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is derived from a combination of server responses (getGivingOptions), URL query parameters, and user input. On initialization, getGivingOptions is always called first. After it resolves, the page parses the current URL for fundId, givingType, and amount parameters. If fundId matches a fund from getGivingOptions.funds that is active and visible online, that fund becomes the selected fund; otherwise the page uses any fund with isDefault == true, or leaves the fund unselected if no default is indicated. If the givingType parameter matches one of the givingTypes.value entries, it overrides defaultGivingType; otherwise defaultGivingType is used. If the amount parameter parses to a valid number greater than zero, it pre-populates the amount input; otherwise the amount field remains blank until the user fills it. defaultFrequency from getGivingOptions initializes the frequency selection unless the user chooses a different option. As the user changes fund, givingType, or amount, the page may update the URL query string via history.replaceState or similar, ensuring the current configuration can be reloaded or shared without relying on localStorage or other client-side persistence. No state is stored in localStorage or cookies; all persisted state between visits must be encoded in the URL parameters or retrieved via the assigned interfaces. After a successful submitPledge call, the page enters a 'confirmation' sub-state where the confirmation component is shown; however, if the user reloads the page, this confirmation is not automatically restored unless the application chooses to encode relevant data in the URL or the user submits again.

components.functionality: Calls getGivingOptions when the page loads, stores the returned funds, givingTypes, frequencyOptions, defaultGivingType, and defaultFrequency in page state, and signals when the form can be rendered with real data.

After getGivingOptions has loaded, reads fundId, givingType, and amount from the URL query string, validates them against the loaded options, and sets the initial selected fund, giving type, and amount fields accordingly.
Displays the givingTypes from getGivingOptions as selectable options (e.g., radio buttons or a dropdown) and lets the user choose the givingType that will be sent with submitPledge.

Displays a dropdown or similar control listing all active and visible funds from getGivingOptions.funds, preselects the fund using URL state or API defaults, and updates the selected fundId used for submitPledge and the summary.
Provides an amount input field for the pledge or immediate giving amount, optionally prefilled from the amount URL parameter, validates numeric input, and updates the Pledge amount state.
Displays frequencyOptions from getGivingOptions and allows the user to select a pledge frequency (including one-time), initializing from defaultFrequency and updating Pledge frequency state.
Collects donorName, donorEmail, optional startDate, and optional notes, validates required fields and email format, and exposes these values for submitPledge.

Shows a read-only summary of the current configuration (selected fund name, amount, givingType, frequency, and donor info) derived from the current Pledge state and funds loaded by getGivingOptions, updating in real time as the user changes inputs.

Wraps the other input components into a single form; on submit, validates all required fields and then calls submitPledge with the selected fundId, amount, givingType, frequency, donorName, donorEmail, startDate, and notes, then routes the response to the confirmation or error components.

Displays confirmation details after a successful submitPledge call using the returned success flag, pledgeId, status, message, and summary (fundName, amount, givingType, frequency); remains hidden until success is true.

Displays an error message when submitPledge returns success as false or an error occurs, using the response message and optionally highlighting problematic fields, without clearing existing form data.
Provides a clickable link labeled appropriately (e.g., 'Learn about our ministries') that navigates the user to ministries.html when selected.

Keeps key form selections (such as fundId, givingType, and amount) synchronized with the URL query string so that the current configuration can be reloaded or shared, updating the URL when these fields change without reloading the page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with approximately 3–4 columns for the left sidebar and 8–9 columns for the main content area within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: Follow the mockup's linear structure: global header and navigation bar at the very top, then a full-width photographic hero band. Below that, a centered content container is split into two columns: a narrow left sidebar (site/ministries navigation) and a wider right column for the "Online Giving & Pledges" page. Within the main column, content flows vertically in logical steps: a small utility row (e.g., RSS-style badge), the page title, a short green introductory paragraph with a small illustrative icon to the right, then the giving form sections arranged top-to-bottom. The data/state components are invisible but conceptually attached to the main form area near the top.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Follow the mockup's linear structure: global header and navigation bar at the very top, then a full-width photographic hero band. Below that, a centered content container is split into two columns: a narrow left sidebar (site/ministries navigation) and a wider right column for the "Online Giving & Pledges" page. Within the main column, content flows vertically in logical steps: a small utility row (e.g., RSS-style badge), the page title, a short green introductory paragraph with a small illustrative icon to the right, then the giving form sections arranged top-to-bottom. The data/state components are invisible but conceptually attached to the main form area near the top.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies the full viewport width and roughly the top 25–30% of height on a typical desktop. The content container below is constrained to a centered max-width (e.g., 960–1140px). Horizontally, the left sidebar uses about 25–30% of this container, and the main column uses 70–75%. Within the main column, form fields generally span close to full column width, while the pledge summary panel occupies a narrower right segment (about 30–35% of the main column) aligned alongside the middle and lower parts of the form on larger screens. Vertical spacing between major sections (title vs. intro vs. each form block vs. summary panel) uses roughly 24–32px; inner paddings and between related fields use 8–16px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Match the mockup's moderate density: the left sidebar is relatively tight with closely stacked navigation, while the main column is more open and step-like. The form fields are grouped into clear blocks with modest breathing room between them, preventing clutter while still conveying seriousness and efficiency. Text lines and labels follow a standard line height with 8–12px spacing, and each form group has distinct separation (24–32px) so the process feels understandable and not overwhelming.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye travels top-down: first the familiar global nav and logo, then the warm, full-width hero image. From there, attention is drawn to the large page title in the upper area of the right column. After the heading, the user naturally reads the green introductory paragraph, then notices the small illustrative icon on its right. The flow continues downward through the form: giving type, fund, amount, frequency, donor info, and finally the submit action. In parallel, the pledge summary panel on the right side of the main column catches peripheral attention and serves as a running recap. The left sidebar navigation sits to the left as a stable, secondary navigation path that users discover while scanning horizontally after the hero and heading.

layout.overall_description: Below the church's global navigation and logo, a full-width photographic hero image spans edge to edge across the screen, occupying roughly the upper quarter of the viewport and setting a warm, reverent tone. Under the hero, the main content is constrained to a centered, fixed-width container that is visually separated from the hero by about 24–32px of vertical space. Within this container, the layout is split into two columns: on the left, a narrow sidebar about one quarter of the width lists ministries and related pages as a vertical menu with thin separators between items. On the right, taking up the remaining three quarters, the "Online Giving & Pledges" content forms the main focus. At the top of this right column, a small RSS-style badge or utility icon sits near the upper-right corner, while the page title "Online Giving & Pledges" appears prominently towards the upper-left, using a larger, bold type with extra margin above and below. Directly beneath the title, a short introductory paragraph in green text stretches across most of the column, explaining the purpose of online giving; to its right, near the midline of this text, a modest illustrative icon reinforces the topic. Below this introduction, the giving workflow is presented as a vertical sequence of clearly separated form sections: first choosing the giving type, then selecting a fund, entering pledge amounts, picking a frequency, providing donor information, and finally confirming and submitting. Alongside the middle and lower portions of this form, a simple, bordered pledge summary panel hugs the right side of the main column, occupying about one third of its width; it begins just below the early form fields and continues downward, summarizing the pledge in real-time. Feedback messages for successful submission or errors appear inline in the main column near the top of the form area, clearly visible without overwhelming the layout. Invisible data-loader and URL state manager components sit conceptually behind the main form, initializing available funds and pre-filling values from the URL before the user interacts. Overall, the page feels traditional, content-first, and quietly structured—reflecting the original mockup's utilitarian, church-appropriate design, with a clear vertical reading path and a dependable left sidebar for navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual data-loader component is logically positioned at the very top of the main right column, just below the hero image and within the same vertical stack as the page title and introductory content. It occupies no visible space on the page but runs before or as the main form renders, effectively sitting 'behind' the giving-type and fund selectors so that by the time those controls appear, they are populated with server-driven options. Conceptually, it aligns with the left edge of the main content column, spanning its full width, but does not alter spacing or push other elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-interactive; executes during page load without taking up layout space or affecting surrounding components’ positions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state initializer is an invisible layer conceptually anchored to the upper portion of the main right column, in the same region as the heading and first form section. It does not have a visible box or occupy height, but it initializes the initial values of the giving-type selector, fund selector, and possibly pledge amount based on URL parameters before these controls are rendered. From a layout perspective, treat it as sitting between the page intro text and the first interactive form group, while not affecting the visual spacing between those visible elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible logic-only component that runs on load and does not change the physical layout; it only influences the default states of visible form fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The giving-type selector is the first visible form block in the main right column, placed directly beneath the green introductory paragraph with about 24px of vertical spacing. It aligns with the left margin of the main content area and spans roughly 65–70% of the main column width on larger screens (leaving space to its right for the later-appearing pledge summary panel). This block includes a label and a dropdown or set of radio buttons arranged vertically, with 8–16px padding inside the block and 24–32px of vertical space below it before the next form section. It sits well above the midpoint of the page, signifying the starting point of the giving process, and maintains a utilitarian, no-frills styling consistent with the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally in the document as a standard block-level form section; it reflows to full-width within the main column on smaller screens, stacking above subsequent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fund selector appears immediately below the giving-type selector, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space to clearly denote it as the next step. It is left-aligned with the main column and uses similar width to the giving-type selector (about 65–70% of the main column on desktop). Inside this block, a simple label sits above a dropdown, list, or radio options with modest vertical spacing (8–12px) between label and control. The density here mirrors the mockup's content-centric look—controls are close enough to feel efficient but not cramped. Vertically, this section occupies a bit more height than the giving-type selector if multiple fund options are visible, but it remains clearly subordinate to the page title and intro above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level form group that sits in the linear vertical flow; it does not float or overlay and collapses to full width inside the main column on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pledge-amount input field group sits below the fund selector with a similar 24–32px gap, aligning flush with the left edge of the main column and matching the width of the previous form sections (about 65–70% of the main column on desktop). This block includes a clear label (e.g., "Pledge Amount" or "Gift Amount") above one or more input fields, possibly with a small hint text or currency indicator arranged to the right or just inside the field. The spacing inside this group follows the same 8–16px padding pattern, and there is a slightly stronger visual emphasis here—perhaps via bold label or careful spacing—since this is a key action element. Vertically, it sits around the middle of the main form stack and lines up horizontally with the upper portion of the pledge summary panel to its right, creating a subtle left-right relationship between entering numbers and seeing the summarized pledge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Regular block-level form group that participates in the vertical flow. On wider screens, it coexists with the pledge summary panel to its right; on narrow screens, it becomes full-width with the summary panel stacking below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The frequency selector is positioned directly beneath the pledge-amount input group with about 24px of vertical separation. It shares the same left alignment and width (approximately 65–70% of the main column) as the earlier form groups. The label sits at the top of this block, and the frequency options (e.g., one-time, weekly, monthly) appear either as a dropdown or a horizontal/vertical set of radio buttons. If displayed horizontally, the options are spaced with 16px gaps to avoid clutter, reflecting the mockup's moderate density. Vertically, this block sits just above the donor info form, and to its right, at roughly the same vertical band, the middle section of the pledge summary panel is visible, reinforcing how frequency affects the overall pledge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a standard block-level element; no fixed or sticky behavior. On smaller screens it simply stacks along with other form fields, retaining clear top-to-bottom ordering.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donor-info form is a taller, multi-field block appearing below the frequency selector, separated by approximately 32px to signal a shift from pledge configuration to personal details. It maintains the same left alignment and default width as the other form sections but may extend slightly taller vertically due to multiple inputs (name, email, address, etc.). Fields inside this block are arranged in a clean vertical stack with 8–12px spacing between individual inputs, following the utilitarian style. On larger screens, the donor-info form’s left edge lines up with the previous controls, while its right side aligns visually with the left edge of the pledge summary panel, creating a tidy columnar relationship. The bottom of this block ends just above the pledge-submit form, with 24–32px of vertical space between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level form section that participates in the vertical flow. On small screens, all fields stack full-width within the main column, preserving readability and clear grouping.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pledge summary panel is a visually distinct, yet understated panel anchored to the right side of the main column, beginning roughly at the vertical level of the pledge-amount input or slightly above it. On larger screens, it occupies about 30–35% of the main content column’s width and is separated from the left-side form fields by a modest horizontal gap (around 24px). The panel extends vertically downwards alongside the pledge-amount, frequency, donor info, and submit sections, with internal padding of 16–24px and a simple border or subtle background tone to differentiate it from the page background without using heavy shadows. Its top aligns a bit below the introductory text but above the midpoint of the page, so users see it as soon as they begin filling out key pledge details. On narrow screens, this panel shifts into the main vertical flow, stacking below the main form sections and spanning nearly the full width of the main column with 24–32px of vertical space above it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, behaves like a right-aligned, floated or flex column within the main content area. It remains scrollable with the page and does not fix to the viewport. On smaller screens, it drops into standard block-flow, stacking beneath the main fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pledge-submit form sits near the bottom of the main column, directly below the donor-info form with approximately 24–32px of vertical space separating them. It is left-aligned with the rest of the form content and uses the same width (about 65–70% of the main column on desktop). This block primarily contains the primary submit button and, if needed, a brief terms/consent line positioned immediately above or below the button with minimal spacing (8–12px). Vertically, the submit button aligns roughly with the lower portion of the pledge summary panel to its right, so users see both the final summary and the call-to-action in the same visual band. There is a comfortable margin (32–48px) below this block before the end of the main content area to avoid a cramped bottom edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element in the vertical flow; the button does not stick or float. On small screens, it spans close to the full width of the column, with the summary panel stacking above or below depending on responsive rules.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The confirmation message appears in the main right column, occupying the same horizontal footprint as the primary form sections (spanning most of the column width, approximately 70–80%). When shown, it is placed just above the pledge-submit form area, with 24px of vertical spacing separating it from the donor-info form above. The message is presented in a simple, clearly bordered or lightly shaded box with 16–24px internal padding, using a calm, positive color accent consistent with the church’s palette but still subdued in keeping with the utilitarian style. If the workflow hides or de-emphasizes the form after success, this panel can shift upward to sit directly below the page title and intro, occupying the central area of the main column and leaving more space below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally in the normal vertical flow; it pushes the submit form down or replaces it depending on implementation. It is not sticky or floating and reflows naturally on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission error alert is displayed as a compact, high-visibility banner-like box within the main right column, typically inserted directly above the pledge-submit form and below the donor-info form, with 16–24px of vertical separation on both sides. It spans approximately the same width as the form fields (about 70–80% of the main column on desktop), aligning with the left edge of the form. The alert uses a subtle but clear error color for the border or left accent bar, with 12–16px internal padding and a concise message, matching the restrained, informational style of the site. If multiple errors are present, the alert may expand vertically but remains confined to this region, ensuring the top section of the page (title and intro) is not visually overwhelmed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in-line without special behavior, shifting other components up or down accordingly. On smaller screens, it remains full-width within the main column and stacks between donor info and the submit button.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ministries navigation link is placed in the left sidebar column beneath the hero image, inside a vertical list of navigation items. It is left-aligned within the sidebar, which occupies roughly 25–30% of the centered content container’s width. Each link, including this one, sits on its own line with approximately 12–16px padding above and below and full-width clickable area. Thin horizontal rules separate it from the links above and below, creating a slightly dense but orderly stack consistent with the mockup’s sidebar. The currently active link (if this page lives under Ministries) may be highlighted with a different text color or simple background strip, while still remaining understated. The sidebar as a whole starts near the top of the content band, aligning vertically with the page title in the main column and extending down alongside the form content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the left sidebar navigation; it scrolls with the page and does not float. On narrow screens, the sidebar (including this link) is expected to stack above the main content, becoming a full-width navigation block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL sync manager is an invisible component logically attached to the main right column, closely associated with the giving-type selector, fund selector, and pledge-amount input. It sits conceptually beneath these form elements in the component hierarchy, monitoring changes and updating URL parameters accordingly. From a visual/layout standpoint it occupies no width or height and does not create any additional spacing; it functions entirely behind the scenes while the user interacts with the visible form controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-interactive; it resides in the normal document flow at the code level but has no rendered presence and does not alter positions or sizing of other components.

name: Care & Counseling Overview
title: Care & Counseling
description: Provides an overview of care and counseling areas, and lets users find pastors and counselors by specialty and navigate to detailed pastor profiles.

core_features: Load and display care overview content (intro and care areas) using getCareOverview.
Display a list of care and counseling areas that can be used to filter pastors by specialty.
Load and display pastor filter options (specialty and sort options) using getPastorFilterOptions.
Search and list pastors and counselors using searchPastors, with filters and sorting applied.
Highlight and prioritize pastors relevant to a specialty passed via the URL parameter.
Allow navigation to the Pastor Profile & Counseling Request page when a pastor is selected.
user_workflows: Browse care overview and pastors: User opens care.html with no URL parameters; page calls getCareOverview to show intro text and care areas; page calls getPastorFilterOptions to populate specialty and sort controls; page calls searchPastors with default filters (no specialty, counselor availability as needed) and default sort; user optionally selects a specialty or changes sort; each change updates the URL querystring and re-calls searchPastors; user clicks a pastor card or name to navigate to pastor_profile.html with the corresponding pastorId.

Arrive with a preselected specialty: User opens care.html with a specialty parameter in the URL; page reads the specialty from the querystring; page calls getCareOverview and getPastorFilterOptions; if the URL specialty matches a known specialty option, the page sets this as the active specialty filter and highlights the corresponding care area and/or filter control; page calls searchPastors with this specialty filter and default sort; user can clear or change the specialty filter, which updates the URL and re-calls searchPastors; user selects a pastor to open pastor_profile.html with the pastorId parameter.

Explore care areas then select a pastor: User opens care.html; after overview content loads, user clicks a care area representing a type of care; the page maps the selected care area key to a specialty filter (if applicable), updates the specialty querystring parameter, and calls searchPastors with the selected specialty; filtered pastors are displayed; user reviews pastor cards and clicks a card or a dedicated action to navigate to pastor_profile.html with pastorId in the querystring for requesting counseling.

Refine pastor list by sorting: User has any current filter state (including possibly a specialty from URL); user opens sort controls populated by getPastorFilterOptions; user selects a sort option; page updates the sort value in the querystring and triggers searchPastors with existing filters plus the new sortBy; pastor list updates according to the selected sort order; user can then click a pastor to open pastor_profile.html with pastorId.
interactions: On page load, the page reads the specialty URL parameter (if present), then calls getCareOverview and getPastorFilterOptions; after filter state is established, the page calls searchPastors with filters derived from URL and default settings.

When the user clicks a care area item, the page sets or updates the active specialty filter based on that care area’s key, updates the specialty parameter in the URL, and re-calls searchPastors with the updated filters.

When the user changes the specialty in the pastor filter controls, the page updates the specialty parameter in the URL, synchronizes the selected state across controls (care areas and specialty dropdown or chips), and re-calls searchPastors.
When the user changes the sort option, the page updates a sort parameter in the URL (for example, sortBy), and re-calls searchPastors with the same filters and new sort option.
When the user chooses to clear the specialty filter, the page removes the specialty parameter from the URL and calls searchPastors with no specialty filter applied.

When the user clicks on a pastor’s name, card, or a dedicated request action, the page navigates to pastor_profile.html, including a pastorId parameter in the querystring derived from the selected pastor record.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL query parameters and API responses. On initialization, the page parses the specialty query parameter from the current URL. After that, getCareOverview and getPastorFilterOptions are called to retrieve dynamic content and available filter options. The active specialty filter is resolved by matching the URL specialty value, if any, against the specialties provided by getPastorFilterOptions and any care area keys from getCareOverview. If there is a match, the specialty filter is set to that value; otherwise, the specialty filter is left unset and the URL parameter can be ignored by the filter logic. A sortBy query parameter is also read from the URL (if present) and validated against the sortOptions returned by getPastorFilterOptions; if invalid or missing, a default sort option is chosen based only on the dynamic sortOptions data. After filter and sort state are derived, searchPastors is called with a filters object (containing the resolved specialty and an indicator for counseling availability) and a sortBy value. Any user interaction that changes the active specialty or sort option results in the URL querystring being updated to reflect the new state (adding, changing, or removing the specialty and sortBy parameters). The current UI (care area selection, specialty filters, sort selection, and pastor list) is always re-derived from the combination of URL parameters and the latest API responses, with no additional persistent storage used.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getCareOverview to retrieve introText, careAreas, and featuredPastors, then exposes this data to child components that display overview content and care areas.
On page initialization, calls getPastorFilterOptions to retrieve specialtyOptions and sortOptions, then initializes filter UI state and validates any corresponding URL parameters.

Parses and manages URL query parameters related to care and counseling filters, including specialty and sortBy. Provides methods to read these parameters during initialization and to update them when filter or sort changes occur.
Displays the dynamic introductory text about care and counseling services retrieved from getCareOverview, providing context about available support.

Displays the list of careAreas from getCareOverview as selectable items. Selecting an item sets the active specialty filter (when the care area key can be mapped to a specialty value), updates the URL specialty parameter, and triggers a new searchPastors call. Also visually reflects the currently active specialty derived from URL and filter state.

Provides user controls for filtering and sorting pastors, using the dynamic specialtyOptions and sortOptions returned by getPastorFilterOptions. When the specialty filter changes, it updates the internal filter state, synchronizes with the URL specialty parameter, and triggers searchPastors. When the sort option changes, it updates the URL sortBy parameter and re-triggers searchPastors. It also supports clearing filters, which removes the specialty parameter from the URL and refreshes the results with no specialty filter.

Displays the featuredPastors subset from getCareOverview as a list or strip of pastor entries, emphasizing key specialties and bio snippets. Each entry is interactive; clicking an entry navigates to pastor_profile.html with the selected pastorId in the querystring.

Coordinates calls to searchPastors whenever filter or sort state changes. It constructs a filters object based on the resolved specialty (from URL parameters and UI controls) and the counseling availability requirement, and passes a sortBy value determined from the sort options and URL state. It then exposes the resulting list of pastors to the pastor-results-list component.

Displays the list of pastors returned by searchPastors, including their names, photo URLs, bio snippets, and specialties. Reflects the current filters and sort order. Each pastor entry supports navigation to pastor_profile.html with a pastorId parameter in the URL when the user clicks the card, name, or a dedicated request action.

Listens for empty results or error responses from getCareOverview, getPastorFilterOptions, or searchPastors. When no pastors are returned, it causes the pastor-results-list to display an appropriate empty-state message derived from configuration or higher-level logic (without hardcoded domain data). When an error occurs, it can trigger a generic error message and suppress further dependent calls until recovered.

Handles click events from featured-pastors-strip and pastor-results-list. When triggered, it reads the selected pastorId from the clicked pastor item and navigates the browser to pastor_profile.html, appending pastorId as a query parameter in the URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with approximately 3–4 columns for the left sidebar and 8–9 columns for the main content area within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear top-to-bottom template as the mockup: global church nav and logo at the very top (outside this component set), then a full-width photographic hero band for the Care & Counseling area. Below the hero, all listed components live inside a centered, max-width content band that is split into two columns: a narrower left sidebar column for care areas (acting like section navigation/filters) and a wider right column for the Care & Counseling overview content, pastor filters, and search results. Within the right column, content is stacked vertically: intro content at the top, then pastor filter controls, an optional featured pastors strip, and finally the main results list or empty/error feedback.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear top-to-bottom template as the mockup: global church nav and logo at the very top (outside this component set), then a full-width photographic hero band for the Care & Counseling area. Below the hero, all listed components live inside a centered, max-width content band that is split into two columns: a narrower left sidebar column for care areas (acting like section navigation/filters) and a wider right column for the Care & Counseling overview content, pastor filters, and search results. Within the right column, content is stacked vertically: intro content at the top, then pastor filter controls, an optional featured pastors strip, and finally the main results list or empty/error feedback.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The content band below the hero uses a 12-column grid with the left sidebar occupying roughly 3–4 columns (about 25–30% width) and the right main column taking the remaining 8–9 columns (about 70–75% width). Vertical spacing mirrors the mockup: a modest gap (around 24–32px) between the hero and the content band, generous spacing around the main page heading and intro text, and tighter spacing within dense elements like the sidebar list and results list. Featured blocks like the intro section and featured pastors strip receive a bit more breathing room (24–32px separation) from neighboring elements, whereas functional controls like filters sit closer (16–24px) to the content they control.
layout_strategies.content_density: The left sidebar is intentionally denser, with care areas listed in a compact vertical stack separated by thin rules and modest padding, reflecting the utilitarian navigation look of the mockup. The right main column is more open: the intro heading and first paragraph have clear vertical separation and line spacing to improve readability, while the pastor results list is moderately dense but still well-structured with consistent vertical rhythm. Overall, the layout balances a content-first, information-heavy feel with enough whitespace to keep the page from feeling cramped, in line with a traditional church informational website.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The primary visual flow starts at the top nav and logo, then moves down to the full-width hero image. From the hero, the user’s eye is drawn into the right-hand page title and intro (inside the care-intro-section) at the top of the main column. After reading the heading and green-highlighted intro text, attention naturally moves downward to the pastor filter controls and any featured pastors strip, then further down into the main pastor results list. The left sidebar is always visible in the periphery, discovered via side vision after the main heading, enabling users to pivot between care areas or specialties without overwhelming the main reading flow.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the church’s global nav and a warm, full-width hero photograph related to care and counseling, the page resolves into a centered two-column content band. On the left, a relatively narrow sidebar column runs from just below the hero down the page, listing care and counseling areas in a compact, rule-separated vertical menu. To the right, a wider main column begins with the Care & Counseling Overview title and a green-accented introductory paragraph, with a small illustrative icon aligned toward the right side of this text block to echo the mockup’s visual cue. Directly beneath this intro, a slim row of pastor filter controls spans the width of the main column, followed by an optional featured pastors strip that showcases a few highlighted pastors in a modest horizontal arrangement. Below that, the main pastor results list occupies the remainder of the right column, showing pastors in a structured, moderately dense list; when there are no matches or an error occurs, a centered, clearly legible message appears in the same area instead. Throughout, the left sidebar remains vertically aligned with the main content, and all elements share a flat, minimalist visual style with subtle spacing in multiples of 8px, delivering a calm, informational layout suited to a church context.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and occupies no explicit space in the layout. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the Care & Counseling Overview page’s content band, just below the hero region, and is responsible for loading the high-level care overview data that will populate the care-intro-section and optional featured content. It does not render any UI; instead, it ensures that the main right-hand column has the data it needs before those visual components appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering data layer; no spatial footprint. It wraps or precedes visual components like care-intro-section and featured-pastors-strip in the DOM but does not affect layout spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is also a non-visual component with no direct on-screen presence. It conceptually sits just above or around the pastor-filter-controls within the main right column, supplying the available specialties, care areas, or tags that populate the filter UI. It does not occupy pixels; instead, it ensures that when the filter row is rendered, all dropdowns and options are fully populated.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering data provider attached to the pastor-filter-controls area; it does not change the layout or spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is an invisible behavioral layer that conceptually spans the entire two-column content band, synchronizing the selected care area in the left sidebar and the filter state in the main column with URL parameters. It does not present UI; users experience it indirectly when they navigate between care areas or filter pastors and see the URL update. Its presence does not affect any spacing or dimensions in the visual layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, global state handler for this page section; no dedicated space, fixed position, or layout impact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The care-intro-section is the first visible block in the right main column, aligned to the top of that column and starting just below the bottom edge of the hero image. Horizontally, it spans the full width of the right column (roughly 70–75% of the total page content width), with 24–32px of padding inside the block to give the heading and text room to breathe. At the top of this block sits the page title (e.g., “Care & Counseling”) in a larger, bold type, left-aligned, with about 16–24px of vertical space above and below it. Immediately beneath the title, there is a green-highlighted introductory paragraph that runs roughly 60–70% across the width of the text area on the left side, leaving enough space on the right for a small illustrative icon. This icon is positioned toward the upper-right corner of the intro text area, either floated or aligned right so that it sits roughly at the mid-height of the intro paragraph, visually echoing the mockup’s right-side illustration. Below the intro, any additional overview text continues in standard body color with modest line height, maintaining a comfortable vertical gap (16–24px) from the intro paragraph. Overall, this section creates a focal point at the top of the main column and anchors the user’s understanding of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element within the right column; scrolls normally with the page and defines the upper anchor for main-column content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The care-areas-list occupies the entire left sidebar column beneath the hero, aligned at the same vertical starting point as the care-intro-section on the right. It spans roughly 25–30% of the content band width and extends downward as far as needed to fit all care areas. Each care area appears as a single-line or two-line row, left-aligned, with modest vertical padding (8–12px top and bottom) and horizontal padding (16px) inside the sidebar. Thin horizontal rules separate each item, creating a stacked, utilitarian list similar to the mockup’s sidebar navigation. The currently selected care area is emphasized by a background color change or bolder text weight and possibly a subtle left border, making it clearly stand out while still within the minimalist style. The list is vertically dense compared to the main column, with relatively small gaps between items, and it scrolls with the page as a single column; it is not sticky by default. The right edge of this sidebar lines up with the gutter between it and the main column, keeping a consistent 16–24px gap between the two columns.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical column on the left within the content band; scrolls normally along with the main column content, maintaining its width and position relative to the page center.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pastor-filter-controls form a slim, functional strip located in the right main column directly beneath the care-intro-section. There is a clear vertical gap of around 24px separating the bottom of the intro section from the top of this filter row so that the intro feels distinct. Horizontally, this strip spans nearly the full width of the right column, with 16px of internal horizontal padding to align controls with the text above. The controls (e.g., specialty dropdown, search field, tags) are left-aligned within the strip, arranged in a single row on wider screens, wrapping onto a second row if space is limited. The overall height of the strip is modest (roughly 48–64px depending on the number of controls), mirroring the compact RSS badge area observed in the mockup while maintaining adequate spacing around form elements for usability. The visual emphasis is functional rather than decorative: flat inputs, simple labels, and subtle spacing create a clean filter panel that does not overpower the intro section above or the results below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element within the right column, immediately following the care-intro-section; scrolls with the page and maintains alignment with the column grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-pastors-strip sits in the right main column below the pastor-filter-controls, separated from the filters by about 16–24px of vertical space. When present, it spans the full width of the right column and appears as a low-profile, horizontally-oriented band showcasing a small number of featured pastors. Each featured pastor occupies a card-like area arranged from left to right within this strip, with 16–24px of horizontal spacing between cards. The strip itself uses modest padding (16px on all sides) to frame the content but remains visually flat and simple, consistent with the overall design. The height of the strip is relatively compact compared to the full results list, similar in visual weight to a “highlight” row under the intro, so it doesn’t overshadow the main list. If there are no featured pastors, this strip collapses entirely, and the pastor-results-list moves up to occupy its vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional block-level element within the right column; it appears only when there are featured pastors and otherwise leaves no gap, allowing other content to shift upward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pastor-search-manager is an invisible controller responsible for executing searches and filtering pastors based on the selected care area and filter controls. Conceptually, it is associated with the main pastor-results-list region in the right column, sitting between the filters and the results in the data flow. It does not render any visible UI, so it has no height, width, or padding in the layout. Users experience it as the logic that updates the list of pastors below the filters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering logic layer; no explicit spatial behavior. It influences which items appear in the pastor-results-list but does not itself occupy layout space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pastor-results-list occupies the main body of the right column below the pastor-filter-controls and any featured-pastors-strip. It is separated from the element above it by roughly 24px of vertical space to clearly mark the start of the results section. Horizontally, the list spans the full width of the right column, with consistent internal margins or padding (16–24px) aligning it with the intro section and filters. Each pastor entry appears as a horizontally oriented row or compact card stacked vertically, with consistent vertical spacing (16–24px) between entries, giving the list a moderately dense but readable rhythm. Important information (name, title, specialty tags, and primary contact or link to profile) is left-aligned, and a subtle visual affordance (e.g., a right-arrow, linked name, or “View Profile” text) indicates that clicking will navigate to the detailed pastor profile. The list extends downward as needed and is the dominant scrollable content in the main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level list within the right column; scrolls with the page and grows vertically based on the number of results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-or-error-state-handler shares the same spatial area as the pastor-results-list in the right column. When there are no matching pastors or an error occurs, it temporarily replaces the results list content with a clearly visible message. This message is centered within the width of the right column, with generous vertical padding (32–48px above and below) so it feels intentional rather than like a missing list. The layout alignment matches the rest of the main column: the text is left-aligned to the same text column as results items, but the block’s overall height creates a spacious, calm state that is easy to notice. When results are available, this handler is effectively hidden and takes up no space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional block within the right column that appears in place of the pastor-results-list area; it collapses entirely when not active, allowing the results list to occupy the space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is a behavioral/navigation layer with no direct visual representation. It is conceptually attached to interactive elements within the pastor-results-list (such as pastor names, cards, or profile links). When a user clicks a pastor entry, this handler triggers navigation to that pastor’s detailed profile page. Because it does not render UI, it does not occupy any independent space; its influence is seen only through the clickable affordances embedded in each result row, which remain aligned within the right column’s layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual navigation handler bound to interactive elements inside the pastor-results-list; no independent spatial behavior or footprint.

name: Pastor Profile & Counseling Request
title: Pastor Profile & Counseling Request
description: Displays a pastor’s profile and provides a workflow to request a counseling appointment within the upcoming week using available morning time slots.

core_features: Load and display the selected pastor’s profile using getPastorProfile(pastorId) based on the pastorId URL parameter.
Show counseling-related details for the pastor (bio, contact information, counseling specialties) when available.
Provide a "Request Counseling Appointment" action that focuses the scheduling section and, if needed, triggers loading of current availability.

Allow the user to select a counseling date constrained to the next calendar week and limited to morning time slots by querying getCounselingAvailabilityForPastor(pastorId, dateFrom, dateTo, timeFrom, timeTo).
Display all unbooked counseling availability slots returned by getCounselingAvailabilityForPastor for the selected date and morning time range, and allow the user to select one slot.
Provide an appointment request form that captures name, email, reason for visit (mapped to CounselingAppointmentRequest.reason enum values), and optional notes.
Submit the appointment request using submitCounselingAppointmentRequest(pastorId, availabilitySlotId, name, email, reason, notes) and show the server response (success, status, message, slotSummary).
Provide clear disabled or alternative messaging when the pastor is not active or not available for counseling (based on getPastorProfile response).
Support navigation back to the Care & Counseling overview via a "Back to Care & Counseling" link that routes to care.html.
Persist and restore key UI state (selected date, selected slot, submission status) via URL parameters so the page state can be reloaded or shared.
user_workflows: View pastor profile: (1) User navigates to pastor_profile.html with a pastorId parameter from care.html or about.html. (2) On load, the page reads pastorId from the URL and calls getPastorProfile(pastorId). (3) When the response is returned, the page displays the pastor’s name, bio, contact details, and counseling specialties. (4) If the pastor is active and marked as a counselor, the Request Counseling Appointment action is enabled; otherwise, scheduling controls are hidden or disabled.

Start counseling request from Care & Counseling: (1) User clicks a pastor link on care.html which opens pastor_profile.html?pastorId={id}. (2) The profile is loaded via getPastorProfile. (3) User clicks the Request Counseling Appointment button. (4) The page scrolls/focuses to the scheduling section and checks the URL for an existing date parameter; if present, that date is preselected, otherwise a default date within the next calendar week is chosen. (5) The page triggers getCounselingAvailabilityForPastor with the selected date range and morning time window to load available slots.

Select a counseling date and morning time slot: (1) In the scheduling section, the user chooses a date using the date selector, which is constrained so that only dates within the next calendar week can be selected. (2) When the date changes, the page updates the date parameter in the URL (e.g., date=YYYY-MM-DD). (3) The availability loader calls getCounselingAvailabilityForPastor(pastorId, dateFrom, dateTo, timeFrom, timeTo) for that specific date and a configured morning time range (e.g., timeFrom='09:00', timeTo='12:00'). (4) The returned slots are filtered to exclude any with isBooked=true, and the remaining slots are shown as selectable options. (5) The user clicks one slot; the selected slot ID is stored in page state, reflected in the URL as slotId={availabilitySlotId}, and the appointment form is enabled.

Submit a counseling appointment request: (1) After a slot is selected, the appointment form becomes active. (2) The user enters name and email, selects a reason aligned with CounselingAppointmentRequest.reason enum values (e.g., marriage, grief, family, spiritual_direction, other), and can enter optional notes. (3) When the user submits the form, client-side validation ensures all required fields are present and a slot is selected; if validation passes, the page calls submitCounselingAppointmentRequest(pastorId, availabilitySlotId, name, email, reason, notes). (4) While the request is in progress, the form is disabled and a loading state is shown. (5) On a successful response where success=true, the page shows a confirmation panel using message, status, and slotSummary. The URL is updated with requestStatus=success and appointmentRequestId={returnedId}. (6) If success=false or the call fails, an error message is displayed, and the user can correct inputs and retry.

Reload or share a pre-filled counseling request view: (1) A user or staff member opens a URL such as pastor_profile.html?pastorId={id}&date={YYYY-MM-DD}&slotId={slotId}&requestStatus=success&appointmentRequestId={id2}. (2) On load, the page reads pastorId and loads the profile. (3) The date selector initializes with the date parameter; the availability loader calls getCounselingAvailabilityForPastor for that date and morning time window. (4) Once slots are loaded, if the slotId from the URL matches a returned unbooked slot, that slot is marked as selected in the UI. (5) If requestStatus=success is present, the confirmation component displays a persisted success state referencing appointmentRequestId, without re-submitting the request.

Navigate back to Care & Counseling: (1) User clicks the "Back to Care & Counseling" link. (2) The browser navigates to care.html without additional parameters (or with whatever parameters are required by that page, as configured outside this page).
interactions: Page load with pastorId: On first render, the page reads the pastorId query parameter. If present, it triggers getPastorProfile(pastorId). If pastorId is missing or getPastorProfile returns an error or an inactive pastor, the page shows an error or not-available state and disables all counseling request interactions.

"Request Counseling Appointment" button: When clicked, the page programmatically scrolls or jumps to the counseling scheduling section. If no date has been chosen yet in the current session, a default date within the next calendar week is selected. The date parameter in the URL is set or updated accordingly, and getCounselingAvailabilityForPastor is called for that date and morning time window.

Date selection for counseling: When the user interacts with the date selector and chooses a different date, the component validates that the chosen date lies within the allowed upcoming week window. If valid, it updates the page state and URL (date=YYYY-MM-DD), clears any previously selected slot, and triggers getCounselingAvailabilityForPastor with dateFrom and dateTo corresponding to that date, and timeFrom/timeTo corresponding to the configured morning window.

Loading counseling availability: On each date change or initial scheduling activation, getCounselingAvailabilityForPastor is called with pastorId, dateFrom, dateTo, timeFrom, timeTo. While the request is pending, the slot list shows a loading state. After data is returned, slots with isBooked=false are displayed as options; slots with isBooked=true are not selectable or are omitted. If the API returns no available slots, the component shows an appropriate "no available times" message and keeps the appointment form disabled.

Time slot selection: When the user clicks a displayed availability slot, the component stores the chosen availabilitySlotId in the internal page state and updates the URL (slotId={availabilitySlotId}). The selected slot is visually marked as active, and the appointment form is enabled and pre-populated with the selected slot reference. Choosing a different slot updates state and URL accordingly.

Appointment form input: As the user types into the name, email, and notes fields or changes the reason selection, the component updates its internal form state. Required fields (name, email, reason, and selected availabilitySlotId) are validated on blur and on submit. If validation fails, inline error indicators are shown and submit is blocked.

Appointment form submission: On form submit, if validation passes, the component calls submitCounselingAppointmentRequest with the current pastorId, selected availabilitySlotId, name, email, reason, and notes. During the call, the form fields and submit button are disabled to prevent duplicate submissions. When a response is received: (a) If success=true, the page displays a confirmation panel, updates the URL to include appointmentRequestId and requestStatus=success, and may optionally collapse or lock the form. (b) If success=false or an error occurs, the page shows an error message, keeps the form data intact, and re-enables the submit button so the user can try again.

Confirmation display: When a successful submission occurs or when the page is loaded with requestStatus=success and appointmentRequestId in the URL, the confirmation component reads these values and displays a status summary using the response.message, response.status, and response.slotSummary fields if available. If the page is reloaded, the presence of these URL parameters ensures the confirmation remains visible without re-calling submitCounselingAppointmentRequest.

Pastor not available for counseling: If getPastorProfile returns isCounselor=false or isActive=false, the scheduling button and form are disabled or replaced with a message indicating that online counseling requests are not available for this pastor. No calls to getCounselingAvailabilityForPastor or submitCounselingAppointmentRequest are made in this case.
"Back to Care & Counseling" link: When clicked, the browser navigates directly to care.html. No additional logic is executed on this page beyond the navigation event.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters to derive and persist its state. Required state: pastorId (string) identifies which pastor to load; if absent, profile loading is skipped and an error state is shown. Optional state: date (ISO date string, e.g., YYYY-MM-DD) indicates the currently selected counseling date; if absent, the date selector initializes to a default date within the next calendar week, and the URL is updated with that date. Optional state: slotId (string) indicates the currently selected availabilitySlotId; after getCounselingAvailabilityForPastor returns for the active date, if a slot with this ID exists and is not booked, it is marked as selected; otherwise the slot selection is cleared and the URL slotId parameter is removed. Optional state: requestStatus (string, e.g., 'success') and appointmentRequestId (string) describe the result of a prior submission and allow the confirmation state to persist across page reloads or shared links. Whenever the user changes the date or selected slot, or successfully submits a request, the page updates the querystring via the History API (pushState/replaceState) without reloading. All components read the current URL query parameters on initialization and on history changes (e.g., back/forward navigation) to keep the visible state synchronized with the URL. No localStorage or other client-side storage mechanisms are used.

components.functionality: On page initialization and whenever the pastorId URL parameter changes, this component reads pastorId from the querystring and calls getPastorProfile(pastorId). It stores the returned pastor object in shared state for other components and exposes loading and error flags.

Displays the loaded pastor’s profile information including fullName, bio, photoUrl, email, phone, and specialties. Shows different states based on availability: (a) a loading state while pastor-profile-loader is fetching, (b) an error state if loading fails or no pastor is found, and (c) an inactive/not-counselor message when isCounselor=false or isActive=false.
Provides a navigational link or button labeled to return to Care & Counseling. When activated, it navigates the browser to care.html.

Displays a "Request Counseling Appointment" button if the loaded pastor is active and available for counseling. When clicked, it scrolls or focuses the view to the counseling scheduling section and, if a date has not yet been initialized, sets a default upcoming date within the next calendar week and triggers loading of availability.

Provides a date input limited to dates in the next calendar week. On initialization, it reads the date parameter from the URL. If the parameter is missing or outside the allowed window, it selects a valid default date within the upcoming week and writes it back to the URL. On user change, it validates the date, updates the URL date parameter, clears any selected slot, and notifies the availability loader to fetch slots for the chosen date.

Fetches counseling availability slots for the current pastor and selected date. When notified of a valid date or when either pastorId or date changes, it computes dateFrom and dateTo for that single date and calls getCounselingAvailabilityForPastor(pastorId, dateFrom, dateTo, timeFrom, timeTo) using a configured morning time window (for example, timeFrom='09:00' and timeTo='12:00'). It stores the returned slots in shared state, along with loading and error flags.

Displays the list of available counseling time slots for the selected date using the slots provided by counseling-availability-loader. It filters out any slots where isBooked=true and presents the remaining as selectable options, showing startDateTime, endDateTime, and location as provided. When the user selects a slot, it records the availabilitySlotId in shared state, updates the URL parameter slotId, and marks the chosen slot as active. If the URL includes a slotId on load and that slot is present and unbooked in the current slots list, it auto-selects that slot.

Captures the details needed to request a counseling appointment for the selected pastor and time slot. It requires a selected availabilitySlotId (from timeslot-list-selector) and includes fields for name, email, reason (mapped to CounselingAppointmentRequest.reason enum values such as marriage, marriage_counseling, grief, family, spiritual_direction, other), and optional notes. The form stays disabled until both a valid pastor and slot are available. On submit, it validates required fields and calls submitCounselingAppointmentRequest(pastorId, availabilitySlotId, name, email, reason, notes). It exposes submission progress, success, and error states for the confirmation component.

Shows the outcome of an appointment request submission. It listens for the response from submitCounselingAppointmentRequest and, when success=true, displays the acknowledgment message, status (e.g., pending), and the slotSummary (startDateTime and endDateTime). It also reads requestStatus and appointmentRequestId from the URL on initialization; if requestStatus indicates success and appointmentRequestId is present, it renders a persisted confirmation state even after a page refresh. If an error occurs during submission, it displays an error message and allows the form component to remain editable.

Coordinates visibility and enabled/disabled states of the scheduling components (date selector, availability loader, timeslot list, and appointment form) based on the pastor’s counseling availability. If the loaded pastor has isCounselor=true and isActive=true, it enables the scheduling pipeline; otherwise, it hides or disables the counseling-date-selector, counseling-availability-loader, timeslot-list-selector, counseling-appointment-form, and request-counseling-cta.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with approximately 3–4 columns for the left sidebar and 8–9 columns for the main content area within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: Reuse the mockup’s structure: global nav and logo at the top, then a full-width photographic hero showing a warm church-related image. Below the hero, place a centered max-width content container split into a left sidebar column and a right main column. The left column carries navigational/context items (including the back-to-care link), while the right column holds the pastor profile and the counseling request workflow in a linear, top‑to‑bottom sequence.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Reuse the mockup’s structure: global nav and logo at the top, then a full-width photographic hero showing a warm church-related image. Below the hero, place a centered max-width content container split into a left sidebar column and a right main column. The left column carries navigational/context items (including the back-to-care link), while the right column holds the pastor profile and the counseling request workflow in a linear, top‑to‑bottom sequence.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero spans 100% width and roughly 25–30% of the initial viewport height. Below it, the content container is centered at about 1080–1200px max width. Inside that, the left sidebar occupies about 25–30% of the width; the main content column uses the remaining 70–75%. Vertically, the pastor’s profile occupies the upper portion of the main column, with the scheduling UI (date selector, timeslots, form/confirmation) stacked beneath, taking the lower portion. Section separations use 24–32px vertical margins; internal padding within components follows 8–16px increments.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a denser, utilitarian feel in the left sidebar (compact link spacing) and a more spacious, readable feel in the main column. The main column uses generous spacing around the page title and pastor profile intro, moderate spacing between the scheduling steps, and tighter spacing within the form fields, timeslot list, and buttons. Overall density is moderate: clearly structured but not airy or ultra-minimal.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the global nav and logo down to the hero image, then into the main column where the user first encounters the page heading/pastor profile information. From there, the eye moves down through an emphasized introductory line, across to the pastor’s photo/illustrative icon on the right, and then continues downward into the counseling request steps: date selection, then visible loading/availability feedback, then time selection, then the appointment form. After submission, the confirmation panel appears in the same main column position, keeping the flow vertical and predictable. The left sidebar remains a secondary, peripheral anchor for navigation rather than the primary focus.

layout.overall_description: Below the church’s global navigation and a warm, full-width hero photograph, a centered content band presents two columns: a compact left sidebar and a wider right main content area, aligned on a shared top baseline. In the left column, the back-to-care navigation link appears as part of a simple vertical navigation stack with subtle separators, reflecting a traditional informational site. In the right column, a modestly sized, right-aligned counseling CTA button sits along the top edge of the content, visually in line with the page heading. Immediately below, the pastor profile summary appears as the main introduction: the first line of text is emphasized in a green accent color, and a small pastor portrait or icon anchors the right side of the text block, echoing the mockup’s illustrative icon placement. Beneath this profile band, the counseling request flow unfolds in a linear, stacked fashion: date selector, availability state (with inline loader and guard messaging when needed), timeslot options, then the appointment form. Each step is separated by about 24–32px of vertical space, maintaining clarity without feeling airy. Upon successful scheduling, the form area is replaced by a green-accented confirmation panel spanning the main column’s width, while the rest of the page structure remains static. The overall impression is of a calm, content-first layout with a slightly traditional, church-appropriate tone and a clear, top-to-bottom flow of information and actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pastor-profile-loader occupies the same footprint as the pastor-profile-summary within the right main content column. It appears directly below the row that contains the page heading and the right-aligned request counseling CTA, spanning roughly 100% of the main column width with 16–24px of padding inside. Vertically, it behaves like a shallow card or block (about 1/4 of the visible main column height) showing skeleton lines or a subtle spinner centered within the text area, leaving at least 24px of clear space below before the scheduling section begins. It aligns flush with the left text edge of the main column and respects the 8–16px internal spacing consistent with other content blocks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline block that temporarily replaces the pastor-profile-summary area until data is loaded, pushing subsequent components (date selector, timeslot list, form) downward as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pastor-profile-summary is the first substantial content block in the right main column, positioned directly beneath the top row that includes the page heading and the small, right-aligned request counseling CTA. It spans nearly the full width of the main column (leaving 24px side padding inside the container), starting about 16–24px below the heading row. The block is roughly 1/3 of the main-column visible area in height. The first line or short introductory paragraph is styled in green and sits at the top left of this block, matching the mockup’s highlighted intro text.
