To echo the illustrative icon placement, a medium-sized pastor portrait or icon is visually anchored on the right side of this block—either right-aligned within the text area or as a narrow subcolumn taking about 25–30% of the block width—while the bio text flows in the remaining left portion. There is 24–32px of vertical space below this summary before the counseling date selector begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline content block; height grows with content but always sits as a continuous band between the heading/CTA row above and the scheduling UI below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-care-link is placed in the left sidebar column, aligned with the top edge of the main content column (just below the hero band). It appears as a full-width text link row within the sidebar, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16px horizontal padding from the sidebar’s left edge. It sits at the top of a vertical stack of potential care-related navigation items, separated from any item below by a thin horizontal rule that spans nearly the full sidebar width. The link text is left-aligned and may be bolded or slightly darker to indicate its relevance to the current context, but it is sized similarly to typical sidebar navigation items. There is minimal vertical whitespace above and below it, matching the densified sidebar style of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky sidebar navigation item that scrolls with the page; it remains confined to the 25–30% width left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The request-counseling-cta is a small-to-medium primary button positioned at the top of the right main content column, along the same horizontal line as the page title. It is right-aligned within the main column, leaving 16–24px of padding from the column’s right edge. The button’s height aligns roughly with a single line of heading text, and its width is compact (about 25–30% of the main column width or just enough to fit a short label plus 16px horizontal padding on each side). There is about 8–12px of vertical breathing room above the button and 16–24px below before the pastor-profile-summary block. Visually, it acts like the mockup’s “RSS badge”—a prominent but compact affordance near the title that signals an action entry point into the scheduling flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element in the heading row; on interaction it may expand or reveal the scheduling section below but does not change its own position. It scrolls away with the content rather than sticking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The counseling-date-selector sits in the right main column directly beneath the pastor-profile-summary block, separated from it by about 24–32px of vertical space. It is left-aligned and spans approximately 60–70% of the main column width, leaving a comfortable margin on the right where the eye can rest or where additional help text could appear. A label and any helper text are stacked above the date input control with 4–8px spacing. The control itself (e.g., a date picker input or a row of selectable upcoming dates) uses 12–16px internal padding and occupies a single horizontal band. There is roughly 16px of vertical space below the selector before the availability loader/timeslot area begins, maintaining the linear, step-by-step feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form-like block within the main column; expands slightly in height if a calendar popover is open, otherwise behaves as a fixed-height row anchoring the top of the scheduling workflow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The counseling-availability-loader appears directly beneath the counseling-date-selector in the main column, occupying the rectangular area where the timeslot-list-selector will appear. It spans roughly the same 60–70% width used by the date selector and is left-aligned with it, sitting about 8–12px below the date selector’s bottom edge. The loader is visually modest: a centered spinner or progress indicator with a short line of text (e.g., “Checking morning availability…”) within a shallow band, about 48–64px tall. When active, it pushes the timeslot list and form downward; when complete, this loader collapses and yields its space to the timeslot list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block that appears and disappears between the date selector and the timeslot list; height is fixed while visible and collapses to zero height when not in use.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeslot-list-selector is positioned in the right main column below the counseling-date-selector (and below the counseling-availability-loader when the loader is showing). It starts about 8–16px beneath the preceding element and spans approximately 60–70% of the main column width, left-aligned with the date selector. Internally, it lays out available morning time slots as a grid or single row of pill-shaped buttons or list items, each with about 8–12px padding, arranged horizontally and wrapping onto the next line as needed. The overall block height depends on the number of slots but generally remains under 1/3 of the main column visible area. A small label or caption can sit above the slot pills with 4–8px spacing, and there is 16–24px of vertical space below the last row of time slots before the counseling-appointment-form begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline selector that expands vertically as more time slots are shown; it pushes the appointment form downward but remains constrained to the main column’s flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The counseling-appointment-form sits directly beneath the timeslot-list-selector in the right main column, separated by 24–32px to clearly indicate a new step. It spans most of the main column width (about 70–80%, centered or left-aligned with the selectors above), using a vertical stack of fields (name, contact details, notes) with 12–16px vertical spacing between fields. The form’s outer container has 16px padding on all sides and a subtle border or background tint consistent with the mockup’s utilitarian style. It typically consumes about 1/3 to 1/2 of the main column’s visible height, depending on the number of fields. The primary submit action is right-aligned at the bottom of the form, with 16–24px of clear space below the button before any content that might follow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline form block that grows in height based on field count. Upon successful submission, it either collapses or is replaced in place by the appointment-confirmation-panel, preserving the vertical flow and scroll position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The appointment-confirmation-panel occupies the same vertical position and width as the counseling-appointment-form in the right main column. When displayed, it replaces the form content, starting roughly 24–32px below the timeslot-list-selector. The panel spans about 70–80% of the main column width, with 16–24px of internal padding and a subtle green accent (border, icon, or background strip) to signal success and echo the green intro text used in the pastor profile. It is shorter than the form in most cases—roughly 1/4 to 1/3 of the main column height—with a brief confirmation message at the top, details of the scheduled date and time in the middle, and perhaps a secondary action (e.g., “Back to care & support” or “Add to calendar”) aligned to the right at the bottom. There is 24px of clear space below the panel before the end of the main content area or any subsequent information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block that replaces the form when an appointment is successfully booked, occupying the same space in the flow. It does not stick or overlay; it scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The scheduler-availability-guard manifests as inline messaging and state control within the main column, primarily between the counseling-date-selector and the timeslot-list-selector. When the selected date has no eligible morning slots or lies outside the allowed upcoming-week window, it presents a narrow message band (about 48–64px tall) spanning the same 60–70% width as the selectors, left-aligned with them and positioned about 8–12px beneath the date selector. The message text is concise, with 8–12px internal padding and a subtle informational or warning color that still fits the conservative, church-appropriate palette. In these states, the timeslot-list-selector is either hidden or visually disabled beneath this message, and the appointment form is either hidden or dimmed, preserving the vertical flow but clearly indicating that the user must adjust their date selection.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible as a structural component but controls the visibility and interactivity of the availability loader, timeslot list, and form. When active, it inserts or removes an inline message band in the space between the date selector and timeslot list without affecting the rest of the layout structure.

name: About
title: About
description: Displays the church’s mission, vision, beliefs, and leadership information, and provides navigation to related pages.

core_features: Load and display mission text from getAboutPageContent
Load and display vision text from getAboutPageContent
Load and display a structured list of beliefs from getAboutPageContent
Load and display a list of leaders with names, roles, photos, and bio snippets from getAboutPageContent
Allow navigation from the About page to the Ministries page
Allow navigation from the About page to the Care page
Allow navigation from a leader item to a detailed pastor profile page when a pastorId is available
Support URL-based section selection (e.g., mission, vision, beliefs, leadership) so users and external links can open a specific section directly
user_workflows:
View mission and vision:
1. User navigates to about.html (from footer link or direct URL).
2. On page load, the page calls getAboutPageContent.
3. The mission-section component displays the missionText.
4. The vision-section component displays the visionText.
5. If a section URL parameter is present and set to 'mission' or 'vision', the page scrolls/focuses to the corresponding section and marks it as active.

Explore core beliefs:
1. User opens about.html.
2. The page calls getAboutPageContent and receives the beliefs array.
3. The beliefs-accordion component renders each belief item.
4. User clicks on a belief header to expand or collapse its body text.
5. If the section URL parameter is 'beliefs', the beliefs area is focused and the first belief item may be auto-expanded based on implementation rules that use only returned data structure.

Get to know church leaders:
1. User opens about.html.
2. The page calls getAboutPageContent and receives the leaders array.
3. The leaders-list component displays each leader’s name, role, photo, and bioSnippet.
4. User can expand/collapse or reveal additional text for individual leaders as determined by the component behavior (using the provided bioSnippet without hardcoded content).
5. If the section URL parameter is 'leadership', the leaders area is focused and highlighted.

Open a pastor’s detailed profile from a leader:
1. User scrolls to the leadership section on about.html.
2. The leaders-list component shows which leaders include a pastorId from getAboutPageContent.
3. User clicks on a leader item that has a pastorId.
4. The browser navigates to pastor_profile.html?pastorId={leader.pastorId}, passing through the pastorId from the leaders array.
5. The pastor profile page (not part of this design) uses the pastorId URL parameter to load detailed information.

Navigate from About to Ministries:
1. User is viewing about.html.
2. User clicks an action/link in the related-links component labeled for ministries exploration.
3. The browser navigates to ministries.html with no additional URL parameters required by this page.

Navigate from About to Care/Pastoral care:
1. User is viewing about.html.
2. User clicks an action/link in the related-links component labeled for pastoral care or similar.
3. The browser navigates to care.html with no additional URL parameters required by this page.

Deep-link to specific sections via URL parameters:
1. A user follows a link such as about.html?section=beliefs.
2. On page load, the section-state-router component reads the section parameter from the URL.
3. The component sets the current active section state accordingly.
4. The corresponding content section (mission, vision, beliefs, or leadership) is scrolled into view and visually indicated as active.
5. When the user clicks on another section tab or in-page navigation item, the section-state-router updates the URL parameter to match the new active section (e.g., about.html?section=leadership) without reloading content from the API.
interactions:
Initial data load:
- Trigger: Page load (about.html).
- Behavior: about-data-loader component calls getAboutPageContent.
- Result: missionText, visionText, beliefs[], and leaders[] are stored in page state and propagated to bound components.

Section tab or in-page navigation clicks:
- Trigger: User clicks on a section control (e.g., Mission, Vision, Beliefs, Leadership) within section-nav.
- Behavior: section-state-router updates internal activeSection state and updates the URL parameter section to the selected section value using history APIs.
- Result: The page scrolls/focuses to the selected section and marks it as active without re-calling getAboutPageContent.

Belief item toggle:
- Trigger: User clicks a belief item header within beliefs-accordion.
- Behavior: The component toggles the expanded/collapsed state for that belief using indices/IDs derived from the beliefs array.
- Result: The corresponding belief body text is shown or hidden. No additional API calls are made.

Leader item interaction:
- Trigger: User clicks a leader card or a dedicated 'View profile' control within leaders-list.
- Behavior: If leader.pastorId is present, the component constructs a URL pastor_profile.html?pastorId={leader.pastorId} and triggers navigation.
- Result: The browser loads pastor_profile.html with the pastorId parameter. If pastorId is not present for a leader, the component can either disable navigation or only toggle local leader details without navigation.

Navigate to Ministries:
- Trigger: User clicks the ministries-related link in related-links.
- Behavior: The browser navigates to ministries.html.
- Result: User leaves the About page and arrives at the ministries listing or detail page.

Navigate to Care:
- Trigger: User clicks the care-related link in related-links.
- Behavior: The browser navigates to care.html.
- Result: User leaves the About page and arrives at the care/pastoral care page.

URL parameter change (section) via browser controls:
- Trigger: User uses back/forward buttons after navigating between sections that updated the URL parameter.
- Behavior: section-state-router listens to URL changes (popstate) and updates the activeSection state to match the section parameter.
- Result: The corresponding section is focused/highlighted again, preserving user context.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The About page uses URL parameters to control which content section is active and scrolled into view. The primary parameter is 'section'. Valid values are determined by the implementation and correspond to logical content segments such as 'mission', 'vision', 'beliefs', and 'leadership'.

On initial load:
- The about-data-loader component executes once to call getAboutPageContent and populate page-level state with missionText, visionText, beliefs[], and leaders[]. This call is independent of the 'section' parameter so that all sections are available for in-page navigation without subsequent API calls.
- The section-state-router component reads the current URL's query string. If section is present and matches a known section identifier, activeSection is set to that value. If section is missing or invalid, a default section (defined by the implementation) is selected.
- After activeSection is determined, the component triggers focus/scroll behavior for the corresponding content section.

On in-page navigation:
- When the user clicks a section control (e.g., a tab or in-page navigation link), section-state-router updates activeSection and uses the History API (e.g., pushState/replaceState) to update the section parameter in the URL without a full page reload, such as about.html?section=beliefs.
- No new calls to getAboutPageContent are made as a result of section changes; state is kept in memory from the initial load.

On browser navigation (back/forward):
- When the user navigates using back or forward buttons and the URL's section parameter changes, section-state-router listens for popstate events and recalculates activeSection from the current URL.
- The page then scrolls/focuses to the corresponding section. Data displayed remains the same, using the already loaded missionText, visionText, beliefs[], and leaders[].

No other URL parameters are required for this page. Parameters related to navigation out of the page (e.g., pastorId for pastor_profile.html) are constructed only when leaving the page and are not interpreted by about.html itself.

components.functionality: On page initialization, fetches all About page content by calling getAboutPageContent and exposes missionText, visionText, beliefs[], and leaders[] to other components via a shared state or props mechanism.

Manages the active section of the About page based on the 'section' URL parameter and user interactions with in-page navigation. Updates the URL when the user switches sections and responds to browser back/forward navigation.
Provides controls (e.g., tabs or links) for switching between mission, vision, beliefs, and leadership sections. Relies on section-state-router to update active section and URL.

Displays the mission text returned from getAboutPageContent when the mission section is active. It can also be displayed alongside other sections depending on layout, but receives focus/highlight when activeSection is 'mission'.
Displays the vision text returned from getAboutPageContent when the vision section is active, and responds to activation changes from section-state-router.

Renders the beliefs array from getAboutPageContent as an expandable/collapsible list. Each item shows a belief title and can reveal its body text when expanded. Expansion state is managed locally per item, independent of API calls.

Displays the leaders array from getAboutPageContent as a list of leader entries, each showing name, role, photo, and bioSnippet. For leaders where pastorId is present, clicking the item or a related control navigates to pastor_profile.html with the pastorId passed as a URL parameter.

Represents a single leader within leaders-list. It receives a leader object (including name, role, photoUrl, bioSnippet, and optional pastorId) and handles user interaction for that item, including navigation to a pastor profile when pastorId is provided.
Provides contextual navigation from the About page to related site areas such as ministries.html and care.html. Does not perform API calls but uses standard link navigation.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with approximately 3–4 columns for the left sidebar and 8–9 columns for the main content area within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a classic informational pattern: global header and navigation at the very top, a full-width photographic hero band immediately below, and then a centered content container that is split into two columns. Inside this container, the left column is a vertical section navigation for the About-related pages, while the right column holds the About content itself in a linear top-to-bottom stack: page title and meta (e.g., RSS badge), followed by the mission, vision, beliefs (accordion), leadership list, and finally related links.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a classic informational pattern: global header and navigation at the very top, a full-width photographic hero band immediately below, and then a centered content container that is split into two columns. Inside this container, the left column is a vertical section navigation for the About-related pages, while the right column holds the About content itself in a linear top-to-bottom stack: page title and meta (e.g., RSS badge), followed by the mission, vision, beliefs (accordion), leadership list, and finally related links.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero image spans the full browser width and roughly the top 25–30% of the initial viewport height. Below it, the main content container is constrained to a comfortable max-width (e.g., ~1120–1200px) and centered. Within this container, the left sidebar (section-nav) occupies about 25–30% of the width (roughly 3–4 grid columns of a 12-column system), while the right main column uses the remaining 70–75% (about 8–9 columns). Each content section in the main column has 24–32px vertical spacing above and below; internal padding within sections and list items uses 8–16px, in line with the base 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The left sidebar is relatively dense: links are single-line text items stacked vertically with modest padding and separated by thin horizontal rules, creating a compact but readable menu. The right column is more open: headings have extra breathing room, the first mission/intro paragraph is given additional emphasis (including a green text treatment), and vertical spacing between sections is clear but not overly generous. The layout aims for a practical, text-forward density typical of a traditional church site—informational, not sparse, and never cluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the global header and logo, then moves down to the warm, full-width hero image. From there, attention naturally shifts slightly right to the large About page title at the top of the main column. Immediately below, the highlighted green mission/intro text is read, with an illustrative icon drawing the eye toward the right side of the content area. As users scroll, the visual flow continues vertically through the mission, vision, beliefs accordion, and leadership list. The left sidebar navigation sits within peripheral vision, discovered either right after the page title or when users look for navigation; it supports lateral movement between sections without competing strongly with the main content.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global header and a full-width photographic hero image that sets a calm, welcoming tone, the About page content sits inside a centered, fixed-width container. This container is split into two columns: on the left, a narrow vertical sidebar lists About-related sections like Mission, Vision, Beliefs, and Leadership as simple text links separated by thin dividers, with the current page subtly highlighted. On the right, a wider main column begins with a small inline RSS badge tucked near the top-left above or beside a large “About” page title. Just under the title, a short introductory or mission paragraph appears in green text, stretching across the column, with a small, flat illustrative icon aligned toward the upper-right portion of the text area. As users scroll down, the mission content, vision explanation, an accordion of beliefs, and a structured list of church leaders appear one after another, each section separated by roughly 24–32px of vertical space. The typography is clean and utilitarian, with minimal decoration and mostly flat color blocks. At the bottom of the main column, a compact related-links area offers pathways to additional pages. The overall effect is orderly and content-first: a stable left-hand navigation spine and a vertically flowing narrative on the right, all framed within a traditional church-appropriate, low-friction layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader component exists at the structural root of the About page content, conceptually positioned just inside the main page wrapper. It does not render any visible UI; instead, it occupies no visual space and does not affect spacing or alignment of other elements. In the DOM, it sits above the two-column content container that holds the section-nav and main content, ensuring that data for mission, vision, beliefs, and leaders is loaded before those visual components render.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-interactive; occupies no layout space and follows standard document flow in the code structure without impacting visual positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-state-router is logically anchored to the main content area of the About page, conceptually sitting just above the section-nav and the first content section in the document structure. It has no visible UI representation, so it does not take up any width or height and introduces no margins or padding. It controls which section is considered active (for example, syncing the URL, sidebar highlight, and scroll position) while leaving the visual layout to the two-column structure below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual state controller; participates only in application logic and not in spatial layout. It scrolls with the page as part of normal document flow but is effectively invisible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-nav is placed in the left column of the main two-column container, immediately below the bottom edge of the hero image and vertically aligned with the top of the main content column to its right. It spans roughly 25–30% of the container width (about 3–4 of 12 grid columns) and runs from near the top of the content area downward, adjusting height based on the number of links. Inside, links are stacked vertically as single-line text items with 8–12px of vertical padding and 12–16px horizontal padding from the left border. Each item is separated by a thin, full-width horizontal rule, creating a compact but clear menu. The currently active About subsection (e.g., About overview or Mission) is highlighted through a subtle treatment such as a slightly darker background color and/or bolder text, while still keeping the look understated and appropriate for a church site. The sidebar aligns its left edge with the main container’s left margin and its right edge with the inner grid gutter between columns, leaving a clean vertical separation between sidebar and main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in standard two-column layout and scrolls with the page. It is not fixed or sticky by default, though on tall screens it may appear to remain in view longer simply due to its relatively short height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mission-section appears near the top of the right main content column, directly below the overall About page title and any small meta elements (such as the RSS badge). The main column itself occupies roughly 70–75% of the container width (about 8–9 of 12 grid columns) and sits to the right of the section-nav with a consistent vertical alignment at the top. The mission-section spans the full usable width of this right column, with 16–24px internal padding on the left and right if it is rendered inside a subtle content block. A section heading such as “Our Mission” is placed at the top-left of this block, with 8–12px spacing above and below the heading. Immediately following, the first mission paragraph is styled as highlighted green text and is given slightly more line-height, reflecting the mockup’s emphasized intro paragraph. Toward the top-right portion of the section’s content area, aligned with the first paragraph’s vertical center, an illustrative icon or flat graphic is positioned, taking up roughly 15–20% of the section’s width; the text wraps naturally to the left and below it. Below the highlighted paragraph, additional mission copy appears as normal body text, with 16–24px vertical spacing separating paragraphs. The mission-section has approximately 24–32px of vertical margin above and below to clearly separate it from the title area above and the vision-section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a standard block within the main content column, expanding vertically as needed. The icon inside the section is right-aligned but contained within the section boundaries and does not overlap other sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The vision-section is placed directly beneath the mission-section in the right main content column, maintaining the same left and right alignment and full width of the column. There is a clear vertical gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of the mission-section and the top of the vision-section. A heading such as “Our Vision” is aligned to the left edge of the main column content, with 8–12px spacing above and below the heading. Below the heading, one or more paragraphs of text provide the vision content, laid out in a single column with standard line-height and 12–16px spacing between paragraphs. Unlike the mission, the vision-section uses regular body text color throughout for a more neutral emphasis, echoing the mockup’s pattern where only the introductory paragraph is highlighted. The overall section maintains internal padding consistent with the mission-section, ensuring the edges of text are well inside the column’s gutters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element within the main column; scrolls with the page and expands vertically with content. It does not have floating elements inside and relies on text-only content for a clean, linear layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The beliefs-accordion sits below the vision-section in the main content column, separated from it by approximately 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the right column, aligning flush with the left edge of the vision text above and the leaders-section below. At the top of the accordion area, a heading such as “What We Believe” is left-aligned with about 8–12px margin below it. The accordion items are stacked vertically within the column, each item stretching nearly the full width of the column with 12–16px internal padding. Accordion headers use slightly bolder text and may include a small disclosure icon aligned to the right, reinforcing the utilitarian yet flat design approach (no heavy borders or shadows). When an accordion panel is expanded, its body content appears directly below the header within the same item block, with 8–12px vertical padding separating header and body content. There is minimal but clear spacing (8–12px) between each accordion item, ensuring the list feels organized but not airy. Thin dividing lines or subtle background tints can be used to separate items in keeping with the simple informational style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in normal document order within the main column. Accordion panels expand and collapse vertically within their allocated width, pushing subsequent content down as needed. No fixed or overlapping behavior is used.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The leaders-list is positioned below the beliefs-accordion in the main column, with roughly 32px of vertical space separating the bottom of the accordion from the top of the leadership content to signal a new major section. It spans the full width of the right column, aligning with all preceding sections. At the top, a heading such as “Leadership” is left-aligned, followed by a 12–16px gap before the individual leader items begin. The leaders-list organizes its child leader-item components in either a simple vertical list or a compact grid depending on screen width: on wider screens, leader items may be arranged in two columns within this main column (each item occupying roughly 48–50% of the column width with a 16–24px gap between columns), while on narrower screens they stack vertically. Vertical spacing between rows of leaders is about 16–24px, giving each entry breathing room while maintaining a moderately dense feel. The overall block feels like a structured directory of people—informational and tidy, without decorative frames.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a flexible list within the main column, using regular document flow. If a responsive grid is used, items reflow based on available width, but there is no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each leader-item is a compact horizontal block within the leaders-list. On larger screens, it occupies roughly half the width of the leaders-list (in a two-column layout) with 16–24px horizontal spacing between items; on smaller screens, each leader-item expands to nearly 100% width and stacks vertically. Inside the item, a small portrait or avatar can be positioned to the left, taking up about 25–30% of the item’s width, with the leader’s name and role to the right. The content area has 12–16px of padding on all sides, following the base spacing system. The name is at the top, left-aligned and slightly bolder, with the title or role directly beneath in regular weight and slightly smaller size. Additional details (such as contact info) can be placed below with 4–8px spacing between lines. The overall look is flat and clean—no heavy borders or shadows—relying on spacing and typography to distinguish items. Vertical spacing between leader-items (or between rows of items) is about 16–24px so the list feels informative but not cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Leader items participate in the leaders-list’s layout logic (single column or two-column grid) and otherwise follow standard block flow. They expand vertically to fit their content, without any floating or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-links component appears at the bottom of the main content column, beneath the leaders-list, separated from it by about 32px of vertical space to signal a distinct, concluding area. It spans the full width of the right column, aligning left and right with the other sections. At the top, a small heading such as “Related Pages” or “More About Our Church” is left-aligned with 8–12px margin below. The links themselves are displayed as a simple vertical list or a compact horizontal row, depending on how many there are: in a vertical list, each link is left-aligned, with 8–12px vertical padding and maybe thin dividers reminiscent of the sidebar style but lighter; in a horizontal layout, links appear inline with 16px spacing or small separators (e.g., vertical bars) between them. The overall treatment is modest and text-forward, sitting visually lighter than the major content sections but clearly accessible as the final navigation option on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits at the end of the main content flow and scrolls naturally with the page. It has no fixed or floating behavior and adjusts its height based on the number of links.

name: Contact & Directions
title: Contact & Directions
description: Provides contact details, directions, parking information, and a general inquiry form for the church campus, using dynamic data from the backend interfaces.

core_features: Load and display the church’s address, primary phone, general email, service time summaries, map location, and parking information via getContactAndDirections.
Render an interactive map area using the latitude, longitude, and mapProvider returned by getContactAndDirections, with a link out to full directions on the provider’s site.
Show a concise service times summary grouped by dayOfWeek using the serviceTimesSummary array from getContactAndDirections.
Provide a general contact form (name, email, message) that submits via submitContactForm and handles success/error responses without hardcoded messages.

Offer navigation links to Plan a Visit (visit.html), Room Reservations (room_reservations.html), and Pastoral Care (care.html), optionally enriched with URL parameters derived from current page state (e.g., section, source).

Support URL-driven state for highlighting sections (e.g., map vs. form), focusing/scrolling to the contact form, and reflecting the last contact submission result (success or error) using URL parameters instead of localStorage.
user_workflows: View basic contact information and service times: 1) User navigates to contact.html (from footer or external link). 2) On page load, a data-loader component calls getContactAndDirections with no parameters. 3) When the response resolves, the address, phone, generalEmail, serviceTimesSummary, map, and parkingInfoText are bound to their respective components. 4) The user can read the address and service times summary; no additional interaction is required.

Get directions and parking information: 1) User lands on contact.html, optionally with ?section=map in the URL. 2) After getContactAndDirections resolves, the map-and-parking component renders the map using map.latitude, map.longitude, and map.mapProvider. 3) If section=map, the page scrolls/focuses the map-and-parking component. 4) The user clicks a “Open in Maps” action, which constructs a provider-specific URL using the returned coordinates (and provider) and opens it in a new tab/window. 5) The user can also read parkingInfoText from the same response for parking details.

Submit a general inquiry: 1) User navigates to contact.html, optionally with ?section=form and/or prefill parameters like ?name=...&email=...&message=.... 2) The contact-form component initializes its fields from URL parameters if present. 3) The user fills in name, email, and message fields. 4) On form submit, the component calls submitContactForm(name, email, message). 5) While awaiting response, the component disables the submit button and may show a loading state (purely functional). 6) When submitContactForm returns, if success=true, the component shows a success state (using the returned message) and updates the URL (e.g., contact.html?section=form&contactStatus=success) via history APIs without reloading; if success=false, it shows the returned message as an error and updates URL to contact.html?section=form&contactStatus=error. 7) If the user reloads or revisits with contactStatus in the URL, the page re-initializes the form component into the corresponding success/error state without needing localStorage.

Navigate to Plan a Visit based on context: 1) User reads serviceTimesSummary on the Contact page. 2) The user clicks a “Plan a Visit” action (global for the page, not bound to a specific service instance because there is no service-level interface on this page). 3) The navigation component constructs a URL visit.html?source=contact&section=services (and may include optional parameters such as dayOfWeek based on the current view or user choice if offered) without hardcoding values; the dayOfWeek comes from an interaction with the displayed serviceTimesSummary item. 4) The browser navigates to the generated visit.html URL.

Navigate to Room Reservations: 1) From contact.html, the user locates a “Reserve a Room” link. 2) On click, the navigation component routes to room_reservations.html?source=contact. 3) The query parameter allows the destination page to know the user originated from Contact & Directions (no additional data is persisted locally).

Request Pastoral Care: 1) User on contact.html wants pastoral care. 2) The user selects the “Request Pastoral Care” link. 3) The navigation component routes to care.html?source=contact. 4) No data is stored locally; the URL alone indicates origin.

Deep linking into sections: 1) External links or internal navigation may point to contact.html?section=form or contact.html?section=map. 2) On load, the page reads the section parameter. 3) If section=form, the contact-form component is focused/scrolled into view. If section=map, the map-and-parking component is focused/scrolled into view. 4) If contactStatus is present (e.g., contactStatus=success), the contact-form component initializes directly into a success or error state without requiring a new submit.
interactions: Page load: When contact.html loads, a data-loader component calls getContactAndDirections once. On success, it passes the resulting object to child components via props or a shared state mechanism. On failure, an error state is raised for components that depend on this data.

Map interaction: Clicking the map’s “Open in Maps” or equivalent control constructs an external URL based on mapProvider, latitude, and longitude from getContactAndDirections.map, then opens it in a new browser tab or window. No static map URL is hardcoded; all parameters come from the API response.

Service times interaction: The service-times summary component iterates over serviceTimesSummary from getContactAndDirections. For each entry, it displays its timesText and dayOfWeek. When the user clicks a contextual “Plan a Visit” adjacent to a particular day, the navigation component generates visit.html?source=contact&dayOfWeek={dayOfWeekFromAPI} (and optionally section=services) and navigates there.

Contact form input: Users can type into the name, email, and message fields. If the URL includes name, email, or message parameters, the form pre-populates those fields from the URL on first render. Any change is kept in in-memory component state only until the page reloads.

Contact form submission: When the user submits the contact form, the component validates that name, email, and message are non-empty (and that email appears valid) using client-side logic only. If validation passes, it calls submitContactForm with the current field values. On success, it uses the returned message field to display feedback and updates the URL’s contactStatus parameter via the History API. On failure, it similarly displays the returned message as an error and updates contactStatus in the URL.

Contact form state from URL: On initial render or on URL change (e.g., via History API), the contact-form component reads contactStatus from query parameters. If contactStatus=success, the component shows a submitted-success state and may disable further editing until the user explicitly clears or resets the form. If contactStatus=error, it shows a generic error state. No submission status is read from or written to localStorage.

Section focus interaction: On initial load, or when the URL’s section parameter changes, the main page controller scrolls/focuses the appropriate section: address/service info, map-and-parking, or contact-form. For example, if section=form, the form component is scrolled into view and the first input is focused for accessibility.

Navigation links: Clicking “Plan a Visit” in the general actions area navigates to visit.html with query params indicating origin and section (e.g., visit.html?source=contact). Clicking “Reserve a Room” navigates to room_reservations.html?source=contact. Clicking “Request Pastoral Care” navigates to care.html?source=contact. All URLs are built dynamically based on current location and query parameter logic, not hardcoded beyond filenames.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL parameters for state management, not localStorage or other client-side persistence. Supported parameters include: 1) section: Controls which section is considered primary (e.g., 'form', 'map', or omitted for default). On load, the page reads section from location.search. If section='form', the contact-form component is scrolled into view and focused; if section='map', the map-and-parking component is scrolled into view and focused. This parameter may be updated via the History API when the user navigates between internal tabs or links within the page. 2) contactStatus: Reflects the outcome of the most recent contact form submission ('success' or 'error'). After submitContactForm returns, the contact-form component calls history.replaceState or pushState to set contactStatus accordingly (e.g., contact.html?section=form&contactStatus=success). On load, the form reads contactStatus and initializes in a corresponding state without needing to resubmit. 3) name, email, message: Optional prefill parameters. If present in the query string (e.g., contact.html?section=form&name=...&email=...), the contact-form component uses them as initial values for its fields. These values are not persisted beyond the current page view and may be overwritten by user input. 4) source: Used when navigating from this page to others (e.g., visit.html?source=contact, room_reservations.html?source=contact). While this page does not interpret source for itself, it may include source=contact for consistency in outbound links. 5) dayOfWeek (for outbound links only): When the user chooses a specific day-of-week in the serviceTimesSummary and clicks a contextual link, the navigation component builds URLs such as visit.html?source=contact&dayOfWeek={valueFromServiceTimesSummary}. The page does not interpret dayOfWeek itself; it only passes it through to the destination. URL parameters are the sole mechanism for persisting or sharing state across page loads; all other state is kept in memory and reset on refresh.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getContactAndDirections with no parameters, handles loading and error states, and distributes the resulting data object (address, phone, generalEmail, serviceTimesSummary, map, parkingInfoText) to child components via a shared state or props.
event_handlers: onSuccess: updates shared state with API response and notifies dependent components
onError: sets an error flag/state that dependent components can react to (e.g., show fallback text)

components.functionality: Displays the church’s main contact information (address, phone, generalEmail) and a concise summary of when services typically occur using serviceTimesSummary, without any hardcoded values.
event_handlers: onDataChange: when getContactAndDirections data updates, re-render address, phone, email, and service times
onClickServiceDayPlanVisit: invoked when a user clicks a contextual 'Plan a Visit' action for a specific day; passes the selected dayOfWeek to the navigation component

components.functionality: Iterates over serviceTimesSummary to render a list of days and time descriptions, and exposes interactions for selecting a day-of-week to use in navigation to the Plan a Visit page.
event_handlers: onItemClick: when a user clicks a particular service summary entry, emit an event with that entry's dayOfWeek and timesText
onPlanVisitClick: when a user chooses to plan a visit from this list, call a navigation handler to go to visit.html?source=contact&dayOfWeek={selectedDayOfWeek}

components.functionality: Uses the map object (latitude, longitude, mapProvider) and parkingInfoText from getContactAndDirections to render an embedded map or map link and display parking details, with a control to open full directions in the map provider.
event_handlers: onDataChange: when map or parkingInfoText updates, rebuild the map embed or link URLs and re-render content
onOpenInMapsClick: construct a navigation URL using mapProvider, latitude, and longitude and open it in a new window/tab
onSectionFocus: when URL parameter section=map is detected, scroll this component into view and set focus as appropriate

components.functionality: Provides a general inquiry form with name, email, and message fields, initializes from URL parameters when present, submits data via submitContactForm, and reflects success/error states based on the API response and the contactStatus URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInit: read URL parameters (name, email, message, contactStatus, section) to set initial field values and determine whether to show a success or error state; if section=form, scroll into view and focus the first field
onInputChange: update in-memory form state as user types into name, email, and message fields
onSubmit: validate the current field values, then call submitContactForm(name, email, message); set a local submitting state while awaiting response

onSubmitSuccess: when submitContactForm returns success=true, show the returned message as confirmation, optionally reset form fields, and update the URL via History API to include contactStatus=success&section=form without reloading the page
onSubmitError: when submitContactForm returns success=false or an error occurs, display the returned message as an error and update the URL via History API to include contactStatus=error&section=form
onUrlChange: when the URL changes (e.g., due to history.pushState or replaceState), re-read contactStatus and adjust the form’s visible state accordingly

components.functionality: Central controller that reads URL parameters such as section and contactStatus on load and on history changes, and instructs the appropriate sections (e.g., contact-form, map-and-parking-section) to focus or adjust state.
event_handlers: onLoad: parse location.search to determine initial section (default, form, map) and broadcast focus instructions to the relevant component
onHistoryChange: respond to changes in URL query parameters (e.g., after form submission) and update child components’ states accordingly
onInternalNavClick: when the user clicks internal section links (e.g., a link within the page to 'Contact Form' or 'Map'), update the section parameter in the URL using the History API

components.functionality: Renders actionable links for Plan a Visit, Reserve a Room, and Request Pastoral Care, constructing URLs including source=contact and optional contextual parameters derived from the current view.
event_handlers: onPlanVisitClick: navigate to visit.html?source=contact, optionally adding dayOfWeek from a selected serviceTimesSummary entry if the click originates from such an entry

components.functionality: Displays a concise feedback banner or message summarizing the most recent contact form outcome (success or error) based on the contactStatus URL parameter and the latest response message from submitContactForm if available.
event_handlers: onDataChange: when a new submitContactForm response is received, update the visible message and ensure the URL contactStatus parameter matches the outcome
onUrlChange: when contactStatus in the URL changes (e.g., after back/forward navigation), adjust visibility and styling (success vs. error) without re-submitting the form
onDismissClick: when user manually dismisses the banner, update in-memory state to hide it; optionally update the URL to remove contactStatus via History API

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with approximately 3–4 columns for the left sidebar and 8–9 columns for the main content area within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom organization: global header and navigation at the very top, a full-width photographic hero band beneath it, then a centered content container split into a left sidebar and a right main column. Within that main column, content is ordered from high-level contact summary and service times, down to practical directions/map, and finally the contact form. Supporting behaviors (data loading, state control, feedback banner) layer on top of or inline within the main column without disrupting this basic flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom organization: global header and navigation at the very top, a full-width photographic hero band beneath it, then a centered content container split into a left sidebar and a right main column. Within that main column, content is ordered from high-level contact summary and service times, down to practical directions/map, and finally the contact form. Supporting behaviors (data loading, state control, feedback banner) layer on top of or inline within the main column without disrupting this basic flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Below the hero, the content sits inside a max-width centered container (around 960–1140px). Horizontally, the left sidebar occupies about 25–30% of this container, with the main content column taking the remaining 70–75%. Vertically, the hero consumes roughly the top 25–30% of the viewport, and the remaining vertical space is dominated by the main column content. Key content blocks (summary, service times, map, form) are separated by 24–32px vertical gaps. Internal padding within sections and list items follows the 8–16px rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: The sidebar is relatively dense: links are close together with thin separators and modest padding to reflect a practical, informational feel. The right main column is more open: the page title, introductory contact summary, and each major section (service times, map/parking, form) have clear separation. Paragraphs and lists are comfortably spaced but not overly airy, maintaining a traditional, utilitarian tone appropriate for a church information site.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the user’s eye passes from the global nav to the hero, it is drawn to the right column’s large page title and then immediately to the introductory contact summary. The first paragraph and icon treatment in the summary create an initial focal point. From there, the eye moves downward through service times, then to the map and parking information where the map on the right provides a secondary visual anchor. Below that, the contact form becomes the final focal zone. The left sidebar of navigation links remains in peripheral vision, providing context and easy movement between related pages. Feedback states (banner) and loader overlays are designed to appear within the main column, close to the content they affect, while staying visually subordinate to the main page hierarchy.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a traditional, content-first church information layout. At the very top is a horizontal global header with the church logo on the left and site-wide navigation on the right, spanning the full width. Immediately beneath is a full-width hero photograph—perhaps of the church building or congregation—taking up about a quarter of the viewport height, anchoring the page with warmth and context. Below the hero, a centered content band appears, constrained to a moderate max-width. This band is split into two columns: a left sidebar with vertically stacked navigation links to other information pages, and a right main column that carries the ‘Contact & Directions’ content. Near the top of the main column sits a small RSS or utility badge aligned with the page title, followed by a prominent page heading. Just below the heading, a contact summary section introduces key details with a highlighted first paragraph and a small icon off to the right, echoing the mockup’s introductory styling. Scrolling down, users encounter a compact list of service times, followed by a broader section combining written directions and parking notes on the left with an embedded map on the right. Further down, a simple, vertically stacked contact form stretches through most of the main column width. Feedback messages appear in a subtle banner near the form, and a loader overlay or skeleton briefly appears over the main content area when data is fetching. Overall, the design feels clean, flat, and informational, with measured spacing and a clear vertical reading path from summary to details to action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader has no permanent visual footprint but appears centered within the right main content column whenever contact or directions data is being fetched. When active, it overlays only the main content area (not the global header, hero, or sidebar), covering from just below the page title down to the bottom of the main column. This overlay is semi-opaque white or very light, preserving the flat design, with a small spinner or progress indicator centered both horizontally and vertically in that column. The overlay respects the main column’s width (roughly 70–75% of the content container) and aligns flush with its left and right edges, maintaining the same margins from the outer container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a temporary, centered overlay within the right main column; fixed relative to the main column content area while active, then fully removed from layout flow when data loading is complete.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact summary section is the first substantive content block in the right main column, positioned directly beneath the page title and any small inline RSS/utility badge. It spans the full width of the right column (about 70–75% of the centered container) and is left-aligned with the main column’s text. There is a generous vertical gap of about 24px between the page title and the top of this section, and another 24–32px below it before the next section (service times). Inside, the first paragraph of text is styled with green typography and slightly increased line-height, occupying roughly the left two-thirds of the section width. A small illustrative icon (for example, a phone or envelope) is positioned to the right of this first paragraph, near the upper-right corner of the section, visually floating in the empty space to the right of the text. The icon does not break the paragraph’s text flow; instead, it sits as a visual accent aligned vertically with the first 1–2 lines of the intro paragraph. The section uses 16px vertical padding and 8–16px horizontal padding to keep text comfortable but compact, matching the informational tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section within the main content column, participating normally in the document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service times summary list sits immediately below the contact summary section in the right main column, separated by approximately 24px of vertical space. It also spans the full width of the main column and is left-aligned with the summary text above. The list is presented as a simple vertical stack of items (e.g., ‘Sunday Morning – 9:00am’, ‘Sunday Evening – 6:00pm’), each on its own line with 8–12px of vertical padding. Items may have subtle horizontal dividers (thin, light gray lines) similar to the sidebar’s style but with slightly more vertical breathing room to keep the main column feeling more spacious. If labels and times are both shown, they are arranged so that labels sit to the left and times to the right on the same line, but constrained within the main column’s width. The list occupies a moderate vertical height, roughly 15–25% of the visible main column content, and is designed to be quickly scannable without dominating the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list within the main column, expanding vertically based on number of items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The map and parking section appears below the service times list, with a clear vertical separation of about 32px to indicate a distinct content block. This section stretches the full width of the right main column. Inside, the space is divided horizontally into two subareas: on the left, a text block containing written directions, parking information, and any brief notes (about 40–45% of the section width); on the right, an embedded map (about 55–60% of the section width). The left text block aligns with the main column’s left edge and has standard paragraph spacing (8–12px between lines/paragraphs). The map is aligned to the right edge of the main column and sits at the same vertical starting line as the text, giving the section a balanced two-column feel within the main column. The map has a fixed height of roughly 280–320px and no heavy borders or shadows—just a thin, flat border if needed. The entire section including both columns uses about 16px internal padding and occupies a prominent band in the middle of the main column, visually standing out due to the map imagery.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, responsive split section: text and map columns shrink proportionally on narrower screens, eventually stacking vertically (map usually below text) while maintaining their internal spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact form is placed below the map and parking section with a 32px vertical gap, acting as the final major content block in the right main column. It is left-aligned with previous sections but slightly narrower than the full main column—occupying about 80–90% of the right column’s width—to create comfortable side margins and reduce line length for form labels. The form fields are arranged in a simple vertical stack: label on top, input beneath, with 8–12px vertical spacing between each pair and 16–24px between field groups. Any multi-column layout is avoided to maintain clarity and the utilitarian aesthetic. The form’s header text (e.g., ‘Send us a Message’) sits at the top of this block with about 16px padding above, and the submit button is aligned to the left at the bottom of the form, with 16–24px spacing above it. The overall form block is visually important but not overpowering; it reads as a practical, approachable end-point for the page’s information sequence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the main column that expands vertically as fields require; scrollable with the page and not fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact section controller is non-visual and occupies no direct space in the layout. Conceptually, it sits behind the right main column and orchestrates which parts of the contact content are visible or in which state (for example, toggling between loading, loaded, and error states, or showing/hiding the feedback banner after form submission). It interacts with the data loader overlay, the feedback banner, and the contact form but does not itself render any visible elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No spatial presence; functions purely as a logic/state layer that influences other components’ visibility and arrangement without altering the grid structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact page navigation links form the left sidebar column that begins directly below the hero image and global nav, aligning vertically with the top of the main column content (near the page title). This sidebar occupies about 25–30% of the content container’s width and is left-aligned to the page center, leaving equal outer margins on either side of the container. The links are arranged in a vertical stack, each item spanning the full width of the sidebar with 8–12px vertical padding and 12–16px horizontal padding. Thin, light gray horizontal divider lines separate the items, giving a slightly dense, directory-like feel. The current page (‘Contact & Directions’) is highlighted using a bolder text weight and/or a subtle background tint, extending across the full width of the sidebar item. The sidebar height extends as long as there are navigation items; it scrolls with the page and does not overlap the hero. It remains visually secondary but consistently present in peripheral view next to the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static column occupying the left side of the content band; scrolls normally with the page, not sticky or fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact feedback banner appears within the right main column, closely associated with the contact form. When active (e.g., after form submission or when an error occurs), it is positioned directly above the contact form block, with about 16px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the map and parking section and the top of the banner, and about 16px between the banner and the top of the form. It spans the full width of the main column (matching the width of sections like the map and summary), with 8–16px of internal padding and left-aligned text. The banner uses a lightly tinted background (green for success, red/orange for errors) with simple flat styling and no heavy shadows. It is tall enough to accommodate one or two lines of text without feeling cramped, but it does not extend significantly beyond that, keeping it compact and in line with the overall informational density. When inactive, the banner is completely removed from the layout so that the map section and the form sit closer together.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears inline within the main column’s flow, pushing adjacent content up or down; not fixed, and always scrolls with the page.

name: Ministries Overview
title: Ministries
description: Shows an overview list of active church ministries with descriptions, contact information, and actions to give, serve, or view related events. Supports deep-linking to a specific ministry via URL parameters.

core_features: Load and display a list of ministries using the getMinistriesOverview interface.
Show each ministry’s name, description, contact person, and contact email.
Provide an action on each ministry to navigate to giving (give.html) pre-filtered for that ministry’s related fund when available.
Provide an action on each ministry to navigate to volunteer opportunities (volunteer.html) filtered for that ministry.
Provide an action on each ministry to navigate to events (events.html) filtered for that ministry.
Support an incoming query parameter highlightMinistryId that scrolls to and visually emphasizes the corresponding ministry.
Allow users to copy or share a link to a specific ministry using the same highlightMinistryId query parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View ministries overview
1. User navigates to ministries.html (directly, from footer, or from other pages).
2. Page initializes and calls getMinistriesOverview.
3. When the response resolves, the page stores the ministries list in memory state.
4. The ministries list component renders one ministry card per ministry item.
5. The user scrolls the page and reads names, descriptions, and contact information.

Workflow 2: Arrive from About or Give page focused on one ministry
1. User clicks a link on about.html or give.html that points to ministries.html with a highlightMinistryId query parameter.
2. On page load, the state controller reads highlightMinistryId from the URL and stores it as pendingHighlightMinistryId.
3. The page calls getMinistriesOverview and stores the result.
4. When ministries are loaded, the controller searches the list for a ministry whose ministryId matches highlightMinistryId.
5. If found, the page programmatically scrolls to that ministry card and marks it as highlighted in state.
6. The highlighted card renders with an emphasized state (purely functional: e.g., an isHighlighted flag) until the user navigates away or clears the URL parameter.

Workflow 3: Give to a specific ministry
1. User locates a ministry card in the list.
2. The ministry card checks whether relatedFundId from getMinistriesOverview is present for that item.
3. If relatedFundId exists, the card shows an enabled "Give to this ministry" action.
4. User clicks the "Give to this ministry" action.
5. The click handler reads the ministry’s relatedFundId from bound data and constructs a navigation URL give.html?fundId=<relatedFundId>.
6. Browser navigates to give.html with the fundId query parameter, allowing give.html to pre-select the fund.
7. If the ministry has no relatedFundId, the card either hides the give action or displays a disabled state with an explanatory message (no additional API calls).

Workflow 4: Serve with a ministry
1. User locates a ministry card.
2. The card computes a volunteer category value derived from the ministry (derivedVolunteerCategory), using a deterministic transformation based on ministryId and/or name (no hardcoded mapping).
3. User clicks "Serve with this ministry".
4. The click handler constructs a URL volunteer.html?category=<derivedVolunteerCategory>.
5. Browser navigates to volunteer.html with the category parameter, allowing that page to interpret the category as a ministry-related filter.

Workflow 5: View ministry-related events
1. User locates a ministry card.
2. The card computes a category value derived from the ministry (derivedEventCategory), using a deterministic transformation based on ministryId and/or name (no hardcoded mapping).
3. User clicks "View ministry events".
4. The click handler constructs a URL events.html?category=<derivedEventCategory>.
5. Browser navigates to events.html with the category parameter, allowing the Events page to filter events associated with that ministry.

Workflow 6: Share a link to a specific ministry
1. User scrolls to a ministry card they want to share.
2. User clicks a "Copy link to this ministry" or equivalent action on that card.
3. The click handler constructs a URL for ministries.html that includes highlightMinistryId=<ministryId>.
4. The handler copies this URL to the clipboard or exposes it for manual copying (implementation detail outside of API scope).
5. When another user opens that URL, Workflow 2 executes, and the same ministry is highlighted.
interactions: Page load: On initial load, the page reads the highlightMinistryId query parameter (if present) and then calls getMinistriesOverview. No other interfaces are invoked.
Ministry list rendering: After getMinistriesOverview resolves, the ministries array is used to render a list of ministry cards, each bound to the corresponding ministry item.

Highlight based on URL: When both highlightMinistryId and ministries data are available, the state controller determines which ministry (if any) matches the ID and sets an isHighlighted flag on that ministry’s card state, then scrolls the viewport to that card once.

Give action click: When the user clicks "Give to this ministry" on a ministry card, the event handler reads the ministry’s relatedFundId and navigates to give.html?fundId=<relatedFundId>. If relatedFundId is absent, the handler does not navigate and may instead trigger a non-API UI message.

Serve action click: When the user clicks "Serve with this ministry", the handler computes derivedVolunteerCategory from that ministry’s data (e.g., based on ministryId) and navigates to volunteer.html?category=<derivedVolunteerCategory>.
View events click: When the user clicks "View ministry events", the handler computes derivedEventCategory from that ministry’s data and navigates to events.html?category=<derivedEventCategory>.

Copy/share link click: When the user clicks a share-link action on a card, the handler constructs a URL for ministries.html including highlightMinistryId=<ministryId> and exposes it to the user (copy to clipboard or visible field).

URL change for highlight: Optionally, when the user scrolls to or interacts with a ministry card (e.g., clicks its title), the controller may update the current page URL’s highlightMinistryId query parameter using history.replaceState or a full reload-safe link, ensuring the current ministry is deep-linkable without additional API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for this page is the URL query parameter highlightMinistryId. On every page load, the state controller parses the current window.location.search to read highlightMinistryId (if present) and stores it in an in-memory variable pendingHighlightMinistryId. The page then invokes getMinistriesOverview exactly once to retrieve the ministries list. When the data arrives, the controller searches the ministries array for an item whose ministryId equals pendingHighlightMinistryId. If a match is found, the controller sets currentHighlightedMinistryId to that ministryId and triggers a one-time scroll into view for the corresponding ministry card. Each ministry card determines its isHighlighted state by comparing its ministryId to currentHighlightedMinistryId, and renders accordingly. If highlightMinistryId is absent from the URL or does not match any ministryId, no card is highlighted and no scrolling is performed. Navigations to give.html, volunteer.html, and events.html are implemented as standard link navigations that construct their target URLs with query parameters based solely on the current ministry’s properties (relatedFundId, ministryId, and/or name) and deterministic transformation logic (e.g., encoding ministryId into the category value). The page does not store any state in localStorage or other persistent client storage; all shareable or restorable state is derived from or written to URL query parameters only.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls the getMinistriesOverview interface, stores the returned ministries array in page-level state, and exposes loading and error states to dependent components.

Manages highlight behavior based on the highlightMinistryId URL parameter and the loaded ministries list. Reads highlightMinistryId from the query string on load, waits for ministries data, identifies the matching ministry (if any), sets a highlighted ministry ID in state, and triggers a one-time scroll to that card. Optionally updates the URL with a new highlightMinistryId when the user selects a different ministry.
Renders a list of ministry cards from the ministries array. Handles empty states (no ministries) and passes per-ministry data plus highlight state down to each ministry card.

Represents a single ministry. Displays the ministry name, description, contactName, contactEmail, and relatedFundName (if provided). Computes whether it is highlighted by comparing its ministryId to the highlighted ID from the highlight controller. Exposes actions to give to this ministry, serve with this ministry, view ministry events, and copy a shareable link to this ministry.

Encapsulates the logic to construct navigation URLs based on ministry data. For the give action, it reads relatedFundId from the ministry item and, if present, navigates to give.html?fundId=<relatedFundId>. For serve and events actions, it computes derivedVolunteerCategory and derivedEventCategory respectively (e.g., via deterministic transformation of ministryId and/or name) and navigates to volunteer.html?category=<derivedVolunteerCategory> and events.html?category=<derivedEventCategory>. If required data is missing (e.g., relatedFundId is undefined), it prevents navigation and can signal the card to display a non-API error or info message.

Builds a shareable URL for a given ministry by combining the current page base URL with a highlightMinistryId query parameter set to that ministry’s ministryId. Provides the resulting URL back to the ministry-card component to display or copy.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column layout likely based on a 12-column grid, with approximately 3–4 columns for the left sidebar and 8–9 columns for the main content area within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the global header and full-width photographic hero, the Ministries Overview page uses a centered two-column content band. The left column is a vertical ministry list acting like a section navigation menu, and the right column is the primary reading area containing the ministry detail cards. Non-visual logic components (data loader, highlight controller, action router) run before and around this band, controlling which ministries are visible and highlighted but not occupying visible space. The ministry share links are small, inline elements anchored to the top-right of each ministry card title area.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the global header and full-width photographic hero, the Ministries Overview page uses a centered two-column content band. The left column is a vertical ministry list acting like a section navigation menu, and the right column is the primary reading area containing the ministry detail cards. Non-visual logic components (data loader, highlight controller, action router) run before and around this band, controlling which ministries are visible and highlighted but not occupying visible space. The ministry share links are small, inline elements anchored to the top-right of each ministry card title area.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero image spans 100% width and roughly 25–30% of the initial viewport height. Beneath it, the main content container is centered with a max width of about 960–1200px. Within this container, the left sidebar (ministry list) occupies ~25–30% of the width, while the right main column (ministry cards) occupies ~70–75%. Vertical spacing between major content groups (hero to content band, section heading to list/cards, and between individual cards) uses approximately 24–32px. Internal padding within list items and cards uses 8–16px, following the base 8px spacing system.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate: the ministry list in the left sidebar is relatively dense to show many ministries at a glance, with single-line items separated by thin rules and modest vertical padding. The right column is more open and readable: each ministry card has comfortable padding, a clear hierarchy between title, body, contact info, and actions, and 24–32px vertical gaps between cards. The page feels utilitarian and information-first, with enough white space to avoid clutter but no large empty areas.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the global nav and logo at the top down to the full-width hero image. From the bottom edge of the hero, attention is drawn slightly right to the main content column where the page title and introductory copy (outside these components) would appear, then down into the stack of ministry cards. As users read down the main column, the left sidebar ministry list sits slightly recessed but always visible in peripheral vision, enabling quick navigation and highlighting the selected ministry. Within each card, the title is seen first, then the body text, then the call-to-action buttons; the small share link is noticed last as a subtle utility positioned at the top-right of the card.

layout.overall_description: The Ministries Overview page sits below a traditional global header and a warm, full-width photographic hero that sets the church context. Beneath the hero, a centered content container divides into two columns: on the left, a narrow sidebar lists all active ministries as a vertically stacked menu with thin separators and a clear highlight on the currently selected ministry. On the right, a wider main column presents the detailed ministry content as a series of full-width cards flowing vertically down the page. Each card begins with a strong ministry title and a small share icon aligned to the right, followed by descriptive text and contact information, and finishing with a row of flat action buttons for giving, serving, or viewing related events. Spacing is orderly and consistent: comfortable margins around the main band, modest padding within list items, and more generous spacing around headings and between cards. The overall effect mirrors a clean, slightly traditional informational layout: the hero provides warmth, the sidebar supports quick navigation, and the main column invites users to read through ministries one by one while always offering clear next steps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual infrastructure component that effectively sits at the top of the content band, conceptually just below the hero and above both the left sidebar and right main column. It initializes and fetches all ministry data before rendering the ministry-list in the left column and the ministry-card instances in the right column.
